March 18, 2023, 11:47 PM

The whole day has felt a bit surreal.

Mom called this morning saying Dad’s back pain was getting really bad, so I spent the afternoon with them at the hospital. Registering, getting X-rays, waiting for results… As I sat in the waiting area, I suddenly realized what I was doing. I was taking notes on everything the doctor said, asking about the severity of the disc herniation, and inquiring how long the physical therapy would take. That’s not like me. Usually, I’d let my parents ask the questions or just sit there and listen quietly. But today, I was just… proactive.

The doctor said we should go with conservative treatment for now—no surgery yet. When I heard that, I felt my whole body relax a little. I even took photos of the diagnostic reports and asked for a list of the doctor’s recommendations. Those photos are all on my phone now, in a folder I created called "Dad’s Health." It’s a bit ridiculous, isn't it? Treating my father’s illness like work data. But I don’t know what else to do. At least this way, I feel like I’m doing something instead of just worrying.

What hit me hardest was something Dad said. After the check-up, he looked at me and said, "Xiaoyu, you’ve grown up." Just that one sentence. I didn’t really react at the time; I just nodded. But now, sitting at home, I’m still thinking about it. What did he mean? That I’m finally acting like an adult? Or that he’s realized he’s getting old and needs me to take care of him? I don’t know. We’ve never had those kinds of conversations.

I emailed my boss saying I might need to take some time off to go home over the next few weeks. I revised the wording so many times, but in the end, I didn't explain why—I just said "family matters that need attending to." I’m afraid he’ll think I’m being unprofessional or that it’ll affect my performance review. But at the same time, I feel like… maybe this time is different. Maybe this isn't about me trying to get something; it’s just something I have to do.

Mom is still worried about Dad’s recovery. I can hear the exhaustion in her voice. I need to look after both of them now. The thought makes me feel a bit breathless. I’ve never been needed like this before.

I’m not sure if I can do this well. But I have to do it.

I think I’ll mention it to Sister Li tomorrow. Maybe… maybe I can tell her what’s going on. I don’t know why I’m thinking that, but after today, carrying the weight of this alone feels a lot harder.

November 7, 2023, Evening

Sister Li just left. I’m sitting here in the kitchen, looking at the unwashed cutting board and dishes. I’m a bit tired, but it’s a different kind of tired.

When I invited her over to make Japanese food today, I was actually a little nervous. Not social anxiety—more like the "can I actually pull this off?" kind of nerves. I picked a relatively complex dish: Tamagoyaki. I’d watched videos of it dozens of times, but I’d never actually made it.

Halfway through, I messed up the heat, and one part of the egg roll got scorched. I could feel that wave of frustration hitting me—that "here we go again" feeling, just like all those classes and plans I’ve abandoned before. My instinct was to give up or just half-ass the rest.

But Sister Li was just sitting there. She looked at me and said, "It’s fine, it adds flavor this way." It wasn't that fake kind of encouragement; she just naturally accepted the mistake. So I kept going with the next roll, focusing on adjusting the heat. When I finally served it, it wasn't perfect, but I had finished it.

While we were eating, she asked me what made me want to do this. I couldn't give her a specific reason, just... I wanted to try. She smiled and said she’s the same way—sometimes you just want to do what you want to do.

Sitting here now, I’m thinking about something strange.

I’ve always felt like I have poor execution, like I can’t stick to anything. But today I realized—maybe the problem isn't that I’m incompetent. It’s that when I’m alone, my self-doubt gets magnified infinitely. I constantly ask myself, "Is this worth it?" "Can I do this well?" "What if I fail?" But when someone else is there, even just as company, I can... keep going.

It sounds simple, but for me, it feels like I’ve finally seen through something.

I realized it’s the same with my yoga group. I can keep going and finish the sessions because people are there, because there’s an environment supporting me. It’s not because I suddenly became stronger; it’s because... I found a system.

Growing up, my mom always taught me to be cautious and not make mistakes. I internalized that voice, so when I’m alone, that voice becomes deafening. But maybe... maybe what I need isn't to change myself, but to change the way I do things. To find the people and environments that can accompany me.

Sister Li asked me when we should do this again. Instead of saying "we'll see" or "if I have time" like I usually do, I gave her a specific date.

It’s strange, but that tiny commitment made me feel something different. It wasn't pressure; it felt more like... being needed? Or rather, being seen?

Time to clean up now. But I want to sit here just a little longer.

March 15, 2023, Night

Sister Li’s words have been looping in my head.

"Your joining us just makes us happier."

I’m sitting on the sofa, holding the book I bought today, but I haven't actually read a single word. My mind keeps replaying that scene in the cafe—me sitting among them, sipping coffee, someone asking what I’ve been reading lately, and I actually answered. And... no one seemed to think I was weird.

It’s strange.

I always thought I turned down these invitations because I "didn't want to trouble others," but today I realized what I was really saying was "I’m afraid of being a burden." Those two phrases sound the same, but they feel different. One is a choice; the other is... fear?

When Auntie Wang invited me to the cafe the other day, my first instinct was to say no. I had a mountain of excuses—I’d ruin the mood, I’m bad at small talk, I’d get exhausted in a crowd. But Sister Li just looked at me and said those words. I heard myself say, in a tiny voice, "Then... okay."

That was the first time I agreed without giving a bunch of complicated reasons.

Thinking back now, I feel a bit of lingering fear. But also... warmth?

It never occurred to me that maybe the problem isn't that I’m unfit for socializing, but that I’ve been overprotecting myself. Overthinking how others feel. Over-judging myself. It’s as if I assumed I was born to be a nuisance, so I had to self-isolate in advance.

But no one thought I was a nuisance today.

Everyone was so kind. The ladies from the yoga group even asked if I was coming back next week, and they sounded genuinely expectant. When I said, "Yeah, I'll be there," they all smiled.

Sitting here alone at home, I suddenly feel like crying. I don’t know if it’s out of relief, or the realization of how much time I’ve wasted on these unnecessary fears.

Maybe I’ve been overestimating how much people judge me.

Maybe... maybe I really can keep doing this. Yoga on Wednesdays and Saturdays, and the occasional cafe meetup. Not forcing myself, but just letting it happen.

But then I start worrying again. Is this feeling just a flash in the pan? Will I crawl back into my shell after a while? I’ve never had much confidence in my own follow-through.

Still... this time feels different. Because I’m not forcing myself to do something I "should" do; I’m doing something I actually want to do. Sister Li was right.

I want to keep going to yoga. I want to go to the cafe again.

Maybe I’m starting to change. Maybe this time it’ll stick.

Maybe.

Dr. Wang, I wanted to share something that happened today.

During our morning meeting, the department was discussing a new project. In the past, I probably would have just sat there quietly unless I was asked something directly. But today, I actually spoke up and said I was interested in being involved. I was very clear, though—I wanted to participate as a collaborator, not as the project lead.

I paused for a few seconds after saying that. I was wondering if it was just that old "I’m not good enough" feeling making me shrink back again. But it wasn't. I was actually thinking about what I really wanted. I realized that I’m genuinely interested in the technical side of this project, but I don’t want the pressure of constant presentations and team management. I just want to do my work well and collaborate with others to get things done.

Then this afternoon, a colleague told me, "You seem different lately." When I asked her what she meant, she said I seemed to have more of my own ideas, but without that feeling of being desperate to prove something.

I’m feeling a bit uncertain now. Is this a real change? Or is it just a fleeting thought?

 I can feel that I *am* different. I’m not overthinking as much, and I don’t feel as exhausted. But I’d like to hear your thoughts—is this a step in the right direction?

I’ve been reading *Sapiens* lately, and one passage has been stuck in my head:

"Human happiness does not come from achieving grand goals, but from small, genuine connections."

A while back, I was agonizing over so many things—promotions, my age, who I "should" become. Now I realize I might have been asking the wrong questions all along.

Life is actually quite simple. Working hard, taking care of my father, and sharing tea, yoga, and conversation with real friends. Spending weekends experimenting with new recipes in the kitchen, watching a documentary, or capturing the way the light hits a certain street corner.

These things might seem trivial, but they are the moments when I feel truly fulfilled.

Perhaps we’ve all been weighed down for too long by the expectation of "what we should become." Sometimes, it’s only when we let go of those expectations that we can finally hear our own true voice.

Let’s keep encouraging each other.

July 29, 2022, past 11:00 PM

…I don’t really know how to start writing this.

I went to my first physical therapy session today. The doctor said there really is something wrong with my cervical and lumbar spine; it wasn’t just in my head. He used some professional jargon that I didn't quite catch, but the bottom line is—it’s not my fault. It’s something that can be diagnosed, something that can be treated.

Sitting there in the exam room, I suddenly felt like crying. As the doctor was examining me, I felt my eyes welling up. He didn’t say anything special; he just calmly explained the condition of my spine and mapped out a treatment plan. But it was that feeling of being "validated"... I haven't felt that in a long time.

Lately, I’ve been wondering if there’s just something fundamentally wrong with me. Not just that I have poor self-discipline, but that I’m a failure as a person—that something inside me is just broken. Every day is a cycle of the same self-reproach, feeling like there’s no point in being alive. But what the doctor said was: your body has sustained physical damage due to prolonged sitting and stress. This can be treated.

*I did something good for myself today.*

I’ve repeated that sentence in my head several times. It’s been so long since I’ve thought that way.

But I also know... this doesn't change much. My job is still the same, my life is still at a standstill, and I’m still alone. Can a diagnosis and a few PT sessions change the reality that I’m 35 and unmarried, haven't been promoted in five years, and spend every day drifting through a void?

Probably not.

But at least... at least I did something today. I didn't do nothing.

As I sat on that treatment table and the doctor pressed on a few points on my shoulders, I could feel my body slowly relaxing. In that moment, I thought, maybe I can allow myself to stop blaming myself for a little while. Maybe it’s not all my fault.

I don’t know if this is real hope or just a temporary illusion. But I want to record it tonight, before I wake up tomorrow morning and forget again.

Before I fall back into that cycle.

# Preparation for Next Week's Team Meeting | Data Visualization Proposal

**Date**: 2023-07-15  
**Meeting Time**: Next Wednesday, 14:00  
**Topic**: Visualization Optimization for User Risk Assessment Models

---

## Core Proposal Comparison

**Why Line Charts + Heatmaps outperform the current Bar Chart approach:**

1. **Time-Series Continuity** — Bar charts fragment the dynamic progression of user risk. In contrast, line charts clearly illustrate risk value trends, making it easier to identify critical inflection points.

2. **Multi-dimensional Risk Correlation** — Heatmaps can simultaneously display User ID, Risk Level, and Time Period. This avoids the visual clutter caused by the multiple groupings required in bar charts.

3. **Decision-Making Efficiency** — High-risk users can be identified intuitively through color intensity, reducing review time by 50% compared to traditional reports.

**Data Sample**: Past 6 months, 3000+ active users, 12 risk indicators.

---

## Presentation Outline (8-minute limit)

- **Background** (1 min): Pain points of current visualizations → Why we need optimization.
- **Demo** (4 mins): Comparison of two real-world cases + Interactive feature demonstration.
- **Technical Implementation** (2 mins): Tools used, data update frequency, and system load assessment.
- **Next Steps** (1 min): Pilot scope & feedback collection plan.

---

## Potential Q&A & Prepared Responses

**Q: Will this increase the system load?**  
A: We have verified this in the test environment. Heatmap rendering takes <200ms, so it won't impact the generation speed of existing reports.

**Q: Why not just use dashboarding tools to generate this?**  
A: Our data updates in real-time, whereas standard dashboards often have latency. This SQL + Python solution allows for near real-time updates.

**Q: Can this be applied to other models?**  
A: Yes. I have already tested it on the anti-fraud model, and the results were equally significant.

---

## Personal Reflection

This is the first time I’ve taken the initiative to present my own ideas at a team meeting.

In the past, I always waited for my manager to call on me, or only spoke up when directly asked. But this time was different—when my manager asked, "Does anyone want to share their thoughts?", I didn't stay silent as usual. Instead, I said, "Let me organize my thoughts."

At that moment, I realized that my ideas have always been there. The problem wasn't a lack of ideas; it was that I never gave myself the chance to express them. Now, all I need to do is articulate this idea clearly.

See you next Wednesday.

September 10, 2022, 11:47 PM

Just got back from my parents' place. Same as always.

Mom was in the kitchen asking how work was going, saying I looked exhausted. I really wanted to give her a proper answer, but I ended up pivoting to insurance instead. I asked if she and Dad had bought supplemental medical insurance and how their physical exam reports turned out. Mom paused for a moment and said, "Only thirty-five, and you're already worrying about our retirement."

I didn't answer. I just kept asking about Dad’s blood pressure.

Now I’m lying in bed, and that sentence keeps playing over and over in my head.

Mom was trying to show she cared; she could see I was tired. But what did I do? I turned her concern into a checklist. Insurance, elder care, medical expenses. Like I was managing a project. Cold and clinical.

Why am I always like this? Why is it that when I talk to them, I just can’t act like a normal daughter? They’re worrying about me, while I’m busy calculating the cost of their future.

And Mom was right. Thirty-five. I’m worrying about my parents’ old age, and they’re worrying about my life. No one is doing well.

What about my life? What am I even doing? Five years at this job, no promotion. Alone. Those Japanese cookbooks are still sitting in the cabinet. I’ve even forgotten the passwords to my online course accounts. Weekends are supposed to be for resting, but I just lie in bed scrolling through my phone, wondering why I can’t do the things everyone else seems to manage.

Mom asked if I was seeing anyone. I said no. She didn't push it.

I know she wanted to ask why. I want to know why, too.

Work tomorrow. Monday again.

Whatever, I’m done thinking. Time to sleep.

Saturday, February 12, 2022, 14:15

Another afternoon home alone.

I came back from my parents' place yesterday. Mom mentioned that Dad has been walking a bit slowly lately and asked if I could take him to the hospital for a checkup. I said "okay," and then... nothing. I didn't actually make an appointment, nor did I ask Dad exactly what was bothering him. I just said "okay" and turned back to organizing my insurance documents.

Those documents are scattered all over my desk right now. They are covered in highlighter marks—yellow for premiums, green for coverage, and red for things that look problematic. I even built an Excel sheet comparing long-term care products from five different companies. The data is clear: if both my parents live to 85, based on current medical inflation rates, the care costs will be roughly...

I stopped. Not because I couldn't crunch the numbers, but because I suddenly realized—even if I get every single digit right, it won't change a thing.

Last night, I signed up for a forum to see how other only daughters are planning. One post put it bluntly: "I'm 35. I've already spent 500,000 on my parents and expect to spend another million. My own savings are only 800,000. If I live another 50 years, this money won't even come close to covering my own retirement."

My breathing quickened as I read that. Truly, I could feel my chest tightening.

This isn't a data problem. It’s... I don’t know how to put it. It’s a fundamental problem. I’ve always made the "safe choices"—a stable job, rational planning, a cautious life. But now I’m seeing the finish line of those choices, and it looks desperate.

I sit here in the dark, asking myself over and over: Can I really handle all of this?

Can I?

I don’t know.

I looked up long-term care insurance, then the legal responsibilities of an only child, then whether my salary will lose its value in ten years. I made tables, took notes, and highlighted key points. I wrapped my anxiety in rationality, pretending that by doing so, I could control it. But deep down, I know I’m just running away.

Because the real problem isn't in these documents. The real problem is: What am I supposed to do?

Should I jump ship for a higher-paying job? But I don't know how. Should I be more proactive about fighting for a promotion? But I'm not even sure I have the ability. Should I get married and have kids? But I can barely take care of myself.

Since I was a child, Mom taught me to be cautious and stay out of trouble. I listened. I’ve always been cautious. But now I’m realizing that caution might just be another form of evasion.

Dad’s pace is slowing down. Mom’s fingers are trembling. And here I am, sitting here with a highlighter, scribbling notes on insurance papers, pretending I’m doing something meaningful.

Can I really handle all of this?

I don’t know.

But I have to know.

# Ingredient Pairing Notes | 6.3

Just got back from the teahouse, and my mind is still buzzing with our conversation from this afternoon. I need to get this down quickly before I forget.

## On the Pairing of Fish and Tofu

Sister Li asked me why I chose to pair white fish with soft tofu. The reason I blurted out at the time feels even more interesting now that I think about it—it’s not because a recipe told me to, but because their "textural logic" aligns. The delicacy of the white fish and the softness of the tofu... one is the firmness of animal protein, the other is the yielding nature of plant protein. How should I put it? It’s like two different kinds of softness having a conversation.

I was flipping through the chapter on the Agricultural Revolution in *Sapiens*, where Yuval Harari mentions how diet shaped human social structures. It suddenly hit me—ingredient pairing is also a form of "cultural dialogue." Japanese cuisine is particularly meticulous about this; it’s not just about piling delicious things together, but ensuring every ingredient has its "say."

Next time, I’ll try using kombu dashi instead of plain water to see if it makes this dialogue a bit clearer.

## Action Plan (Maybe?)

- Hit the wet market this weekend; look for exceptionally fresh white fish.
- Remember to buy that specific Japanese tofu, not the silken variety.
- Could try adding a hint of yuzu... but need to be careful not to let it upstage the main characters.

When Sister Li said "no pressure," I realized I’ve actually been projecting a certain expectation onto these little experiments—as if they only "count" if they’re a success. But the truth is, a failed dish can teach me just as much.

Next time, I’ll treat it as play rather than "completing a task."

## I. New Criteria for Assessing Career Opportunities

I used to constantly ask myself, "Why haven't I been promoted?" I now realize I was asking the wrong question.

**The new assessment criteria should be:**
- ✓ Does the job provide stable income and security (pension, medical)?
- ✓ Does the job allow me the time to care for my parents?
- ✓ Does the job make me feel trusted and valued?
- ✗ Promotion itself is no longer the sole metric of success.

My current job meets the first three. That is enough.

---

## II. Potential Work Adjustments (To be further discussed with my manager)

1. **Flexible Scheduling**
   - Fixed Wednesday afternoons at home with my father (Initial discussion held with my manager; he expressed support).
   - Possibility of remote work (Data analysis is well-suited for remote work, allowing me to handle emergencies at home).

2. **Maintaining Work Boundaries**
   - No replies to non-urgent emails on weekends (Already implemented; results are positive).
   - Clearly defined response windows.

3. **Project Allocation**
   - Prioritize projects that do not require frequent travel.
   - Avoid committing to deadlines that cannot be met on time.

---

## III. Preliminary Framework for Father’s Care Plan

**Short-term (Within 3 months)**
- One fixed day per week to go home, have dinner, and take a walk with my father.
- Assess my father’s current health status (Need to schedule a physical exam).
- Communicate with my mother regarding the division of caregiving duties.

**Mid-term (Within 6 months)**
- Purchase appropriate insurance products for my parents (Research underway).
- Establish a family doctor or regular check-up mechanism.
- Evaluate the need for domestic help.

**Long-term**
- Elder care planning (Financial, living arrangements, medical).
- Establish a more open communication style with my parents (The hardest part, but also the most important).

---

## IV. Reminders to Myself

**Professional Value ≠ Promotion**

My value lies in:
- My ability to independently complete complex data analysis.
- My manager’s trust in my professional expertise.
- The actual value my work creates for the company.
- My ability to use this job to support my life and my parents.

These are real facts; they do not change just because there is no promotion.

- [ ] Confirm specific remote work arrangements with my manager next week.
- [ ] Research insurance options for my father this weekend.
- [ ] Book a doctor’s appointment for my father’s physical exam.
- [ ] Call my mother to discuss the care plan.

February 27, 2022, 7:45 PM

I deleted those numbers.

This afternoon, sitting at my desk, I opened that retirement cost spreadsheet. Looking at the dense rows of entries—heart stents, hip replacements, long-term care costs—I deleted them one by one. It’s not that I don’t need to prepare, it’s just… I don’t know how to put it.

Dr. Wang said last time, "You’re using perfect planning to fight uncertainty." I didn’t argue then, but I didn’t really listen either. I only understood it today. Those numbers were like me trying to negotiate with death—as if by preparing enough, by making the spreadsheet detailed enough, I could somehow stop something bad from happening. But that’s impossible, isn’t it?

At the top of the sheet, I wrote a single sentence: "This is not control; this is preparation."

It looks simple, but my hand shook a little as I wrote it.

Then I messaged Sister Li to ask when the yoga class is next week. I sat there, staring at the send button for five minutes before I finally pressed it. Something like this might seem ordinary to others, but for me… it felt like making a massive decision. She replied quickly, saying I was welcome to join. I didn't overthink it further and just sent back a "thanks."

All these small things added together seem to be saying: I can’t control everything, but I can do something. I don’t know what will happen to Mom and Dad, and I don’t know if I can carry all of this, but I can go to yoga. I can delete the numbers that make me feel powerless. I can text a neighbor.

It sounds ridiculous, doesn't it? In the face of such overwhelming anxiety, these things are trivial.

But sitting here tonight, I feel a strange sense of relief. Not because the problems are solved—they aren't. People will still grow old, and I still have to face it all. But maybe… maybe accepting that is a little easier than pretending I can stop it with perfect planning?

I am not up to this responsibility. But I have no choice.

Both of these statements are true at the same time. I am learning to live with this contradiction.

Work tomorrow. Yoga next week. Keep doing those tiny things that won't change the big picture.

That's enough for now.

# Diary

**Friday, December 15, 2023, 10:00 PM**

Today, I found myself thinking about what Dr. Wang said last time. She asked me, "Do you really think your lack of follow-through is just a matter of weak willpower?"

I’ve been struggling to figure that out. But these past few months of cooking with Sister Li and the yoga group have suddenly made something click.

When I make Japanese food with Sister Li, I can stick with it. It’s not because I’ve suddenly developed iron resolve; it’s because I have company, a fixed time, and a concrete goal. We remind each other, and we fail and succeed together. In that kind of environment, I can do it.

Yoga is the same. I almost never miss a Wednesday or Friday session. It’s not that I’m incredibly self-disciplined; it’s just that people are waiting for me, and there’s a set schedule.

But those courses I signed up for alone, those books... I just can't keep up with them. I used to blame myself constantly, thinking I just lacked grit. But now I’m thinking that maybe the problem isn't me—it’s that I haven't built the right system for myself.

Change requires more than just individual willpower; it requires environment, support, and structure.

I’m not writing this to make excuses for myself. It’s just that... I finally don’t feel so guilty. I can see myself for who I am—neither "incompetent" nor "actually amazing but just lacking opportunity." It’s just... I have a clearer understanding now of what kind of environment I need to grow.

This realization makes me feel a bit lighter. It’s like I’ve been carrying something heavy and I finally understand why it was so burdensome.

My sleep has been much better lately. My mood is stable, too. Sometimes I feel this inexplicable sense of contentment—not because anything big happened, but just... being able to accept myself as I am and seeing myself slowly change is enough.

I want to talk this through with Dr. Wang during our next session.

# Self-Reflection Memo
**Date: June 2, 2024 (Evening)**
**Keywords: Execution / Support System / Psychological Barriers**

---

## Today’s Discovery

While planning the photo walk with Sister Li, I said something that I feel the need to write down:

**"It’s not that I’m afraid of organizing events; I’m just afraid of bearing all the responsibility alone. With someone by my side, everything becomes simple."**

This sentence is important. I need to understand it.

---

## The Real Reason for My Execution Barriers (Redefined)

I used to think I just had poor execution. I bought books I never finished, signed up for classes I didn't stick with, and constantly procrastinated on things I wanted to do. I’ve blamed myself for this for a long time.

But today I realized: **The problem isn't ability; it's psychological pressure.**

Specifically:
- When I take on a project alone, I overthink everything that could go wrong.
- I double-check every detail, terrified of missing something.
- This perfectionist anxiety drains my energy until I eventually give up.
- This isn't laziness; it's being crushed by my own worries.

But when Sister Li planned the event with me:
- The responsibility was shared.
- I was no longer the sole decision-maker.
- Even if something goes wrong, someone is there to face it with me.
- This sense of security allows me to relax, think, and execute.

**Conclusion: My execution issues = Perfectionist anxiety + Lack of a support system.**

---

## The Role of a Support System

The success of this photo walk isn't because I suddenly became a "doer." It’s because:

1. **Shared Responsibility**: Having Sister Li to consult with meant I wasn't fighting a lone battle.
2. **Emotional Support**: Her enthusiasm and encouragement dissolved my anxiety.
3. **Practical Assistance**: Having someone to brainstorm and share tasks with.
4. **Psychological Safety**: Knowing that even if things aren't perfect, they will be accepted.

This made me realize something I hadn't considered before: **My "poor execution" might not be a personal flaw at all. It’s that I’ve been working the wrong way—carrying it all alone, overthinking, and striving for perfection.**

---

## Next Steps (Actionable Items)

1. **Short-term**: Continue collaborating with Sister Li on events to reinforce the effectiveness of this support system.
2. **Mid-term**: Gradually try taking on more organizational work independently, but while maintaining a foundation of support.
3. **Long-term**: Build my own "support network"—not necessarily the same person, but a circle of people who understand and are willing to stand by me.
4. **New Self-Awareness**: I don't need to transform into someone with "natural high execution." I need to find a way of working that suits me—one that involves support, companionship, and a sense of security.

---

## A Small Reminder

The next time I fall into the self-blame of "my execution is so poor," I need to remember today’s discovery.

Ask myself: **Is it that I truly can't do it, or am I trying to do it in a way that causes me pain?**

It’s very likely the latter.

---

*9:45 PM, sitting on the sofa, recording these thoughts on my phone. Will continue discussing event details with Sister Li tomorrow.*

# The "Right Way" to Open a Box of Pasta—Or Rather, Some Discoveries About Thinking Itself

**Updated August 5th, Afternoon**

Lately, while doing my homework on pasta, I stumbled upon some interesting things. Nothing earth-shattering, but for me personally, it felt like opening a new door.

---

## Starting from History

It started with just wanting to cook a decent meal, but I ended up diving headfirst into the history of pasta. While researching, I learned that pasta as we know it didn't truly take shape until the Middle Ages, and that the pairings of shapes and sauces across different regions follow a specific logic—the North leans toward butter and cream, while the South favors tomatoes and olive oil. This isn't random; it’s dictated by geography, climate, and the availability of produce.

I jotted all this down in my notebook and even drew a simple map with annotations. A colleague saw it and laughed, saying, "It's just cooking; is all that really necessary?"

In the past, I would have felt a bit embarrassed, thinking I was "overthinking" things. But a recent gathering changed my perspective on this.

---

## On Being "Different"

That day, Sister Li was in the kitchen watching how I handled the sauce. I was explaining why I use water at different temperatures to adjust the consistency, and how to balance the acidity of the tomatoes with the richness of the cream. She suddenly said, "You know, this is what makes you different from everyone else."

I got a bit nervous, thinking she was about to offer some criticism. Instead, she said, "Others might just follow a recipe, but you’re thinking. You’re asking *why*."

In that moment, I realized I had always treated this trait as a flaw—feeling like I thought too much, acted too little, and wasn't "decisive" enough. But actually... maybe this is just my way. Not a defect, but a methodology.

---

## A Small Experiment with Four Sauces

For this gathering, I tried four different sauce pairings, all with detailed notes:

**1. Tomato Basil (Pomodoro)**
- Used San Marzano tomatoes (lower acidity, higher sweetness).
- Kept the heating time under 15 minutes to preserve the freshness of the tomatoes.
- Added the basil at the very end to prevent the aroma from dissipating.

**2. Creamy Bacon (A Carbonara Variant)**
- Instead of the traditional egg yolk, I used a combination of Parmesan and black pepper.
- Heat control is key—you can't let the cream separate, so I mixed it after taking it off the heat.
- Data: A ratio of pasta water to cream of about 1:1.5 worked best.

**3. Seafood Tomato Sauce**
- Sautéed garlic and chili in olive oil first to release the fragrance.
- The ratio of tomato sauce to white wine was 2:1.
- Seafood went in last to maintain its texture.

**4. Pesto**
- This was the most "thought-intensive"—the ratio of basil, pine nuts, Parmesan, and olive oil directly affects the layers of flavor.
- My formula: 100g basil, 30g pine nuts, 40g cheese, 80ml olive oil.
- No food processor; I used a mortar and pestle to grind it slowly, which preserves the cell structure of the basil.

Seeing my friends' reactions when they ate made me feel that these "overthought" details were worth it.

---

## Current Thoughts

I’m thinking that maybe I’ve always had a biased self-evaluation. It’s not a "lack of execution," but rather that I "haven't found the true motivation." When I cook for myself, I procrastinate and hesitate; but when I know friends are coming over, I can turn all that thinking into action.

This has made me rethink a lot of things. Work, too. Maybe whether I get promoted or not isn't that important. What matters is—what am I doing that makes me feel like it's meaningful?

Sister Li said my thoughts deserve to be seen. I used to think that was just a polite platitude. But now, I’m starting to believe that maybe it’s true.

---

## Next Steps

In August, I want to try Japanese home cooking. I’ve already bought a few books and am studying the dashi ratios for miso soup and how to pair seasonal ingredients. My friends asked when we’re getting together again, and I said—maybe we could make it a regular thing?

The idea makes me a little nervous, but mostly, I’m looking forward to it.

---

*p.s. If anyone else likes to look up a mountain of data before cooking, feel free to leave a comment. I want to know if I’m the only one. Or is this actually a very common "affliction" that no one wants to admit to?*

Today, I actually said it, and it was well-received.

May 6, 2023 – Just past 10 PM

I just got out of the shower and I’m sitting on my bed, but I still feel like I need to write down what happened today.

When I went to the wet market with Sister Li this afternoon, I suddenly realized something strange—I didn't look things up beforehand like I usually do. When we came across a vegetable I didn't recognize, I actually just asked her directly. We both laughed and said, "I have no idea," and then we just asked the vendor. It was that simple.

But for me, it didn't feel simple at all.

I used to feel like I had to be fully prepared before I dared to do anything. I’d check recipes before buying groceries, watch tutorials before learning to cook, and even rehearse my phrasing several times in my head before a conversation. It was like... I had to "possess the knowledge" to be worthy of trying. If I didn't know something, it felt shameful.

I didn't feel that way today.

When Sister Li took my arm, I actually went stiff for a split second. I could feel my body resisting. But I didn't pull away. I just... followed her lead. It sounds like such a small thing, but sitting here thinking about it now, my heart still races a little. That feeling of being seen, of being accepted—it’s still so foreign to me.

The thing that shook me the most was what she said: "No pressure."

I always thought my problem was a lack of execution. The books I bought but never finished, the classes I signed up for but didn't stick with, the plans that all fell through halfway. I’ve been constantly blaming myself, thinking I’m just someone who lacks willpower. But today it suddenly hit me... maybe the problem isn't execution itself? Maybe I’m just putting too much pressure on myself?

What if I didn't demand that I "must persist," "must finish," or "must have results"?

The thought is a bit terrifying. Because thinking this way means my previous self-evaluations might have been wrong. It also means I need to redefine what "success" looks like. I don't know if this is a release or a breakdown.

When she asked if I was coming back next week, I nodded without a moment's hesitation. No guilt, no expectation that "I’ll probably flake." I just... wanted to go.

But I’m still scared. Scared that this time will be like all the others—starting with excitement and then slowly fading away. Scared that I’ll disappoint myself again. Scared that this is all just a temporary illusion.

But what about that laughter? That laugh we shared in the market. It was real. It wasn't to please anyone, or to show that I was fitting in. I was... just genuinely happy. How long has it been since I laughed like that?

Maybe this time really can be different.

Maybe.

But I still don't quite dare to believe it.

[Image: A screenshot of a book page on Douban, staying on the table of contents]

Saw another recommendation, so I bought it. I’ll definitely finish it this time.

...Never mind. Forget I said anything.

January 23, 2023. Evening.

Sitting at my desk, the clamor of the cafeteria is still ringing in my ears.

I made a small decision at noon today—to go to the cafeteria and eat with my colleagues instead of opening my lunchbox at my desk like I usually do. I’m not even sure why I did it so suddenly. Maybe it was because of those recent conversations with Dr. Wang; she said I could "try making some small changes." Or maybe I was just in a decent mood today.

The strangest thing was that Xiao Wang, who was sitting across from me, said, "Xiaoyu, you look different today."

I froze for a second. Different? How was I different? I wasn't wearing makeup, my hair was just tied up haphazardly, and I was wearing that same gray sweater. But I didn't deny it or make a self-deprecating joke like I usually would. I just smiled and said, "Maybe it’s because the weather is nice today."

Thinking back now, that smile was real. It wasn't one of those polite, fake smiles, it was... I don't know how to describe it. A kind of acceptance?

But the thing is, am I really changing? Or was it just a whim? Sitting here alone in the office, I’m suddenly hit by a strange sense of unease. If this really is change, what does that mean? Does it mean I’ve been wasting my time all these years? Does it mean I can actually become a different person?

I’m afraid of the answer.

I’m also afraid of its opposite—what if this isn't a real change? If tomorrow I’m back at my desk, eating alone, working alone, living alone... how disappointing would that be?

Dr. Wang said last time that change doesn't happen in an instant; it’s built up bit by bit. Does going to the cafeteria for lunch count? Does it count as a "little bit"?

I’m not sure. But at least today, someone saw something different. At least today, I didn't deny it.

Maybe that’s enough?

Maybe it’s not.

I don't know.

I asked him about the timeline and whether this would impact the progress of my existing projects. Then I told him that if I were to take this on, I’d need clear deadlines and resource allocation; otherwise, I couldn't commit to it. He looked a bit surprised, but he agreed.

My life has felt so much fuller lately—photography walks, yoga, time with friends... these things give me a lot of fulfillment. Work is still important, but it’s no longer the only place where I can prove my worth.

I’m a bit unsure if this is a good thing. Is it that I’ve truly grown, or am I just avoiding things? What do you think?

# February 1, 2023, 8:30 PM

Sitting at my desk with the consultation appointment slip in my hand, I still can’t quite believe I actually did it.

When I walked out of Dr. Wang’s office this afternoon, my head was spinning. One thing she said keeps looping in my mind: "You aren't a flawed person; these are reasonable reactions to a specific high-pressure environment." I’ve re-read the notes she gave me several times. Maybe I’ve been misunderstanding myself all along.

Before, I always felt like something was wrong with me. Poor execution, not extroverted enough, unable to say no, prone to anxiety... it felt like these were symptoms of some disease inside me. But Dr. Wang asked me, "If a person grew up in an environment full of expectations and restrictions, was hurt in a relationship where she was constantly asked to change, and stayed for five years in a position where she felt invisible—how would she react?"

I didn't answer. But I knew the answer.

She isn't flawed. She’s just tired.

I wrote that sentence in my diary, over and over. Pressing my fingers against the paper, it felt like I was confirming something.

Sister Li was right that day. I really did need help. I thought that meant I was weak, but now... maybe it just means I’m honest enough. Brave enough to admit I don’t know what to do.

Opening my calendar, I marked the next session on February 15th. Next to it, I wrote down three topics:
- Why is communication with my parents always so stiff?
- Do I really want the promotion? Or am I just meeting expectations?
- How can I learn to say "no" without hurting myself?

As I wrote this, it suddenly hit me: this is the first time I’ve taken such an active role in planning for my mental health. It wasn't forced, and it wasn't because I felt I "should"—it was because I truly want to understand myself.

It’s a strange feeling. Like I’ve been fumbling in the dark for a long time, and suddenly someone handed me a lamp. The light is still small and doesn't reach very far, but at least... I can see the path beneath my feet.

I still have to go to work tomorrow, still have to face those emails and data. But maybe... maybe I can look at it all in a different way.

Not a flaw. A reaction.

I need to remember that.

I opened my chat with Dr. Wang on WeChat, then closed it—three times.

In the end, I decided to send it.

---

**Draft message to Dr. Wang** (revised multiple times):

Hello, Dr. Wang. I know it’s a bit late, but there’s something I wanted to share with you. I think I might need your professional opinion.

My sleep has been terrible these past two weeks. I can’t fall asleep until one or two in the morning, and even then, I keep waking up. I feel drained at work during the day, yet my mind is hyperactive—always spiraling into dark thoughts. I worry about my father’s health, about what life will look like in ten or twenty years, and whether my own body is starting to fail.

Today, I found a self-assessment scale for anxiety online and went through the symptoms one by one. The result was "moderate anxiety."

I wanted to ask: does this mean I need treatment? Or is this just a normal reaction to stress?

I’ve also noticed that my anxiety seems to be expanding. It started with worrying about how to care for my father as he ages, but now it’s bled into a fear of my own future. It feels a bit out of control.

I’m sorry to bother you so late. If you have time, I’d like to discuss this with you during our next session.

---

I stared at the text, then deleted the last sentence.

I changed it to:

I’d like to discuss this with you during our next session.

---

Then I deleted "I’m sorry to bother you so late."

I changed it to:

But if it’s too late, please don’t feel pressured to reply right away.

---

My finger hovered over the send button.

Deep down, I knew I might not actually send this. Just like the books I buy but never read, I would usually just re-read these words over and over, checking for flaws in the phrasing, worrying if I sounded too frantic or too fragile.

But this time, I didn’t hit delete.

I pressed send.

Then I immediately locked my phone screen.

# April 21, 2022, Night

Compromised again.

I stood in the supermarket for nearly ten minutes, staring at that fish and doing the math. 68 yuan. My budget was 50. I told myself it was a "flexible adjustment," but in reality, I just gave up. I ended up buying tofu—the same chilled tofu I made two weeks ago.

Looking at my shopping basket at the checkout, seeing just those few items, I suddenly felt a wave of misery. This isn't how I planned things to be.

It’s been a month. I’ve stuck with it for a month. One new dish every week, documenting every single one on Douban. My colleagues have been eating my bento boxes, and some even said they’re delicious. Logically, I should feel good about this. So why am I getting more and more exhausted?

Choosing what to cook has become a struggle. Every time I open those three cookbooks, my brain just turns to mush. It has to be fresh, cheap, simple, documentable, and make people think, "Wow, you’re really improving." I spend forever overthinking every dish, and then while cooking, I have to take photos, write notes, analyze the flavors... Sometimes a single dish takes three hours. Is it worth it?

When I ask myself that question, I already know the answer. It’s not. But I can’t stop.

Because I’ve already invested so much. I spent money on the cookbooks, money on ingredients every week, time on the photos and records, and now my colleagues expect my bentos... If I quit now, what was this past month for? It would just prove that I really have no follow-through. That I’m exactly the kind of person who buys a bunch of courses and never sticks to them.

I hate this feeling. I chose to do this myself, yet now I feel trapped by it.

I checked my old college classmates' social media. Someone else got promoted. Someone else is posting photos of their kid. What am I doing? I’m agonizing over the price of a fish and then compromising with tofu. I’m spending three hours on one dish just to write a bunch of self-reflections on Douban.

Is there something wrong with me?

Tomorrow, it continues. Chilled tofu... what else should I add? Maybe tomato and eggs? Simple, cheap, doesn't require much thought. But if I do that, I won't have a "new dish." Am I going to lie to myself and call it a "creative pairing"?

Whatever. I'll deal with it tomorrow.

Going to sleep now.

## On Caring for Parents and Personal Boundaries

I’ve been thinking a lot about the question Dr. Wang asked me: "If you stop chasing that promotion, does your work still have meaning?"

I just realized that I might have been using the goal of "getting promoted" to justify every sacrifice I’ve made—the overtime, the lack of rest, even giving up on a relationship. But the promotion didn't happen, so I started doubting myself.

But that’s not actually where the problem lies.

Now that Dad has retired, Mom has started frequently asking, "When can you come home more often?" I can feel the weight of that invisible expectation. I know that as an only child, I need to take on more responsibility. But that doesn't mean I have to completely give myself up.

**Remember this:**
- Caring for parents ≠ Giving up on yourself
- Balance = Being responsible for your family while setting boundaries for your own needs

**Next time Mom makes a request:**
1. I can say no.
2. I can offer an alternative (e.g., visiting on the weekend instead of taking time off work).
3. My needs are just as important—yoga class, reading time, rest.

I am not being "unfilial." I just need to learn that taking care of others and taking care of myself are not mutually exclusive.

A stable job, a reliable income, and a routine life—these things matter to me. Not because I lack ambition, but because they give me the capacity to do what I want to do. Including taking care of my parents.

**Finally:** The next time I feel guilty, read this memo.

January 10, 2024, 8:30 PM

I dug up a diary entry from a month ago and felt a bit embarrassed. Back then, I kept asking myself on paper, "Have I really changed?" My tone was full of uncertainty. Looking back now, the answers to those questions have become quite clear.

Change is indeed happening. It’s not a sudden, dramatic transformation, but a quiet one that seeps into every corner of my life.

Take work, for example. During a team meeting last week, I took the initiative to propose a new idea for a data model. The old me would have overthought it in my head and eventually chosen silence. Now, I still think things through, but I no longer treat that thinking as "analysis paralysis"—instead, I see it as "meaningful preparation." When I actually spoke up, I wasn't even that nervous. My boss listened intently and asked a few questions. We discussed it for half an hour. It felt very different.

Then there are those afternoons making Japanese food with Sister Li. I’ve realized that I can stick with something when I have someone by my side. It’s not that I need to be supervised, but rather—having someone understand what I’m doing makes all the difference. Last week, we tried making Wagyu sushi; we failed several times, but I didn't give up. The old me would have been spiraling into self-blame long ago.

The yoga group, too. Every Wednesday night, I now proactively chat with the people there. It’s not that forced, exhausting kind of socializing; it’s just natural conversation. Someone asked what I’ve been reading lately, so I mentioned *Sapiens*. We ended up talking for an hour. I realized that I’m not someone who is "bad at socializing"; I just needed to find the right way to do it.

What brings me the most relief is that I’ve finally stopped viewing my introversion as a flaw. Being cautious isn't cowardice; it’s wisdom. Being alone isn't isolation; it’s strength. These traits used to make me feel "not enough," but now I see their value.

My conversations with Dr. Wang have helped a lot, too. She said that change doesn't require overthowing who you are; it just requires understanding yourself. I believe that now.

The only lingering regret is that these changes are so private. No one knows what I’ve been through. But maybe that’s just my way. I don’t need to prove anything to anyone. I just need to be clear with myself.

And right now, I am very clear.

I accept myself. This 35-year-old, introverted, cautious woman who is slowly changing.

Onward to tomorrow.

September 8, 2023, Evening

While chatting with a friend at a café today, I suddenly found myself saying something with total clarity: "What I want isn't a promotion or a high salary. What I want is a stable job that leaves me enough time for other important things."

The moment the words left my mouth, I surprised even myself. Not because of the thought itself, but because of how calmly and openly I was able to say it. In the past, I never dared to express this; I always felt it sounded like making excuses for my own failures.

My friend said, "Knowing what you want puts you ahead of most people."

In that moment, I felt understood. Truly understood. It wasn't the "don't worry about it" kind of comfort, but the feeling of being seen.

I’ve been constantly asking myself: Why haven't I been promoted in five years? Is it because I’m not working hard enough? Am I really just not competitive? But today I realized that maybe I’ve been asking the wrong questions all along. I’m not failing; I’m simply making a clear choice.

A stable job. Taking care of my father. Doing yoga with my group. Occasionally reading a book or trying out a new recipe. All of these things combined are more important than any job title.

I used to feel guilty about thinking this way—feeling like I lacked ambition or the drive to succeed. But I don’t feel that way anymore. This isn't a compromise; it’s a priority. This is a decision I’ve made proactively, not a reality I’ve been forced to accept.

The funny thing is, once I stopped obsessing over the promotion, I actually started doing my job better. Without that sense of restlessness, I’m able to focus more deeply on every project. My boss still trusts me just as much, and my colleagues still cooperate with me as they always have. Nothing has changed, yet my mindset has shifted completely.

As I write this, I realize I’ve finally let go of something. Maybe it’s the obsession that "success only counts if you get promoted." Maybe it’s the fear that "not being promoted by 35 is a failure."

I feel very peaceful right now. Not a fake "everything is fine" kind of peace, but a real, grounded sense of certainty.

Onward to tomorrow.

**2022-03-13**

I just spent an hour standing in the kitchen.

My mom called while I was whisking the eggs. She used that tone I know all too well: "Started another new hobby, have we?" I could tell what she was really asking—how long I’d last this time. I didn’t give her a direct answer; I just told her I’ve been doing this for three weeks now, every single week.

After hanging up, I decided to document today’s tamagoyaki.

---

## About the Dish

Tamagoyaki looks simple, but every layer requires precision. The Japanese method involves pouring the egg mixture in stages, rolling each layer up. The heat has to be just right—too high and the eggs burn; too low and they won't spread.

The cookbook I bought emphasizes the word "patience." At first, I thought it was a bit dramatic—it’s just fried eggs, right? But after actually doing it, I understand. Before every pour, you have to wait for the previous layer to semi-set; every time you roll, you have to nudge it gently with a bamboo skewer. You can’t rush it.

Today, I used Japanese eggs I bought from the supermarket; the yolks were a deep, rich orange. I once read a note on Douban saying that a good tamagoyaki should be golden on the outside, tender on the inside, with a gentle sweetness in every bite. That’s exactly what I’m aiming for.

---

## First Time vs. Third Time

In the first week, the heat was too high and the edges got charred. I took a photo of it; it’s still on my phone because I can’t bring myself to delete it.

In the second week, I switched to medium heat, but it ended up a bit undercooked. I felt a bit discouraged then, thinking maybe I’m just not cut out for things that require such delicate handling.

Today was the third time. I read the recipe three times beforehand and even wrote "medium-low heat, 30 seconds per layer" on a sticky note. A colleague saw the tamagoyaki in my bento and asked if I’d bought it from a Japanese restaurant. In that moment, I really wanted to laugh—no, I made it myself.

I think today’s result is close to what was described in that Douban note.

---

## The Word "Persistence"

When Mom asked, "Started another new hobby?" it suddenly hit me—maybe she wasn't asking if this hobby would last, but whether *I* could last.

I bought the cookbooks, signed up for the classes, and made the plans. All of that is real. But more importantly, I’ve been doing this every weekend. Not because I’m head-over-heels in love with it, but because I want to prove to myself that I’m not the kind of person who gives up after the initial excitement fades.

It’s been three weeks. There will be a fourth, and a fifth.

I wrote "Three months, one new dish per week" in my memos. Right now, it doesn't seem impossible.

---

## Plans for Next Week

I want to try tempura. Or maybe just stabilize my heat control for tamagoyaki a bit more. I haven't decided yet.

But regardless, I’m going to keep going.

Every dish is a promise kept to myself.

---

*P.S. If anyone else is learning Japanese cuisine, feel free to reach out. I’m a complete beginner, but I’m taking this seriously.*

The way my boss looks at me has changed.

I stayed silent during the meeting again this morning. When it was my turn to report on the project progress, I just read out a single number and stopped. He asked if I had anything else to add; I said no. He gave me a look... the kind of look you give someone who is sick.

This afternoon, he pulled me aside to ask how I was doing and if work had been too busy lately. I told him no, I’m fine. But I could tell he didn't believe me. He said I haven't seemed like myself recently and asked if I needed my workload adjusted.

I said no.

But deep down, I know he sees it. I thought I was hiding it well—I put on makeup this morning and wore a decent, clean outfit. But he saw right through me anyway. I can’t even get this right.

My hands are shaking. I can feel it as I type.

I didn't sleep well again last night. I was still scrolling through my phone at 3 a.m. I wasn't even processing anything; I was just scrolling. My eyes hurt today.

Those Japanese cookbooks are still on the shelf. I don't want to look at them anymore. Why bother? I feel embarrassed now, remembering how I told my colleague I was going to start making bento boxes. She hasn't asked me about it lately; she probably forgot, or maybe she realized I gave up.

Last time my mom called, she asked if work was too exhausting. I didn't answer. She asked again, and I just said no, everything is great. Her silence afterward made me feel even worse.

Is there really something wrong with me?

Why can't I stick to even the simplest things?

Why do I seem so close to breaking?

I have to go to work again tomorrow.

# MEMO

**Date:** Sunday, May 7, 2023, 19:00

**Subject:** Something my instructor said in yoga class

---

Today, my instructor said: "You are listening to your body; you are accepting your body."

At that moment, I was holding a pose I’d never been able to do before. It wasn't because I had suddenly become more flexible, but because I wasn't forcing it like I used to. I was simply listening to my body’s feedback, moving into the posture slowly and gently. When the instructor walked over, I felt a flash of tension, but instead of saying "you're not doing enough," she said those words.

I paused for a moment.

Something about that sentence hit home. Perhaps it’s because I’ve always been at war with my body—pushing through work despite a localized backache, or staring down at a screen even when my neck is screaming in protest. I’ve treated my body’s signals as obstacles to be overcome, rather than voices to be heard.

The word "acceptance" feels particularly poignant. I never thought that acceptance and effort could be the same thing. I used to think acceptance meant giving up, and giving up meant failure. But maybe it doesn't.

Maybe listening to yourself and accepting your limitations is actually a more honest way of trying.

---

**Keywords:** Listening / Acceptance / Perfectionism / Body Signals

**Reminders:** Discuss this with Dr. Wang. Also, does this apply elsewhere? Work, relationships, expectations of myself...

June 8, 2023. Late night, 23:30.

Insomnia again.

I’ve been lying in bed for nearly two hours. My mind is a mess—thinking about everything, yet processing nothing. I finally just got up, sat at my desk, and opened that notebook that’s been sitting in the corner forever.

The question Dr. Wang asked me last time keeps circling in my head: If you aren't chasing a promotion, does work still have meaning?

I don’t know. I really don’t.

But tonight I did something strange—I made a list. I just wrote down all the messy things in my heart that felt important. No particular order, just a brain dump. And then… I started to rank them.

Looking at the final result, I actually scared myself a little.

"Promotion" was way down the list. Way down.

Above it were: Taking care of my parents. Secure social connections. Personal hobbies. Physical health. Time spent with people I trust.

And the promotion? It was maybe seventh or eighth. I can’t even remember exactly.

I stared at the list for a long time, and then I wrote a giant question mark next to it: **But do I actually believe this? Or am I just escaping the failure of not being promoted?**

That question mark terrified me.

Because I don’t know the answer. I really don’t. The fact that I haven't been promoted in five years sits on my chest like a heavy stone. I always thought I was "accepting reality," but… am I just making excuses for myself? Am I using the lie of "I don't actually want it" to protect myself?

But on the other hand… when I look at the list and see "Taking care of my parents" at the very top, I feel a sense of relief. My father is retired now. They’re both getting old. I can’t keep thinking only about work and promotions like I used to. Those things are real. They aren't excuses.

I can’t believe my own thoughts. It’s so strange.

I’ve always felt like I *should* want the promotion, the higher salary, the bigger office, the more power. That’s the "right" way to feel. That’s "normal." But now I’m realizing I might not want any of it. Or rather… I’m not sure what I want anymore.

Sister Li said in the yoga group that sometimes we need to stop and ask ourselves what we *actually* want, rather than what we think we *should* want. I didn’t pay much attention at the time, but now… those words are echoing in my head.

Maybe I’ve been chasing the wrong things all along. Maybe I’m just afraid to admit it. Maybe it’s both.

I don’t know.

It’s late, it’s quiet outside, and as I sit here looking at this list and that question mark, I feel both scared and… a little relieved? It’s a contradiction. I hate contradictions. I like clear answers.

But maybe… maybe there are no clear answers. Maybe I just need to sit in this uncertainty for a while.

I’ve tucked the list away in my drawer. I’ll see how I feel about it tomorrow.

For now, I think I might finally be able to get some sleep.

# Friday Afternoon, April 12, 2024

Sitting in the office, the sunlight outside the window is blinding, so I’m wearing sunglasses just to look at my screen. My colleagues are busy with their own tasks, but my mind is still stuck on everything that happened at the cafe earlier.

I still can’t quite believe it. The photo walk this morning… I was actually the organizer.

When Sister Li asked me a couple of days ago if I’d lead everyone to capture the city’s light and shadows, my first instinct was to refuse—the idea of "hosting" made me so nervous. But she framed it so naturally, like asking "Do you want to walk with us?" rather than "Can you teach us?" That’s why I agreed.

I spent two hours preparing last night. Picking the route, thinking about what to shoot, how to explain things… I realized I was actually happy while doing it. Not that forced, "faking it" kind of happy, but a genuine desire to share these ideas with others.

I was a bit tense when we headed out this morning. But once we started walking, once I picked up my camera, everything became natural. I showed Auntie Wang how the light hit the buildings, explained why I chose certain angles, and even discussed depth of field and focus with her. I was talking. Really talking. Not just answering when asked, but proactively sharing.

The moment that hit me hardest was something Auntie Wang said. She looked at my camera, then into my eyes, and said, "Xiaoyu, you’re like a real photographer."

I was a bit lost for words. I felt shy, but more than that… I felt seen? It was as if she wasn't just saying "you take good pictures," but rather "this is who you are." I allowed myself to accept that identity. I no longer felt like I was pretending.

Later at the cafe, as everyone looked through my photos, someone said, "Wow, this one is beautiful," and I didn't just stop at a "thank you." I started explaining my thought process—why I chose that specific moment, why that composition, and I even touched on some technical details I’d been considering. I could hear myself speaking; my voice was clearer than usual, and my thoughts were sharp.

I understand now. My issues with execution aren't some fundamental flaw in my character. The things I couldn't do before weren't because I was incapable, but because I hadn't found the right way to do them. When someone believes in me, when I’m doing something I truly love, when I’m invited rather than forced… everything changes.

Those goals in my five-year plan don’t seem so distant anymore. I’m already doing it. I’m already changing.

And I’m not changing alone. I’m changing alongside Sister Li, Auntie Wang, and my therapist, Dr. Wang… alongside people who truly understand me.

Work emails are still flashing on my screen, but I don’t mind them as much now. This job is stable and secure; it gives me the time and energy to do these things. I accept it. And I’ve accepted myself—both a data analyst and a photography enthusiast, both an introvert and someone who can take the lead to share.

I believe now that I can do this.

Dr. Wang, I wanted to share something that happened during my yoga class today.

I’ve been thinking about it ever since class ended, and I feel like I need to process it a bit.

Today, while the instructor was explaining a pose, she said, "Don't fight your body; accept it as it is right now." I know that sounds like a cliché, but in that moment, it suddenly hit me—I’ve been fighting my body all this time, and in fact, I’ve been fighting my own personality, too.

I sat there on my mat, and my eyes got a little misty. It was a strange moment because I started wondering why I’m always criticizing myself—criticizing my body for not being flexible enough, criticizing myself for being too introverted, for lacking execution, or for not getting promoted or married like everyone else. It’s as if I’ve been at war with a "version of myself I should be," rather than accepting who I am right now.

I wrote this thought down in my journal later. I feel like this might be related to those patterns of self-criticism we discussed before? That mindset of—I know there’s something wrong with me, so I have to constantly fix myself, but in the process of fixing, I’m constantly rejecting who I am in the present.

I’m not saying I don’t need to change. I still want to change certain things, like my work-life balance. But maybe the prerequisite for change is that I first stop fighting myself?

The idea is still a bit fuzzy, and I’m not sure if it really makes sense. But I’d love to hear your thoughts on it.

June 28, 2023  Sunny  Past 11:00 PM

I just finished showering and I’m sitting on my bed, still a bit in disbelief about what happened today.

During the team meeting this afternoon, I spoke up on my own initiative. It wasn’t a response to a question; I actually raised my hand and said, "I think we should adjust the threshold for user risk assessment." My heart was racing a bit as I spoke, but my voice remained steady. There were over a dozen people in the conference room—I would never have done that before.

What shocked me even more was that my boss pulled me aside afterward. He told me the report was well done and specifically mentioned "your transformation."

"Your transformation"—he actually saw it.

Sitting there in the office listening to him say those words, my mind went a bit blank. Because I’m still adjusting to this change myself, having someone else point it out felt surreal. But at the same time, I felt *seen*. Not because I got a promotion or did something extraordinary, but because someone noticed that I am changing.

I’m wondering, when did this change begin? Was it when Sister Li invited me to the yoga group? Or even earlier? I’m not sure. But I do know that over the past few months, the way I view myself has indeed shifted.

I used to think I was just a steady data analyst—that finishing my work on time without making mistakes was enough. But now... now I seem to be starting to believe that I can be something else, too. I can voice my opinions in meetings, I can do yoga with strangers, and I can talk to Sister Li without feeling so nervous.

But I’m also a little scared. Is this confidence just temporary? Will I go back to being that silent person in the next meeting? Or... have I truly changed?

Regardless, the feeling today was real. That sense of groundedness when I spoke my mind, and the moment my boss mentioned "your transformation"—those were real.

I thought of something Sister Li once said: "You don't need to become an extrovert; you just need to be your authentic self." I didn't quite get it back then, but I think I understand a little better now. Maybe I always thought changing meant becoming someone else, but actually, change is just allowing yourself to show up more authentically.

Work again tomorrow. I don’t know if anything will be different. But at least for today, I know one thing: I am more than just "steady."

June 28, 2022, 7:20 PM

It’s starting again. That familiar, suffocating feeling.

At 2:00 PM, I got an email from my boss. He wants me to join next week’s departmental strategy meeting to share some new ideas on the user risk assessment model. My very first thought when I read that sentence was: *I’m going to make a fool of myself again.*

Seriously, those exact words. Not "this is an opportunity," not "I can give it a try," but "I’m going to make a fool of myself again." Even I was startled by that reaction.

Then, I spent twenty minutes editing my reply.

Draft 1: "I will prepare a detailed report and strive to present the best analytical approach possible."
— No, that’s too confident. What if I mess up the presentation? Will people think I’m full of myself?

Draft 2: "I’ll do my best to prepare, but it might not be particularly outstanding, as the topic is quite complex."
— This sounds too weak. It makes me look like I have zero confidence. Will my boss regret choosing me?

Draft 3: "I will prepare the data analysis portion and hope to provide some useful insights."
— This is the safest bet. Not overconfident, but not too retreating either.

I sat there deleting, retyping, and deleting again... watching the cursor blink while voices in my head screamed, "You’re definitely going to screw this up" and "Anyone else in this position would do so much better." Finally, I hit send.

I regretted it the second I sent it.

Sitting in the office, staring at that sent email, I kept telling myself: *Another opportunity I’m going to ruin. I don’t deserve to be trusted. Why did my boss pick me? It must be because he doesn’t realize how incompetent I am yet.*

Five years. Five years without a promotion. I always thought it was because I wasn't proactive enough or good at self-promotion. But now I’m starting to think that’s not it at all. Maybe it’s just because I’m truly not good enough.

I think about what Zhang Hao said—"Why can't you be more extroverted?" "Why are you always so negative?" At the time, I felt wronged, like he didn't understand me. But now... maybe he was right? Maybe the problem really is me.

The expired food is still in the fridge. That book is still stuck on Chapter 3. The progress bar for my online course has stopped at 15%.

I’m just someone who can’t follow through. It’s not the environment, it’s not a lack of time—it’s me. My personality, my ability, my everything.

Since I was a child, my mom taught me to be cautious and stay out of trouble. I did it. I’ve spent my whole life walking on eggshells. And the result of living like this? I’m 35, and I’m still alone. No progress at work. Nothing has changed.

I’m even starting to think that maybe things will never change. Maybe this is just my fate.

I have to go to work tomorrow. I have to go to that strategy meeting next week. I’ll sit there, listen to others talk, say a few things about data analysis, and then go back to my seat.

That’s it. It’s always like this.

I don’t know what else to do.

October 22, 2023, Night

When I went home for dinner today, my dad asked me, "How’s work lately? You seem to be in a good mood."

I was a bit taken aback. He rarely initiates conversations like this.

I told him that maybe a promotion isn't what I actually want. What I want more is meaningful work and genuine connections.

As the words left my mouth, I surprised even myself. These were things I had never said to him before. I expected him to be like my mother—to start lecturing me about being ambitious and competitive. But he didn't. He just listened quietly and nodded.

"As long as you're happy," he said. It was that simple.

Sitting there at the dining table, it suddenly hit me—maybe I’ve misunderstood him all along. It’s not that he doesn’t care; he just doesn't know how to express it. But he’s always been watching, always been listening. That silent company, those little things he does for me—that’s his way.

Why was I always waiting for him to say something, instead of understanding what he was already doing?

For years, I’ve felt like a failure. No promotion, no marriage, none of the things my former classmates have. I saw these absences as a source of shame. But after saying those words to my father today, I suddenly felt that maybe I haven't failed. Maybe I’ve just chosen a different path.

And for me, this path might be the right one.

Meaningful work. Genuine connections. A conversation like this with my father—it was long overdue, but it finally happened.

Being home alone tonight, I’m wondering: what is this feeling? It’s not excitement, not that urge to change the world. It’s just... peace. The peace of being seen.

I don’t need to become who others expect me to be. I don’t need to strive for things just to prove a point.

Maybe this is what growing up feels like.

It’s still a bit strange, though. I always thought my cynicism toward relationships was because there was something wrong with me. But now I think maybe I just haven't met someone who truly understands me yet. Or rather—I’m only just beginning to understand what true understanding actually is.

My father understood me today.

Sister Li understands.

Dr. Wang understands.

So, it’s not that I’m unworthy of being understood. I just need to find the right people.

This realization makes me feel so much lighter.

I sat on the sofa, my phone screen glowing, my thumb hovering over the input box for a long time.

Delete. Retype.

Delete again.

Outside the window, the sky had already dimmed. My yoga mat was still lying in the corner, still holding the warmth of today’s practice. Sister Li’s words kept circling in my head: "You’re different now."

I wasn't sure what she meant. I wasn't even sure if I should record this moment.

In the end, I didn't post it.

Instead, I opened my Notes app and quietly typed a single line:

"Sometimes, simply being seen is enough."

Then I turned off my phone and went to the kitchen to brew a cup of tea.

---

(A few minutes later, I opened Moments again. I scrolled through other people's updates—someone's promotion, someone's kids, someone's trip. I swiped past them without stopping.

Still, I didn't post anything. Let it be.)

📷 [A screenshot of a phone memo with several lines of notes on colorful digital sticky notes. The handwriting is neat but looks a bit rushed.]

Change begins with being seen.

After yoga class today, Sister Li asked me if something was different about me lately. I froze for a second before realizing that I really am changing, slowly. It’s not a dramatic transformation, but something very subtle—starting to speak up more, starting to believe that I’m capable of doing things.

I wrote a small three-month plan for myself. No grand goals, just some very specific little things. I used to think I just had poor self-discipline, but I’ve only recently realized—maybe the problem wasn't my execution, but that I was always forcing myself to do things I didn't actually want to do.

A bit nervous to post this, but I wanted to document this moment.

#Yoga #Change #BeingSeen

May 8, 2022  11:15 PM

Another day of overtime, staying until 8:30 PM. By the time I got home, I was so exhausted I could barely move.

I had originally planned to make an upgraded version of tempura today—I wanted to experiment with different flour ratios and document the whole process with photos. But the moment I sat down on the sofa, I knew I couldn't do it. My mind was a total blank, and my hands felt heavy. I opened the fridge, stared at the ingredients, and eventually decided to just make miso soup. The same recipe I used a month ago.

No photos. No step-by-step notes. I didn't carefully consider every detail like I used to. I just mechanically chopped the vegetables, added the broth, and let it boil. After eating, I opened Douban, intending to update this week’s notes. But then I closed it again.

I told myself I’d make up for it next week.

But I know I’m lying to myself.

Sitting here, I’m thinking—is this the first time? The first time I’ve actively skipped it. No matter how tired or frustrated I was before, I’d always take a photo, or at least write a few words. But today, I just... gave up. I didn't want to record anything. I didn't want to prove that I was sticking with it.

This project has been going on for a month now. I’ve spent so much money on cookbooks and ingredients, and so much time in the kitchen and writing notes on Douban. I even posted on WeChat Moments saying this was my "new commitment." And now? Now I can’t even make it through a single month before I start slacking off.

I find myself thinking about my old classmates from university. They’re getting promoted, getting married, posting photos of their kids. And what am I doing? I’m giving up on a cooking plan. I’m telling myself "I'll make it up next week" while knowing full well I won't.

This isn't the first time. Those courses I bought, those books... it’s always the same. I start with so much passion, stick with it for a while, and then start making excuses. Work is too busy, I’m too tired, I’m not in the right mood. Eventually, I just quit entirely.

Am I truly incapable of sticking to anything?

Or is it that I have no idea what I actually want, which is why it’s so easy to give up?

I’ve already invested so much. If I quit now, everything I’ve done before becomes meaningless. But if I keep pushing... I don't know what the point of that is either.

Whatever. I don’t want to think about it anymore. I have work tomorrow.
