January 22, 2023, 7:00 PM

I dug out my old diary from college. I’m not sure why I suddenly thought of it today; maybe it’s because my mind has been spinning in circles lately, and I was looking for some proof that I wasn't always like this.

I flipped to the entries from 2018. Back then, I was still writing down thoughts about statistics and my passion for data analysis. The handwriting was neat, with little charts doodled in the margins. It seems like the "me" from that time was genuinely curious about everything.

Then I turned to the pages from five years ago. In early 2019, I wrote: "Am I really not good enough? Why can't I ever get things right?" The same questions were repeated over and over below it, the handwriting becoming a mess. Seeing those words, I felt a sudden, indescribable wave of sadness. It was like reading the diary of a disappointed stranger, only that stranger was me.

I don’t remember exactly what happened back then. Maybe it was a project at work that went south, or something else entirely. But the point is, from that moment on, I seemed to get stuck in a loop—every time I messed something up, I’d ask myself, "Am I really not good enough?" and I never managed to ask anything new after that.

During those four years of college, I spent so much time in the library—not because I was running away from anything, but because I truly wanted to understand those things. And now? Now I open my laptop every day, finish my work, and close it again, like a program running on autopilot.

Tonight, standing in front of the mirror, I whispered to myself: "Maybe it’s time for a change."

But I’m not sure if I actually want to change, or if I’m just tired. Maybe it’s both.

I’m seeing Dr. Wang tomorrow. I have no idea what I’m going to say to her. I’m afraid that saying too much will make me look fragile, but I’m also afraid that saying too little will make her feel like I’m wasting her time. I’m afraid she’ll tell me truths I already know but have been running away from.

Yet, I’m also a little hopeful. Maybe I’m just looking forward to having someone listen to me—someone who won't make me explain why I am the way I am, or make me apologize for my failures.

Maybe.

I keep using that word, "maybe."

Maybe that in itself says something.

Sometimes, the most ordinary scenes suddenly take on a deeper meaning.

Perhaps it’s not that the scenery has changed, but rather the way I look at it.

I stood on the street corner for a while today, capturing these moments on my phone. No particular reason—I just felt like... this moment deserved to be seen.

Grateful for those who, at one point or another, made me feel understood.

September 3, 2022. Late night, 23:47.

Insomnia again.

I’ve been lying here for an hour, and my head is still buzzing. I just opened my phone again and searched for a bunch of things—“the 35-year-old woman’s dilemma,” “how to fix a lack of execution,” “living life alone”... I feel even more hopeless after reading them. Those articles say things like “change starts now” or “persistence pays off.” I just want to laugh. Persistence? I don’t even know what I’m supposed to be persisting at.

Those three Japanese cookbooks are still on the shelf. I don’t even want to look at them. They still have the sticky notes I wrote—"one new dish every week." It looks like a joke now. The course certificates are still there, too. I was so excited when I bought them; I told my colleagues, "This time is different." And now? Now I’m just a fraud.

I wrote the words "poor execution" on a sticky note. Then I crossed them out. Wrote them again. Crossed them out again.

Why can everyone else do it? Xiao Wang can juggle projects while learning new things; Sister Li sticks to her yoga and even organizes group activities... Why can’t I get anything done? I can’t even follow through on a simple cooking plan. What does that say about me? It says I can’t change at all. This is who I am—someone incapable of change.

Work is still all overtime; the weekend was swallowed by bugs. My neck hurts like hell, but I haven't gone to the hospital. It’s a waste of time anyway. Now I’ve even lost my sleep. I was just looking at sleeping pills... I didn't buy any, but I was looking. What does that even mean?

My old classmates updated their Moments again. Another promotion. Another person posting photos of their kid. Here I am at 35, still dealing with insomnia, staring blankly at these books and courses.

Mom asked me last time, "Are you just too tired from work?" I didn't answer. How could I? Tell her that I’m not just tired, but that I feel completely useless? Tell her I’ve even started to doubt whether I’m fundamentally incapable of change?

I’m just stuck. Stuck in my job, stuck in my age, stuck in my body, and stuck in myself.

I have work tomorrow.

I really... I don't know what to do anymore.

December 3, 2023 Afternoon

I just got back from Sister Li’s place, my hands still dusted with a bit of flour. Sitting on the sofa, I suddenly felt a bit lost on what to do, so I opened my diary.

What happened today might not seem like a big deal, but I can feel its weight.

During the cooking activity, I took the initiative to suggest an idea—about how to adjust the sauce proportions. In the past, I definitely would have replayed it in my head a dozen times and then chosen silence for fear of saying the wrong thing. But today, I just said it. And when someone offered a different opinion, I was able to defend my idea with clear logic, even explaining why I thought it would work better.

The moment I finished speaking, I felt something. It wasn't nervousness, but... being understood? Sister Li listened to me intently and nodded. No one else in the group mocked me; instead, someone said, "Sounds good, let's give it a try."

What surprised me most was that as the activity was ending, someone asked if I’d be coming back next time. They even said they enjoyed doing these things with me. I was a bit stunned. I never imagined that I would be actively "needed" like that.

On the way home, I was thinking—this is exactly what I’ve always been afraid of: making a fool of myself, being judged, saying the wrong thing. But today I discovered that even if what I say isn't perfect, no one is going to reject me for it. On the contrary, my ideas were heard and valued.

It reminds me of what Dr. Wang said. She said my problem isn't that I'm unfit for socializing, but that I’ve been waiting for "perfect acceptance." But in reality, perfect acceptance doesn't exist—there is only "good enough acceptance." And I am experiencing that kind of acceptance right now.

I wonder if it would have been better if I had understood this sooner. But perhaps it’s precisely because I’ve spent so many years waiting and doubting myself that I can truly feel the weight of this warmth in this moment.

I have work tomorrow. But tonight, I want to look over some Japanese recipes again. Maybe next time, I can take the initiative to invite Sister Li over to my place.

The thought makes me a little nervous, but more than that, I feel expectant.

September 2, 2023. Night.

I just got back from my parents' place. I haven't even washed up yet; I just sat down on the edge of the bed and opened my diary. My fingers still carry the warmth of the kitchen.

My mom told me in the kitchen that when she was young, she also thought about going to work in a big city. That one sentence left me completely stunned. For over twenty years, I thought she was simply someone who valued "caution and stability"—as if that was all there was to her. But when she spoke about those old dreams, there was a light in her eyes I’d never seen before. It wasn't regret; it was more like... peace? When she said, "I'm proud of you," she took my hand. I could feel the warmth of her palm.

I blurted out, "I'm proud of you, too."

The moment I said it, I realized something had shifted. Not her, and not me, but the relationship between us. I was no longer just listening to her tell me what to do; it was more like... two adults truly seeing each other. It was a strange feeling, but incredibly real. My eyes stung, but I didn't cry.

My dad talked a lot, too. He’s been helping people in the community and making new friends. I never knew that. As he sat there sharing these little stories, there was a sense of vitality in his voice that I rarely hear. It suddenly clicked—he’s responding to me in his own way. He sees that I’m changing, so he’s changing, too.

The whole dinner felt like a confirmation of everything I’ve been thinking about since July: change really is possible. Not the kind of change where you have to completely reinvent yourself, but... slowly understanding yourself, and slowly being understood. I don't need to become someone else, and neither do my parents. We just need to be real with each other.

On the subway ride back, I kept wondering why my mom saying "I'm proud of you" touched me so deeply. I suppose it’s because I’ve been waiting to be seen. And now, I finally feel seen. Not just by them, but by myself.

I should remember to mention this family dinner to Dr. Wang before I go to sleep. She might have some thoughts on it.

Goodnight.

# Diary

**Sunday, June 18, 2023 | Sunny**

Sitting in the office, the sunlight outside is blinding. That conversation I had with my boss last Friday is still looping in my head.

I keep wondering where I found the courage to say those things. "My father has been having some health issues lately, and I need to go home regularly to take care of him"—such a simple sentence, yet I rehearsed it in my mind countless times. Every single time, I worried: Would this be seen as unprofessional? Would it change how my boss views me? Would it become yet another hurdle on my path to promotion?

But I said it anyway.

My boss’s reaction was completely unexpected. He didn’t frown; there was none of that "this is going to be a hassle" look. He just nodded and said we could discuss more flexible work arrangements. Remote work, flexible leave... things I never imagined would be offered proactively.

I let out a long breath. Truly, in that moment, I felt a sense of relief I’ve never known before.

It made me realize something strange—for the past five years, I’ve been operating under the assumption that career and life must be kept separate, that I had to hide my real needs to protect my professional image. But in reality, maybe no one actually cares if I’m "totally devoted" to work. Maybe my boss just needs to know what I’m doing, rather than needing me to hide everything.

I don’t need a promotion to prove myself. Saying that now, the thought feels incredibly clear.

I used to think being introverted and cautious were my flaws. Back then, Zhang Hao said I wasn't outgoing enough, and I kept reflecting on whether something was wrong with me. But now... I don’t know, maybe there’s nothing wrong with those traits. I can do my job well, I can take care of my family, and I can speak up for myself when I need to. This is who I am. I don’t need to change into someone else.

When I went home yesterday, my father looked much better. We sat in the living room, and he asked how work was going. Instead of brushing him off with a quick "it's fine" like I used to, I actually talked to him for a while. I told him about my recent struggles and my new thoughts on work. He listened intently and even offered some advice. It was only then that I realized he’s always been listening; I just never truly spoke to him.

My mother was in the kitchen, and I could hear her humming. Moments like these make me feel more fulfilled than any promotion ever could.

I’m still thinking—maybe I’ve always split my life into two parts: the "work me" and the "life me." But they were always meant to be one whole. I don’t need to switch between two identities; I am just me. An introverted, cautious, but reliable person. That is enough.

That is more than enough.

# Some New Thoughts on Execution

Lately, while cooking for friends, I suddenly realized something: I’ve always thought I had poor execution, but I might have been misinterpreting myself.

In the past, it always went like this—I’d see a recipe, and my mind would start racing: *What if I mess up this step? Should I adjust the heat? Is this the optimal combination of ingredients?* I’d get trapped in overthinking, and in the end, I’d either give up or do it half-heartedly. I used to call this "procrastination" and saw myself as an indecisive person.

But recently, things changed when I started going to the market with Sister Li. We’ll see an ingredient, a dish immediately comes to mind, we buy it right then and there, and I cook it as soon as we get back. We’ve made over a dozen dishes together this month, which would have been impossible for me before.

I finally understand—execution isn’t about how long you can grit your teeth and persist; it’s about how clear your goals and motivations are.

My problem before wasn't "thinking too much"; it was the lack of a connection between my thoughts and my actions. I was thinking, but my goals were blurry: "I have to make it perfect," "I have to stick with it," "I have to prove I have good execution." These goals were too abstract, so my thinking turned into a tangled mess.

Now, it’s different. My goals are concrete: "I want to make a delicious meal for my friends," "I want to try this new recipe." With a clear goal, my thinking becomes about optimization and creativity rather than an obstacle.

I’m starting to feel that my ability to think is actually an asset, not a flaw. I can be a thinker and a doer at the same time—as long as the goal is clear enough and the motivation is real enough.

The most interesting part is that once I let go of the expectations to "be perfect" or "must persist," I actually found it easier to keep going. When there’s no pressure, I end up doing more.

This realization has brought me a sense of relief. Maybe I’m not someone who "lacks execution"; I’ve just been holding myself to the wrong standards.

---

*P.S. This note got some likes and comments. Someone said, "This perspective is really inspiring," which surprised me. It turns out that sharing my own thoughts can actually help others.*

Dr. Wang, I wanted to share something that just happened with me.

Earlier today, I finally plucked up the courage to email my boss. I told him that the recent workload has been quite heavy and that I was hoping to adjust my schedule. To be honest, my hands were shaking as I hit send. I must have rewritten it a dozen times, terrified that any wrong word would make me look unprofessional or uncommitted.

But his response was completely unexpected. Not only did he agree, but he also proactively asked to meet this Friday to "discuss specific adjustments to the workflow." I’m still processing it—I’ve always operated under the assumption that the workplace is all or nothing: you’re either giving it your absolute everything, or you’re being unprofessional.

Our preliminary agreement is that I can leave after 3:00 PM on Wednesdays and Fridays, and that after-hours emails are no longer considered urgent, allowing for more flexibility. You know, Dr. Wang, when I saw his response, it hit me—maybe the problem wasn't a lack of ability or discipline, but the fact that I never actually communicated my real needs. I’ve just been enduring it in silence, constantly assuming people would be dissatisfied with me.

It reminds me of the question you asked last time: "Maybe the issue isn't a lack of execution, but a lack of clear motivation." I’m only now realizing that my "lack of execution" was because I was doing something I didn't truly want—working for the sake of a promotion, pulling overtime just to prove my worth. But now... I don't know, it feels different.

Still, I’m a bit scared. Can this change really last? Will I end up overworking again out of guilt and eventually sabotage this agreement? And... I’m starting to embrace the idea that "work is just a tool," but is that too cynical?

I’d love to hear your thoughts.

Dr. Wang, I wanted to give you a heads-up about something that happened this week before our next session.

I made a rookie mistake in a data report this morning—I missed an entire data segment. In five years, I’ve never done anything like this. My boss didn't call me out in public; he just sent a polite email asking me to double-check it. But I know how unprofessional it looks.

The thing is, I know exactly why it happened. For the past two weeks, my mind has been completely consumed by my father’s herniated disc. The doctor’s advice, the physical therapy schedule, his medication reminders... I’ve been replaying these things in my head constantly. I’m thinking about him while I’m working, while I’m in meetings, and even while I was writing that report.

I tried to quantify the problem with data—calculating the percentage drop in my productivity to see if I could find some kind of equilibrium. But then I gave up. I realized this isn't a data problem.

I’m a bit confused. Is this mistake just because I’m distracted, or is it that... my priorities have subconsciously shifted? I’ve always viewed my work as the foundation of a stable life, but now that my father needs me, that "stability" suddenly feels less important.

I’m not saying I want to quit. I just... don't know how to handle this feeling of being torn in two. I need to take care of my father, but I also don't want to keep slipping up at work. Should I talk to my boss about flexible working arrangements? Or should I focus on adjusting my mindset first?

I’d like to focus our discussion on this this week. Thank you.

April 10, 2022, 8:30 PM

The tempura is burnt again.

This time, I didn’t bother adjusting the angles, fixing the lighting, or finding the "best side" to photograph like I did with the tonkatsu last time. I just took the picture as it was—charred bits and all—and sent it to Li Xiao. She replied with a "thank you" emoji and said, "Thanks, Xiaoyu."

Looking at that message, I felt a strange, inexplicable flicker of happiness. Then, the unease set in.

Why did I go out of my way to leave a note saying, "Li Xiao, yours is in the freezer"? What am I doing? It’s as if I was terrified she’d take the wrong one, or... as if I was trying to signal to her, "This was made specifically for you." When did I become like this?

Sitting on the sofa, I dug out my old diary. Those words I wrote three weeks ago—"This time is truly different," "I’m going to prove to myself that I have follow-through." Reading them now feels like looking at something written by a complete stranger. Back then, I was genuinely excited; I truly felt like I was changing.

And now?

Now I’m wondering if this three-month plan... was it ever about proving I could change, or was it about making sure others *saw* me changing?

The tempura was a failure, but I wasn’t angry. I didn’t obsessively check the recipe or overthink where I went wrong like I used to. I just... accepted it. I even felt a bit of relief. But at the same time, there’s this sense of emptiness. Because I know Li Xiao will eat that burnt tempura, and she’ll still say thank you.

I’m becoming dependent on that gratitude.

That realization scares me a little.

I bought all those books and signed up for all those courses, telling myself it was for learning, for "growth." But... was it actually just so people would think, "Look, Lin Xiaoyu is working so hard to change"? When I write those detailed notes on Douban and take all those photos, is it because I really want to track my progress, or do I just want whoever sees them to think I’m serious?

Ever since I was a kid, Mom taught me to be careful, to be cautious, to stay out of trouble. I learned. I did it well. But it seems I also learned something else—that I have to achieve things to earn validation, to prove that I am worthy.

Zhang Hao says I’m not extroverted enough, not social enough. I thought the problem was my personality. Now I’m thinking maybe that wasn’t the problem at all. Maybe the problem is—I care too much about how others see me.

The tempura burnt, and I didn’t remake it. Is that a sign? Is my perfectionism finally loosening its grip? Or am I just tired?

I don’t know.

My head is a mess right now. And I have work tomorrow.

September 28, 2023  Sunny

I just stepped out of Dr. Wang’s office. Sitting here in the cafe, my fingers are still trembling slightly.

She asked me, "If there were no societal expectations, what would your ideal professional life actually look like?"

I froze for a moment. It seemed like such a simple question, yet I had never asked it of myself. The first things that flashed through my mind were—promotions, management roles, being recognized... but those weren't my voice. That was my mother’s voice, the voice of my former classmates on WeChat, the "shoulds" I had fabricated for myself.

Then I heard myself say, "I want exactly what I have now."

The moment the words left my mouth, I startled myself. But immediately after, a sense of relief unlike anything I’ve ever felt washed over me.

I don’t need a promotion. I really don’t.

I do my current job well. I can complete tasks independently, and my analyses are trusted. For me, that is enough. It’s not that I wasn't promoted because of a lack of ability; it’s just that—I never wanted that position. I’ve been feeling guilty for not wanting what I "should" want, without ever asking myself what I actually want.

Dr. Wang followed up: "Then what is it that you truly want?"

I thought about it for a long time. Then I said, "I want to be able to be myself, and to be understood."

As I said it, my eyes grew hot.

For so many years, I’ve been changing myself to fit others' expectations—trying to be more extroverted, more proactive, more "respectable." Zhang Hao wanted me to change. My mother taught me from childhood to be cautious, to be careful. And I kept asking myself: Am I too introverted? Am I not working hard enough? Is there something wrong with my life?

But now I think, maybe nothing is wrong. Maybe I was just looking for answers in the wrong places.

I don’t need to become someone else. I need to find people who truly understand me and a lifestyle that fits me. Sister Li doesn’t need me to change. The people in my yoga group don’t need me to change. Dr. Wang certainly doesn’t. They accept me exactly as I am—quiet, introverted, and thoughtful.

Sitting here in the cafe, my coffee has gone cold, but I feel an unprecedented sense of peace.

Not that fake "positive energy" kind of peace, but something real and deep. It’s like a string that has been pulled taut for years has finally been allowed to slacken. I no longer need to apologize for my personality. I no longer need to blame myself for being 35 and unmarried. I no longer need to doubt myself for not getting a promotion.

None of these are problems. This is just my life.

My life.

I thought of the way my father looked at me during our last dinner—that look of pride and approval. He never needed me to change. He just wanted to see me happy.

And right now, I am happy.

# Diary

**June 27, 2023, 9:00 PM**

I just finished the dishes and sat down on the sofa when it suddenly hit me that I should write something. It feels like I’ve had a lot on my mind lately, but I haven't known where to start.

I’ll start with today. Sister Li and I went to the wet market again this afternoon. That makes four times this month. Four times. I’m a bit surprised myself. In the past, I’d plan out a bunch of cooking classes and buy a mountain of ingredients, only for them to end up rotting. Now, Sister Li and I just wander around, buy whatever looks interesting, and come back to see what we can whip up.

Today we bought fresh peas and eggs and made a simple stir-fry. Nothing fancy, but while eating, it occurred to me that we’ve made over a dozen dishes together this month. Over a dozen. I never thought I was capable of sticking with something like this.

I once wrote on Douban that "execution isn't about how long you can persevere; it's about how clear your goals and motivations are." When I wrote that, I felt like I had figured something out. But experiencing it in the flesh now feels different.

I used to blame myself constantly. I thought I lacked execution—I couldn't stick to the courses I signed up for, I never finished the books I bought, and my plans always fell through halfway. I treated this as a flaw, like it was an inherent weakness. But this month... I’ve realized that maybe that’s not the case.

When I stopped forcing myself to be "perfect" or "persistent," I actually got more done. Going to the market with Sister Li involves no pressure; we just chat casually, look at the produce, and think about what to eat. This casualness is exactly what allows me to go back again and again.

My tendency to overthink isn't actually a flaw. I’m always thinking, analyzing, and reflecting—that’s not procrastinating; it’s optimizing. I can be a thinker and a doer at the same time. The two aren't mutually exclusive.

It sounds a bit strange, but this small realization seems to have eased my work anxiety too. Going five years without a promotion used to be a thought that kept me up all night. Now... I’m wondering, maybe a promotion isn't what I actually need? Maybe the "success" I’ve been chasing isn't even what I want.

My mom taught me from a young age to be cautious and stay out of trouble. I internalized that into a need to be "perfect, persistent, and high-achieving." But maybe... maybe I can allow myself to just live casually? To allow myself to feel fulfilled through cooking, rather than needing a promotion to prove my worth?

I’ve already invited Sister Li and a few people from the yoga group for a cooking get-together next week. In the past, I would have been so nervous, double-checking the menu and worrying the food wouldn't be good enough. Now, I just think: I like cooking, and I want to share that joy with everyone. It’s that simple.

I’m a bit tired, but it’s a satisfied kind of tired.

More tomorrow.

📸 [A photo of an exquisitely plated Japanese meal—freshly made sushi rolls, miso soup, and several side dishes on white porcelain plates, bathed in soft light]

I’ve been obsessed with Japanese cuisine lately, and today I finally got the chance to cook for my parents.

I was so nervous at first, worrying about the heat being off or the plating looking messy. But as I kept adjusting things in the kitchen, it suddenly hit me—people can truly see the heart you put into something. It’s not about how perfect the dish is; it’s about feeling that you care.

My father took a bite of the sushi and asked when I had learned to make it. My mother said, "This soup tastes lovely." They didn't say much, but in that moment, I felt like I heard everything.

Maybe I’ve had it wrong all along. Their silence isn't indifference; it's just their way of expressing love. And all the effort I put in—they see it.

Time to keep learning new recipes. I think I'll try tempura next time.

*Recorded on the afternoon of Jan 15, 2023, at home*

Dr. Wang, I wanted to share a small thing that happened today.

Just now during lunch, I opened my notebook at my desk to write in my journal. I never used to do this—I always felt it was inappropriate to write such private things at work, or rather, I didn't want others to see me "wasting time thinking." But today, I just sat there and wrote for about twenty minutes. Even when a colleague walked by, I just looked up and smiled instead of hurriedly closing the notebook.

This detail might sound minor, but it means a lot to me.

I was writing about my father. Remember when we talked about that phrase, "How has work been lately?" I’ve been reflecting on it all month. Today, it suddenly clicked—he has always cared about me; it’s just that my habitual defensiveness prevented me from truly hearing him. I wrote in my journal: "The meaning of life might not lie in reaching some perfect goal, but in the process itself, and the connections I build with the people I love along the way."

As I wrote that, I felt like crying, yet I also felt very peaceful.

I can feel the changes from this past month myself. My anxiety has definitely lessened—not that all my problems are solved, but I’ve started to coexist with them. I no longer agonize over the wording of an email for ages. My sleep has improved, too; I’ve had almost no insomnia lately. I’ve even started initiating deeper conversations with my mother. Although it can still feel a bit stiff at times, the feeling is different now.

I think you’ll understand what I mean—it’s not that everything suddenly became perfect, but that I’ve started to accept the problems, my parents, and myself.

Thank you for your company this past month. I feel like I’m slowly becoming a version of myself that I like more.

That said, I’d like to keep exploring something with you—I still have moments where I feel lonely, especially when I see my former classmates' posts on social media. Is this feeling something that can also be understood and accepted? Or is it something I need to work on changing?

See you next week.

February 7, 2022, Night

I just called Mom. There wasn't really a specific reason; I just wanted to ask how they were doing. But I found myself pausing for a long time before dialing, even rehearsing what to say in my head, terrified of using the wrong words.

Mom asked, "Why the sudden concern?" That hit me hard. I realized I truly never take the initiative to care about them. Even when I go home for dinner once a month, I just sit there passively and retreat to my room as soon as the meal is over. I haven't even asked how Dad’s blood pressure has been lately, or why Mom’s fingers have started to tremble.

I used "making future plans" as an excuse to explain why I was calling. It sounded so stiff. But I really don't know how to express it any other way. Saying "I’m worried about you" directly feels too sappy, too vulnerable. So instead, I bombarded her with questions about physical exams, insurance, and medical costs. Mom seemed a bit bewildered by the interrogation and eventually just said, "Everything is fine, don't worry."

After hanging up, I opened my laptop and created a document: "Parents' Health Records." I logged Dad’s blood pressure (140/90, a bit high), Mom’s recent check-up results, and the medications they are each taking. I marked the items needing urgent attention with red exclamation points.

Doing this gave me a strange sense of relief. It’s as if by organizing all this information and listing every possible risk, I can somehow maintain control. But I know this is self-deception. Data doesn't change anything. Neither does my anxiety.

I have to do something. This thought keeps circling in my head. But what exactly is "doing something"? Calling more often? Saving more money? Studying the legal responsibilities of an only child supporting their parents? Or is it just... admitting that I’m truly terrified of them growing old, and terrified that I won't be able to carry all of this alone?

It’s 11 PM now, and I’m still sitting at the computer. The document is still open, and I’m repeatedly adjusting the font size. I have work tomorrow. But I know I won't be able to sleep.

I don't know why I'm like this.

Dr. Wang, I was wondering if we could talk about something during our next session?

I was going through some of my old journals today and had a bit of an unexpected breakthrough. I realized that my understanding of that past relationship had been stuck in a certain place for a long time, but today, everything suddenly... clicked.

I wrote this down: "I understand now that it wasn't my fault. I don't need to change who I am to fit anyone else. I am an introvert, and that is not a flaw."

To be honest, I had mixed feelings when I saw what I’d written. It wasn't painful; instead, it felt like a sense of relief. It’s as if for the past three years I’ve been constantly asking myself, "Was I not good enough?" when that wasn't even the issue at all.

I’ve been thinking lately that I might have been way too hard on myself with many of my self-evaluations. Like you said before, accepting yourself isn't about giving up on change—it's about understanding yourself first. I think I’m finally starting to truly understand what that means.

I still have a lot of things I haven't quite figured out yet, so I’d like to dive deeper into this topic during our session. Do you have any time next week?

# August 9, 2023, Night

Something strange happened today while I was cooking with my father.

As I was chopping scallions, he suddenly started talking about his time in a student theater troupe when he was young. I stopped what I was doing and turned to look at him. He just stood there, handling the fish on the cutting board, recounting stories I had never heard before in a very calm voice—the stage lights, the laughter during rehearsals, and that feeling of being seen.

I didn't know what to say. This man, the father I’ve known my whole life as silent and introverted, had actually stood on a stage once.

We kept cooking. He didn't say much, but those fragments were enough for me to see a version of him I never knew existed. I wondered, when did he stop telling these stories? When did he become the person he is now—the one where every time I go home, it’s just dinner, TV, and then retreating to our separate rooms?

Suddenly, it hit me. I’ve always blamed myself for a lack of drive, for being too cautious, but these aren't just my problems—they are things I inherited. My mother taught me from a young age "not to cause trouble," and my father modeled what a "safe life" looks like through his silence. I chose a stable job and a stable path not because I truly lacked other possibilities, but because I was taught to believe that stability is the only form of safety.

But watching the spark in his eyes as he told those theater stories, I wondered—if he hadn't given those things up back then, would he be different now? Or perhaps, he never truly gave them up; he just hid them away in his heart.

We didn't discuss any of this over dinner. We just made a meal together, creating a meaningful moment in the kitchen. It sounds simple, but I realized that this is what real connection is—not just because we share the same blood, but because we did something together.

I’m sitting in my room now, and I can still smell the fish and scallions from the kitchen.

I think maybe I should ask him: what happened to those theater stories later on? Maybe he still wants to tell them.

And maybe I should start telling some stories of my own that I’ve been keeping inside.

[Image: Yoga mats neatly lined up on the grass, with trees and morning light in the distance. The camera angle captures a water bottle and a towel on one of the mats.]

The park on a weekend is even more refreshing than I imagined.

I spent ages at home debating whether to head out, but I finally made it. Now, I’m so glad I did. The air is crisp, everyone is so relaxed, and there’s zero feeling of being judged. Sister Li said she’s coming back next week, and I think I want to, too.

Keeping up with the simple things doesn't seem that hard after all.

*Written by Xiaoyu at 21:30*

Saturday Afternoon, August 5, 2023

I can’t stop thinking about what Sister Li said.

"That’s exactly what makes you different from everyone else."

She said it while looking through my notebook—the one filled with the history of pasta, sauce ratios, and precise cooking times. I felt a bit embarrassed at the time, thinking I was being too obsessive, but her tone wasn't mocking at all. She was serious.

I used to think of this as a flaw. Always replaying things in my head, always taking notes, always overthinking. Zhang Hao said I think too much; my mom said I make things too complicated. So I always viewed it as a burden—something that made me seem indecisive, not enough of a "doer."

But lately, I’ve started to feel that maybe it’s not a flaw at all.

After the last cooking gathering, I wrote a long entry on Douban. It wasn’t just a recipe; it was a reflection on the whole process—why I chose that specific sauce, how I adjusted the heat, how my friends reacted. I hadn't even planned on posting it; I just wanted to record it. But then I thought, maybe these reflections have value in themselves. Maybe I should share them.

Looking back, what has happened over these past two months?

In June, I was still drowning in anxiety. Why haven't I been promoted in five years? Why are all my old classmates married with families? Why am I still stuck in the same place? Those questions are still there, but they don't feel as hopeless anymore. Or rather, I’ve started looking at them differently.

The promotion. I’m honestly not sure if I even want it anymore. It sounds strange because I always felt like it was something I *should* want. But through the small act of cooking, I’ve seen my own capabilities in other areas. I can create an environment where people feel welcome; I can turn an idea into reality; I can see the fine details as well as the big picture. These skills exist in my work too, but maybe I’ve been applying them to the wrong things. Maybe what I need isn't a promotion, but a way to make my work more meaningful.

And then there’s the issue of "execution." I used to beat myself up for not sticking with those online courses or finishing those books. But now I understand—I have no motivation when I’m just cooking for myself, but when I’m cooking for friends, I see it through to the end and even strive for perfection. The problem isn't a lack of discipline; it’s that I was doing things without a "why."

Socializing, too. I always thought I wasn't cut out for it. But Sister Li and the friends from the yoga group have shown me that I am actually accepted. I don’t need to be an extreme extrovert. I just need to be my authentic self and invite others to join in on the things I love.

So, what now?

I don’t know. But it feels different. It’s not that fake "toxic positivity," but a genuine sense of fulfillment. I’m doing something meaningful. I am being seen.

Next month, I’ll continue with the Japanese home-cooking theme. Sister Li asked if we could get together again. I said yes.

When I said "yes," it didn't feel forced at all.

---

By the way, I’ve noticed I’m acting a bit differently at work lately, too. I actually spoke up and shared an idea during the last meeting. I never would have done that before.

Maybe this is the beginning.

# Monday, September 5, 2022, 11:47 PM

Here we go again.

At 9:00 AM, my boss sent a message saying there was a bug in the system that needed immediate attention. My first reaction upon seeing that message was "here we go again," and then, without a second thought, I opened my code editor. I skipped the scheduled team morning meeting; it wasn't anything important anyway.

I spent the entire morning tracing that logic error. Around 3:00 PM, my neck started to ache. I gave it a couple of rolls and kept working. A colleague came by to ask if I wanted to grab coffee; I just shook my head and put on my headphones. She didn't say anything else and left.

It’s almost midnight now. The bug is finally fixed. Another weekend gone.

I wonder, why is it always like this? Why am I always so quick to say, "Okay, I'll handle it"? Why have I never said "no"?

No, that’s not right. I have said "no." But that was to invitations from friends. When it comes to work, I never seem to be able to say it.

I’m thinking about those words: "here we go again." What’s packed into those four words? Habit? Helplessness? Or... do I actually have no choice at all?

No, it’s not that I don’t have the right to choose. It’s that I don’t have the courage.

I looked at myself in the mirror. Deep dark circles under my eyes. My neck hurts. My lower back hurts too. But none of that matters, because I still have bugs to fix, reports to generate, and... what else?

I remembered those Japanese cookbooks. They’re still on the shelf, the bookmark stuck at Chapter 3. I once told a colleague at the office, "I’m going to try making Teriyaki chicken this week." It’s September now. I haven’t made a thing.

It’s the same every time. I buy books, sign up for classes, make plans, get my hopes up, and then... then there is no "then." Overtime, exhaustion, disappearing weekends, abandoned plans. It’s a cycle, and I’m trapped in it.

The scariest part is that I’m starting to believe this is just who I am. I am this person—someone who can’t follow through, who can’t change, who is swallowed whole by work. This isn’t just a phase; this is my essence.

I wonder, if I can’t even stick to a cooking plan, how could I ever have a breakthrough at work? How could I ever find someone who truly understands me? How could I ever make my life any different?

Maybe I should just accept this reality. Thirty-five years old, alone, career stalled, health declining, every weekend consumed by overtime. This is my life.

I should go to sleep. I have work tomorrow.

A new day, and there will be new bugs.

Here we go again.

# February 10, 2022, 5:20 PM

When I walked out of the insurance company building, I lingered downstairs for a long time. I didn't call a cab right away; instead, I just stood there taking deep breaths, clutching that thick proposal tightly in my hand.

I don’t know why I’m doing this. Or rather, I do know, but admitting it scares me.

When I walked in at two this afternoon, I was well-prepared—I had listed my parents' ages, health conditions, income, and existing coverage in a spreadsheet. The consultant said it looked very professional, but what I heard was: *Look, you’ve reached the point where you have no choice but to do this.*

He walked me through long-term care insurance, medical insurance, and accident coverage. I took notes diligently, asking about premiums, claims processes, and waiting periods. My voice remained steady throughout, as if I were analyzing a work report. But when he handed me the proposal, my fingers trembled. I saw it. He probably saw it, too.

The annual premium is over 8,000. And that’s just the basic plan. If my father needs long-term care in the future, the monthly cost could be 5,000 to 8,000. I did the math in my spreadsheet—if he lives to 85, the total amount would be... I don’t want to calculate it again.

What scares me most isn't the numbers themselves, but what they represent. They mean I can no longer pretend this is some distant future. They mean my salary, my savings, and my entire life plan must be restructured around this reality.

When I went home last week, I noticed my father’s pace had slowed down. He said it was just the cold weather making his joints a bit stiff. But watching the momentary struggle as he pushed himself up from the sofa, I started calculating—if he really loses his mobility one day, what will I do?

My mother’s hands have been shaking slightly lately. She says it’s just from working too long, that it’s no big deal. But I saw that subtle tremor when she held her chopsticks. I thought of the articles I’ve read online—about Parkinson’s, cervical spondylosis, and various geriatric diseases. I didn’t ask her about it, because asking would mean admitting my fears, and once I admit them, I can no longer pretend that everything is okay.

I’ve been running away. I’m only realizing that now. I’ve filled my life with work, reading, researching Japanese cuisine, and weekly yoga classes—all to leave myself no time to think about this. But you can’t outrun it. The numbers catch up. Age catches up.

Sitting across from the consultant, I felt a sudden wave of guilt. Why didn’t I start preparing sooner? Why do I always wait until things become urgent to act? I am a responsible person; my boss trusts me, and my colleagues rely on my analytical skills. So why, when it comes to the most important things, am I always so passive?

The consultant said, "It’s not too late to start now." I nodded, but I knew that wasn't entirely true. Or rather, it’s a half-truth. It’s never too late to start, but time is indeed slipping away. Every single day is slipping away.

As I left the building, I took out my phone and messaged Dr. Wang, asking if she had any consultation slots next week. I need to talk to her about this. Or perhaps, I just need someone to listen, even if I don’t yet know what to say.

Now, sitting at home, that proposal is lying on my desk. I haven't opened it yet. I know I eventually will; I’ll study every clause and draft a detailed financial plan. That’s my way—using logic and data to combat fear.

But for now, I just want to sit for a while.

Sitting alone.

Thinking of nothing.

During the day, I’m just in a daze—staring at the computer screen, but nothing is sinking in. Those books are still just sitting on the table, and I haven't touched the Japanese cookbooks again. I don't even want to go to yoga anymore. Sister Li asked me again the day before yesterday if I was going this week, and I just made up an excuse to say no.

I feel like I’m breaking down. Not all at once, but bit by bit... I can’t do anything right. I can’t do my job, I can’t stick to my plans, and I can’t even manage to hide how I’m really doing. Sometimes I think maybe this is just who I am—someone who can’t change.

I don't know where this is heading. I was wondering if I should find a time to come see you again? I feel like I need help, but I don't even know where to start.

Sorry, this message is probably a bit of a mess. I just wanted to let you know how things are right now.

# June 28, 2023  Sunny

I’m sitting in the office this afternoon. Outside, the weather is sweltering, while the air conditioning inside is blowing a bit cold. But my heart feels warm.

Something happened when I went home yesterday. I’ve been thinking about it ever since, and only now do I have the courage to write it down.

Dad was sitting on the sofa, recovering well. The doctor said he’ll be mostly back to normal in another two weeks. He looked at me and suddenly said, "Xiaoyu, I see how much you’ve done for us." I was a bit stunned, not knowing how to respond. Then he added, "You’re taking care of yourself while taking care of us. That’s good."

His eyes were a little moist. I had never seen Dad like that before.

I replied, "Taking care of you is my responsibility, and it’s also my choice." As I said those words, I realized they were true. I don’t know when it happened, but I no longer feel like this is a forced burden.

For a long time, it felt like I was waiting for something—waiting to be recognized, waiting for someone to tell me that my choices were right. Why didn't I get that promotion? Because I put more time and energy into my family. Why am I not dating? Because I don't want to change myself just to fit someone else's life. These choices used to make me feel guilty, as if I were wasting something, as if I weren't trying hard enough.

But what Dad said yesterday felt like a kind of permission. He saw it. He understood. He wasn't judging me; he was affirming me.

Mom didn't say anything either, but I saw her nodding beside him.

Sitting here in the office, I feel like crying. Not out of sadness, but from a deep sense of release. It’s as if I’ve been carrying something heavy for a long time, and suddenly, someone helped me hold it for a while.

I keep wondering why such a simple acknowledgment can change so much. Maybe it’s because, growing up, no one ever truly told me—your choices can be different; you don’t have to live according to everyone else’s expectations.

Now I know.

Sister Li messaged me tonight asking if I wanted to go to yoga this weekend. I said yes.

It feels like many things are slowly getting better lately. Not because of any major external change, but because I’ve started to accept myself.

And that is enough.

Sometimes I realize that the smallest "thank you" can mean more than the grandest achievements.

Lately, I've been organizing my father's pillbox—filling each slot, marking the times, making sure everything is ready for each day. When he said "thank you," it hit me what I was actually doing. It wasn't just a task to check off; it was companionship.

No matter how busy work gets, this moment made me stop and think for a long time. Maybe I’ve been chasing the wrong things all along.

April 16, 2024, Evening

Something Sister Li said today has been looping in my head.

After yoga class this afternoon, she looked at a few photos on my phone—the ones I took at the park this weekend. I felt a bit self-conscious at first; after all, I’ve only been at this for a few days, so my technique is bound to be poor. But she looked at them quite intently and asked if I’d ever thought about organizing a small outing—just a few people going out to take photos together.

My gut reaction was to flat-out refuse. "There’s no way I could organize an event... I’m just doing this for fun." The words came out fast, like a reflex. I could hear that familiar sense of shrinking back in my own voice.

But Sister Li didn't give up. She said, "I'll help you."

Just those four words.

I sat there, suddenly a bit speechless. It wasn't just because I was moved—though I certainly was—but because I realized that, in that moment, two voices were fighting inside me. One said, "You can't do this, it'll be awkward," while the other said, "Maybe... maybe I could try."

In the end, I said, "Let me think about it... maybe I could give it a try."

Sitting at home now, I keep asking myself: Why was I able to say that? Why didn't I just say no?

Maybe it’s because lately—buying the camera, taking photos, keeping notes—I feel like I’ve actually accomplished something. It’s not about how good the photos are; it’s that I’ve proven to myself I can move from "wanting to do it" to "actually doing it." Things I used to say I couldn't stick with, I’m actually sticking with this time.

So when Sister Li said "I'll help you," I wasn't as afraid. It’s not that the fear is gone, it’s just... having someone by your side makes it feel different.

But now the nerves are kicking in again. How do I organize an event? Where should we go? When? Will it be awkward? Will I mess it up?

I’m thinking that maybe this is what growth feels like. It’s not that the fear disappears, but that you can find a little bit of courage even while you're afraid.

I’ll think about the details tomorrow. My brain is a bit tired right now.

But... it looks like I’m actually going to do this.

# September 9, 2022, 10:00 PM

Another sleepless night.

I got an email about a new project this afternoon, and I ended up opening a recruitment site. I don’t know why; I just felt like looking. I scrolled through a few job descriptions—Data Analyst, Risk Assessment... they’re all the same. I even opened my draft resume. Then I closed it.

I wouldn't have sent it out anyway.

I know what I’m thinking. I know exactly what would happen if I actually applied—I’d stammer through the interview, unable to articulate my strengths, my mind going completely blank when asked, "Why do you want this position?" Forget it. Let it be. It’s been five years; changing jobs now won’t change anything. This is just who I am.

My neck is acting up again. I’ve been at the computer all day, and the pain was particularly sharp this afternoon. I used a hot water bottle for a bit and then kept working. It’s no big deal. Compared to everything else, it’s nothing.

I saw a promotion announcement from a college classmate on WeChat. Another one. She said the new role is challenging and the team is great. I gave it a "like" but didn't reply. I didn't know what to say. The word "congratulations" feels so hollow coming from me now.

I’m 35. My classmates are all getting promoted; some have kids now. And what am I doing? Sitting at home staring at those three Japanese cookbooks and that unfinished online course. I was so sure this time would be different. I even told my colleagues at the office that I was going to cook one new dish every week. Now those books are just gathering dust in the corner, looking at me like they’re mocking me.

I just can’t stick to anything. If I can't even manage a simple cooking plan, why am I even thinking about career development? Why am I thinking about change? It’s not the environment, and it’s not a lack of time. It’s me. I’m the problem.

Mom asked me last time if I was working too hard. I didn't give her a straight answer. How could I tell her that I’m actually falling apart? She and Dad are retired now, and everything has become my responsibility. I have to prepare for their medical bills and living expenses. I have to be the reliable only child. But I can’t even manage my own life.

Zhang Hao once said I needed to change. He said I’m too introverted, too unwilling to take the initiative. I was angry at the time, but now... he might be right. This is just who I am. Incapable of change.

More overtime tomorrow. The weekend bug fixes aren't finished yet.

I’m tired.

So incredibly tired.

# Checklist for Parents

**Updated: 2022.02.01, 2:30 AM**

---

## Health Monitoring
- [ ] Dad: Book an EKG (the last physical mentioned an irregular heartbeat; need to confirm).
- [ ] Dad: Ask if he’s had chest tightness or dizziness lately—he says no, but I’m not sure if he’s hiding something.
- [ ] Mom: Is her vision getting blurry? I’ve noticed her squinting at her phone more often lately.
- [ ] Mom: Slight hand tremors—could just be aging, but do I need to rule out Parkinson’s? Observe more closely next time I’m home.
- [ ] Blood pressure and glucose levels for both—need to buy a home monitor for them.

## Medical Cost Estimates
- [ ] Check local health insurance reimbursement rates (is retiree coverage the same as when they were working?).
- [ ] Long-term care insurance: How long is the waiting period? What are the premiums?
- [ ] Average treatment costs for common age-related conditions:
  - Long-term hypertension meds: ?
  - Diabetes management: ?
  - Osteoporosis: ?
  - Inpatient nursing care (if needed): ?
- [ ] Emergency medical fund budget—how much savings is actually enough?

## Insurance & Legal Documents
- [ ] Look into commercial health insurance for them (are they still eligible? Will age hike the premiums?).
- [ ] Compare long-term care insurance (look at at least 3 providers).
- [ ] Wills and Power of Attorney—how do I even bring this up? It feels so heart-wrenching.
- [ ] Medical Power of Attorney (who has decision-making authority if they can’t decide for themselves?).
- [ ] Research legal obligations for an only child—am I legally required to provide support?

## Living Arrangements
- [ ] Is their current home suitable for aging in place? Are there too many stairs?
- [ ] Consider renovations: non-slip bathroom floors, hallway grab bars, better lighting.
- [ ] Cost of regular housekeeping services? Or should I handle it myself?
- [ ] List of their daily medications—need to organize this so I can tell a doctor quickly in an emergency.

## Financial Planning
- [ ] How much of their expenses can my current monthly salary cover?
- [ ] What would the monthly cost be if they need long-term care?
- [ ] How much emergency savings do I need? (3 months? 6 months?)
- [ ] Are their pensions enough for daily expenses, or do I need to subsidize them?
- [ ] Mortgage, property taxes, HOA fees—how will these be handled after they retire?

## Miscellaneous
- [ ] Next visit home—should I visit more often? Is once or twice a month too little?
- [ ] Are there other health issues they’re hiding? How do I ask without making them feel like I’m spying?
- [ ] What if I get sick? Can I still take care of them?

---

*This list is never-ending. Every time I think of a new possibility, I have to add a line. Sometimes I wonder if it’s even possible to plan for everything. Maybe I’ll miss something vital.*

Evidence that this is the kind of person I am.

I’m even thinking that maybe I should stop trying altogether. Stop buying books, stop signing up for courses, stop having any expectations for myself. That way, I won’t be disappointed anymore.

My mom always said since I was a kid to be cautious and stay out of trouble. Maybe she was right. Maybe I should just accept my mediocrity—go to work, come home, repeat. Stop struggling, stop hoping.

Zhang Hao said I’m incapable of change. Maybe he was right, too.

It’s past 2:00 AM. I’m sitting here in the dark, looking at my blank Notes app, feeling an eerie sense of relief.

Let it be.

No more changing.

Dr. Wang, I’d like to schedule a consultation for next week. There’s something I’ve been wanting to talk to you about.

I actually called my mom this afternoon. I originally intended to ask about their health so I could better handle their insurance and financial planning... but then she asked me, "Why the sudden concern?" and I just didn't know how to answer.

I realized that I probably haven't been paying enough attention to them lately. But what hurts more is that I don't know if I’m genuinely caring for them or if I’m just scared. Scared that something will go wrong, and scared that I won't be able to handle the burden. So, I used "planning" as this very businesslike excuse, and as a result, the conversation became very stiff.

My mom might have sensed something; she didn't say much after that, and we just chatted about daily life. When I hung up, I felt so guilty—it felt like I was using the guise of "filial piety" to manage my own anxiety, rather than truly caring for them.

Lately, I’ve been looking at all kinds of insurance products and long-term care information; my head is just full of numbers and costs. But the more I learn, the more frightened I get. Can I really handle all of this? If the day comes when they need me, will I be strong enough?

I think I need your help to sort through my thoughts. This isn't just about "planning," is it?

# Phone Memo

**Tags: #Family #Realization #Father**

---

When Dad asked me, "How’s work going lately?" it suddenly hit me—I’ve been judging them all this time, instead of trying to understand them.

Silence isn't indifference. Caution isn't control.

His question was simple, but it took me all these years to truly hear it. Maybe he’s been asking all along; I just wasn't listening.

---

When Mom talked about wanting more choices when she was younger, her gaze drifted off. It never occurred to me that she has regrets about her own life, too. I always saw her "over-cautiousness" as a leash, but in reality... she was just protecting me the only way she knew how.

She doesn't regret raising me, but she has her own "roads not taken."

Thinking about it this way, my resentment toward her just... dissolved.

---

I don't need to change them.
And I don't need to feel guilty for being their daughter.

What I want now is to just *be* with them, in a more authentic way.

---

**To be continued: Why I used to avoid these conversations**

March 11, 2022, past 11:00 PM

Just finished my shower and I’m sitting on the bed, but I still feel like I need to write today down.

This afternoon, a colleague asked me what was in my bento box, and I… actually stood up. It sounds like such a small thing, but I know what it means to me. Usually, I just sit there and give a nod at most, but today I actually stood up and explained the ingredients one by one in a clear voice—what sushi vinegar is, why you have to use Japanese rice, how to pick the right pickled radish. My colleague listened so intently and even said, "Wow, you’re really taking this seriously."

In that moment, I felt seen. Truly.

I’m wondering, is this what change feels like? Not the dramatic, overnight transformation into a different person, but... being able to speak clearly in the office, taking the initiative to share what I’m doing, and letting others see that I’m trying. Isn’t this exactly what I’ve always wanted?

But now, lying in bed, I’ve started overthinking again. Am I really changing, or did I only express myself because I was given a reason to? If no one asks me anything tomorrow, will I shrink back into my shell? And that three-month plan I set for myself—one new dish every week—can I actually stick to it?

I know my own flaws. I get excited about new things easily, and then... nothing happens. What about those courses I bought last time? The books? I never finished any of them. So why would this time be any different?

And yet... that feeling today was real. My colleague’s validation was real. Those notes I’ve been writing on Douban—every single one is something I actually did. None of it is fake.

I think I need to stop asking myself "Can I stick with this?" and start asking "What do I want to do right now?" Right now, I want to keep cooking. Tomorrow, I’ll probably feel the same way. As for three months from now... let’s not think that far ahead yet.

I’ve already made the grocery list for tomorrow. I’m going to try making tempura this weekend.

I hope I don’t give up halfway again.

But even if I do, this moment today really happened. And maybe that’s enough.

December 15, 2023, 10:00 PM

While we were on our yoga mats today, Sister Li told me I’ve changed lately. She said my poses are steadier and my breathing is deeper. I just smiled, but I knew she wasn’t just talking about yoga.

Through my recent conversations with Dr. Wang, I feel like I’ve finally realized something. I used to beat myself up for a lack of "execution"—signing up for so many classes, buying so many books, only to let them all fall by the wayside. I saw it as a personal failure. But now, I see it differently.

When I’m making Japanese food with Sister Li, I can stick with it. When I’m with my yoga group, I show up consistently. It’s not because these things are easier; it’s because I have company and a fixed structure in place. What I need isn't more willpower, but the right system. This realization has taken a huge weight off my shoulders.

I’ve made a list for myself—not grand goals, but things that are realistic and actionable:

Keep up with yoga every week with the group. I’m already doing this.

Sit down with my parents once a month for a real talk about their retirement and our care arrangements. Not just small talk over dinner, but a real conversation.

Try to voice a thought or two at work. Not for a promotion, but because I now believe my ideas have value.

Maybe keep cooking with Sister Li. Having someone to share it with makes it feel like more than just a task.

I used to think I was someone who just wasn't cut out for change. Now I understand that I just need to find the right way to go about it. I’m not "broken"; I’m just someone who needs support and structure. It might sound like I’m making excuses, but I’m not. I’m seeing myself for who I truly am.

The things about work, the promotion, being 35 and still unmarried... they’re all still there, but they don't feel quite as heavy anymore. I’m starting to believe that life isn’t a race you have to win. My stable job gives me the time to be with my parents, to do yoga, and to cook with friends. And that’s perfectly okay.

As I head to bed, I feel a sense of peace. Not the peace of running away, but the peace of understanding and accepting myself.

Tomorrow, I keep going.

Sister Li, are you there?

I’ve been thinking about this for days before finally working up the courage to send you this message. It’s a bit embarrassing, but I still wanted to tell you... I went to counseling.

To be honest, I was so nervous before going. I kept wondering if I was just making a big deal out of nothing. But... maybe I wasn’t. I said a lot of things there that I’ve never told anyone. Some things I hadn't even figured out myself, but once I said them out loud, it felt different.

June 15, 2023, 8:30 PM

Something small happened today, but I feel like I need to write it down.

This afternoon in the WeChat group, Sister Li asked everyone for advice on meditation. When I saw the question, my first instinct was to just scroll past. That’s what I used to do—see a topic, feel like I had nothing special to contribute, and stay silent.

But this time was different. I thought about my experiences in yoga class these past few weeks and how meditation has changed me. I paused for about five minutes, my fingers typing, deleting, and retyping on the screen. I was worried that I didn’t sound professional enough, or that others would find my advice too personal and subjective.

And then... I just sent it.

I wrote: "For me, yoga isn't just physical exercise; it's a form of mental healing. During meditation, I’ve learned not to judge my thoughts, but simply to observe them. It’s a very gentle process."

My heart was racing a bit when I hit send.

But the reaction that followed surprised me. It wasn't that kind of hollow "Wow, well said" praise; people were actually responding thoughtfully. Someone asked for specifics on how to do it, and someone else said they wanted to try it too. Sister Li even replied specifically, saying, "Xiaoyu is absolutely right. This is exactly why I invited everyone to the yoga group."

Sitting at my desk, looking at those messages, my eyes felt a little stung with tears.

It’s not like how it was with Zhang Hao. Back then, he always wanted me to be someone else—more talkative, more proactive, more... extroverted. As if who I was naturally wasn't good enough. I tried to change, but that kind of change was exhausting, and in the end, it failed anyway.

But now it’s different. No one is asking me to be anyone else. Sister Li invited me to the yoga group not because I needed to change into an extrovert, but... simply to accept me as I am. I shared my thoughts in the group not because I had something to prove, but because I actually had something to say.

Maybe the problem was never how introverted I am, but whether I had found the right environment.

I’m sitting at home now, drinking tea and looking at the night view outside. I have work tomorrow, with a pile of emails and data to deal with. But tonight, I’m allowing myself to feel a little bit of pride.

I did something that used to feel so hard. And it wasn't that hard after all.

## Morning Work Log

### Team Meeting - Discussion on Risk Assessment Model Optimization

10:00-11:30 Attended this quarter's risk model review meeting.

During the morning meeting, I took the initiative to propose several directions for improving our current risk assessment model. I came well-prepared for this one—yesterday afternoon, I re-examined the data anomaly records from the past three months and identified a distinct pattern: the weight assigned to long-tail data might be too low, causing certain low-frequency but high-risk transaction behaviors to be overlooked.

Specific improvement proposals:
- Adjust the weight coefficient for long-tail data, increasing it from the current 0.3 to between 0.45 and 0.5.
- Add a "transaction frequency mutation" dimension to the feature engineering stage.
- Suggested implementing stratified processing for high-risk customer segments rather than using a uniform threshold.

My supervisor nodded after listening and said this line of thinking is worth exploring in depth. Colleagues also asked a few technical questions. I answered them one by one and felt that my explanations were quite clear.

After the meeting, my supervisor pulled me aside and said it was a good idea, asking me to produce a detailed technical proposal and test plan by the end of the week.

## Reflections

To be honest, I rarely expressed my ideas so fully in meetings before. Usually, I’d only speak when asked, or I’d write detailed emails but stay silent during the actual discussion. This time was different—I prepared in advance, spoke up proactively, and presented my thoughts systematically.

I felt much less nervous. Perhaps it’s because I had actually done my homework and knew exactly what I was talking about. Or maybe it’s due to some recent changes—cooking with Sister Li, the yoga group, and my conversations with Dr. Wang—which have given me a bit more self-confidence.

Regardless, I need to get the proposal written this week. I plan to finish the first draft by Thursday.

Saturday, June 8, 2024, 15:30

I’m sitting in the office right now, and my mind is still a bit of a blank.

My boss pulled me aside this morning. He said there’s a new project and he wants me to head up the data modeling side. Usually, when I hear something like that, my first instinct is to just say "okay" and then start silently calculating how much overtime I’ll have to put in. But today... I actually asked him about the working hours. I asked. I really did.

I said, "What’s the timeline for this project? Will it require frequent overtime?"

He looked a bit stunned, then said we could discuss the specifics. I followed up by saying I have some personal commitments lately and want to ensure my weekends stay free. He said that could be coordinated.

Sitting here now, I can hardly believe what I just said.

But what’s even stranger is that when he said, "This is a great opportunity for a promotion," I didn't feel that old, urgent, desperate craving. Instead, I found myself thinking—do I even want a promotion anymore?

It’s been five years. Five years without a promotion. I used to lie awake at night obsessing over it—why them and not me? Am I not working hard enough? Am I really that uncompetitive? That anxiety gnawed at me for a long time.

But now... I don't know. Over the past few months, my life seems to have changed. The photography walks, the yoga group, the chats with Sister Li... what have these things given me that makes me suddenly care so much less about moving up?

I’m thinking that maybe the promotion itself hasn't changed; what’s changed is how much I crave it. Or rather, a promotion used to be the only way I could prove my worth. Now I have other ways. I can organize events, I can connect with people, I can do interesting things. Those things prove who I am, too.

But I’m also a little scared. Am I just escaping? Will I regret this? Am I lying to myself, saying "work isn't that important" just because I can't get promoted anyway?

...

To be honest, though, this feeling is actually quite nice. At least I’m not as exhausted. At least I can set some boundaries for myself instead of being swallowed whole by work.

I don’t know if this is the right decision. But at the very least, the "me" today is different from the "me" yesterday.

I feel a little proud of myself. And a little uneasy.

Whatever, I’ll stop overthinking it. I have yoga later.

March 31, 2023, 11:00 PM

Sitting at my desk, it’s pitch black outside. The office lights are still on, and I can see a few lit windows in the building across the street. I’m tired, but my mind is still racing.

When my boss called me in for a chat today, for a moment, I thought I was in trouble. Instead, he told me that my recent proposal for optimizing the risk model was very insightful. He said that while they can’t implement it immediately, they’re going to keep looking into it. I was stunned for a second.

He said, "Your ideas are valuable."

I wrote that sentence down in my work log. Thinking back on it now, it still feels a bit surreal. In five years, I’ve never had an idea recognized like that. Usually, I just finish a task and wait for the next one. I never thought my own thoughts could actually be *seen*.

The strangest part is that I didn’t feel disappointed—even though he didn't adopt my suggestion right away. Instead, I found myself thinking that maybe this is how change happens. Not all at once, but slowly, bit by bit. That thought brought me a sense of relief.

After yoga this afternoon, I was chatting with Sister Li. She asked if I’d been in a particularly good mood lately. I smiled and said, "A little." She said I seem like a different person. When I asked her how, she said I’ve started initiating conversations instead of always sitting in the corner.

I didn't argue with her. Because I know she’s right.

But then I wonder: is this really a "change"? Or have I always had this in me, and it just never got triggered?

Xiao Mei asked if I could help her look at her financial data, so I spent an hour doing a simple analysis for her. She said my thinking was so clear that it made everything click for her. I noticed that while I was explaining things, my tone was natural and I wasn't nervous at all. It surprised me—realizing I can actually relax when talking about things I’m good at.

When Mom called the other day to ask what I’ve been up to, for the first time ever, I told her about the yoga group and my boss’s feedback. She paused, then said, "That’s good." Just those three words, but I could hear a shift in her voice.

Maybe she’s been waiting for me to open up. Maybe I’ve been waiting to be seen.

Sitting here now, I’m asking myself: if I’ve always had this ability, what have I been so afraid of for the last five years?

It wasn't a fear of failure. It was a fear of being seen. A fear that if I showed what I could do, I’d have to carry the weight of higher expectations. A fear that change meant losing that safe, predictable life.

But... my current life isn't exactly "safe" either. It’s just a different kind of insecurity—the insecurity of being trapped in my own stagnation.

I don’t know what happens next. Maybe my boss really will consider me for a promotion. Maybe he won’t. But this time, it doesn't feel like a twist of fate. It feels like something I’m actually a part of.

I have a choice.

That realization is a little scary, but mostly... exciting?

I’ll think about it tomorrow. Time for sleep.

Sunday, August 28, 2022, Night

That conversation has been playing on a loop in my head all day.

I talked to my boss about my career development this afternoon. To be honest, I’d been preparing for a long time—I even took three deep breaths in the office restroom before I found the courage to knock on his door. I had planned exactly what to say, but when I actually sat down, the nerves kicked in. This time was different, though. Instead of my usual stuttering and hesitating, I just came out and said it.

I said, "I’ve always believed I wasn’t cut out for management because I lack leadership and public speaking skills. But lately, I’ve started to realize that maybe these are things I can learn, rather than fixed flaws that can't be changed."

I surprised even myself when I said that. It didn't sound like something I would say. But the words just came out.

My boss’s reaction was even more unexpected. He told me he’s always valued my technical skills and that he just wanted to see me improve my communication. Then he said he would support my development.

I’m still processing this.

For five years, I’ve been telling myself that I don’t deserve a promotion, that I lack this or that, that I’m just not built for it. But what he said was—I can *improve*. How can those two words feel so different? "Unfit" versus "improvable." One is absolute; the other is full of hope.

I’m starting to think Dr. Wang’s explanations about "cognitive distortions" might actually make sense. Maybe I’ve spent all these years looking at myself in such absolute terms. Either perfect or a failure. Either a genius or a total write-off. No middle ground.

But maybe the real world *is* the middle ground. Maybe I can be neither perfect nor a failure, but just an ordinary person who can improve step by step.

Even as I say this, I’m starting to doubt myself again. Is this just wishful thinking? Will I be back to my old self in two days, unable to change a thing despite this brief burst of excitement?

But I’ve also noticed that things *have* been different lately. At the team meeting last week, I spoke up with an idea. My voice was quiet, but I said it. And a colleague actually responded. That wouldn't have happened before.

Sister Li mentioned the other day that I’ve looked "less exhausted" lately. I didn't think much of it at the time, but thinking back, maybe she’s right. I’ve stuck with my yoga classes, even on the days I wanted to bail.

Maybe the problem isn't that "I have no self-discipline," but that I’ve been looking at myself the wrong way. Maybe I’m not someone who is "incapable of change," but someone who needs time, needs help, and needs to stop being so damn hard on herself.

I don’t know how long this mindset will last. But at least for today, a promotion doesn't feel like some distant, unrealistic dream. It feels more like... a possibility? A goal that I can work toward step by step?

The feeling is unfamiliar. A little scary. But also a little... maybe.

Sister Li, the yoga group, Dr. Wang—they are all supporting you. Your job is stable and gives you time. Your parents are slowly starting to understand you.

Your way of thinking is not a flaw. Your introversion is not a flaw. Your caution is not a flaw. These are your strengths; you just need to find the right way to express them.

Stop feeling guilty about "not being promoted" or "not being married yet." You are building a more meaningful life—connections with people who truly get you, a deeper understanding of yourself, and an appreciation for life. These things are more precious than any job title or status.

Take it slow. You are already changing.
