# Memo

**Date: Saturday, June 22, 2024**

While organizing my photography work just now, I stumbled upon my diary from a year ago. Back then, I was constantly blaming myself for not getting promoted, and every time I saw a former classmate’s social media post, I’d spiral into a fit of anxiety. Looking back at those words now, it feels like I’m reading someone else’s story.

It’s not that all my problems have been solved, or that I’ve suddenly become an extrovert. It’s just that... slowly, I’ve found my own rhythm.

The idea of "Photo Walks" seems so natural now, but it took me forever to work up the courage to actually organize one. I still remember when I first invited Sister Li and the people from the yoga group—I sat in front of my phone, rewriting the message over and over again. But now, every time I see the photos everyone takes and read their thoughts in the comments, I feel a genuine sense of connection. This isn't forced socializing; it’s what I actually wanted.

Work doesn't feel as suffocating anymore, either. It’s not because I got promoted or because of some sudden shift. It’s just that now that I have other sources of fulfillment—photography, friends, observing the details of the city—work has become a part of my life, rather than the whole of it. Ironically, in this state of mind, my performance at work seems more stable.

The most interesting thing is that I’ve started to realize something: it’s not that I’m incapable of change, nor was I born to be lonely. I just needed to find a way that aligns with who I am. For me, that means instead of forcing a smile at large parties, I do interesting things with like-minded people. I feel much more at ease this way.

Another thing is that I’ve stopped being so hard on myself. Books I haven't finished, courses I didn't see through to the end—these things used to make me feel so guilty. Now, I allow myself to be imperfect. Life doesn't need to be grand; being authentic, content, and meaningful is enough.

A lot of small changes have happened over the past year, but perhaps the biggest one is that I’ve started to believe in myself. It’s not blind confidence, but a quiet assurance built up slowly through these small experiences.

Keep moving forward. I still haven't decided on the theme for next week's walk.

August 16, 2023, 8:00 PM

Sitting on the sofa, it’s pitch black outside the window. The cold air from the AC feels a bit dry; I took a sip of water before starting to write this.

Xiao Mei cried.

She reached out to me on WeChat this afternoon, saying the pressure at work has been overwhelming lately. Her boss keeps messaging her on weekends, and she doesn’t dare refuse for fear of being seen as unprofessional. I told her about how I’ve been communicating with my own supervisor recently—nothing fancy, really, just that I clearly defined my boundaries and explained that I would respond to weekend emails during working hours on Monday.

The words I used were: "Maintaining work boundaries isn't selfish; it’s a form of respect for yourself and for the work."

I surprised myself a little when I said that. Did those words really come out of my mouth? I guess they did. And I meant them.

Xiao Mei’s eyes welled up over the video call. She said, "You’re right. I’ve always felt so selfish for not wanting to work overtime." Hearing that hurt—she was feeling guilty for trying to protect herself. I know that feeling all too well.

Then I realized something strange. When I was helping her, I wasn't reciting theories or preaching. I was sharing my actual experiences, my real changes. And that experience actually seemed to help her.

It gave me a feeling that felt very foreign. It wasn't the sense of worth that comes from a job title, a promotion, or a bonus. It was... the realization that my life experiences, the path I’ve walked, and the things I’ve learned could tangibly help another person.

I’ve always felt like a professional failure—no promotion in five years, no sense of achievement. But now it suddenly occurs to me that maybe it wasn't a failure. Maybe the things I’ve learned over these five years—about balance, about boundaries, about how to live with dignity in an ordinary position—maybe those are the things that are truly valuable.

Not all value can be measured by a job title.

As I write this, I feel like laughing and crying at the same time. What is this feeling?

I have work tomorrow, but tonight, I feel incredibly at peace.

Sunday Afternoon, April 3, 2022

The tonkatsu was a failure again.

The first batch was burnt. I spent fifteen minutes adjusting the angles and the lighting just to get that one shot that looked decent. When I posted it on Douban, I wrote, "An unexpected little challenge." Sitting here now, I realize I was lying.

It wasn't an "unexpected little challenge." It was just a failure. I messed up the heat.

But why did I hide it? Why couldn't I just write, "The first batch burnt, and I'm frustrated"?

Yesterday, a colleague asked if I was a professional chef. I said, "Just a hobbyist," and then followed up with three more comments explaining my technical approach. Looking back, I was over-explaining. It’s exactly what Zhang Hao used to say—I’m always using long-winded arguments to prove myself, as if that’s the only way people will believe me or validate me.

I realize now that I care deeply about what others think.

It’s a painful realization. I thought I was doing this for the sake of change, to prove that "I’m truly different this time." But in reality, I might just be seeking validation. Buying the books, signing up for the courses, making a new dish every week... it’s all just to show everyone that "I’m trying."

My mother taught me from a young age not to cause trouble, to be cautious. Now, I feel the need to be perfect even when cooking; I even have to wrap my failures in pretty words. Is that where this comes from?

Sitting here in the kitchen, looking at that plate of charred pork chops, I suddenly feel like a hypocrite.

But then... I wonder, what if I give up? What if this ends up like all those previous courses, abandoned halfway through? I’ve already told my colleagues about this three-month plan. I’ve already been documenting my progress on Douban for two weeks. If I quit now, it’s the same as admitting I have no follow-through.

So I have to keep going. I’ll try again next week.

Maybe this failure isn't actually a failure. Maybe it’s just part of the learning process. I need to remember the heat settings; I need to adjust the temperature. This is progress, not a setback.

...There I go again, making excuses for myself.

Whatever. Let’s just eat. The other half of the plate is still edible.

Tomorrow is another day.

# Saturday, March 18, 2023 | Night

Sitting on the sofa with a cup of warm tea in my hand, the sky outside has already turned dark. I just finished talking with Xiaomei, and my mind is still a bit of a mess.

To be honest, I was really nervous before our dinner today. I don’t know why, but when Xiaomei asked what I’d been up to lately, I just blurted out that I was in therapy. The moment the words left my mouth, I regretted it, thinking things were about to get awkward. But Xiaomei’s reaction was completely unexpected—there was no look of pity, no "what's wrong with you" kind of concern. Instead, she very naturally mentioned that she’s also seeing a therapist, and that she’s been working through some work stress and relationship issues lately.

I was stunned for a moment.

Then she said something that I’m still playing over and over in my head: "Maybe a lot of people are going through similar mental challenges, and it’s not something that needs to be hidden."

I had never thought about it that way. In my mind, going to therapy was like admitting I was broken or flawed. That’s why I never proactively told anyone. But today, hearing Xiaomei say that, I suddenly realized... maybe I’ve been demonizing the whole thing.

Xiaomei said she’s learned so much about herself through counseling; she even sees it as a form of self-investment. Her tone was so casual, as natural as discussing a gym membership. As I listened, I felt myself slowly start to relax.

Sitting here at home now, I’m wondering—why do I feel like being seen and understood is such a rare thing?

Maybe it’s because I’ve been used to handling everything on my own since I was a kid, not wanting to burden others. Maybe it’s because I always felt like I should be able to cope with anything. But today’s conversation with Xiaomei made me feel something different—not sympathy, but a genuine connection. She didn’t try to "fix" me or give me advice; she simply shared her own experience.

It makes me feel like... maybe I’m not that alone.

Dr. Wang told me that I’m under pressure, not that I’m flawed. Now Xiaomei is saying that many people are going through similar things. These two things together seem to be telling me a truth I never allowed myself to believe: that changing and seeking help isn't something to hide.

I have to go home to see my parents tomorrow. I suddenly feel like talking to them about this. I’m not sure where to start, but maybe... maybe we can give it a try.

The context is that my father was diagnosed with a lumbar disc herniation and needs regular physical therapy, usually on weekends. When my mom called to tell me, I could hear the worry in her voice. I just thought, I need to adjust my schedule to be there for him. So I emailed my boss and was very direct about the reason and what I needed.

Do you know what shocked me the most? He agreed. Just like that. He even said, "Family is important; just let me know what kind of time you need."

I’m sitting in my office right now, and my mind is a bit of a mess. I’m wondering, why have I never done this before? For five years, I’ve never advocated for myself. I always felt that if I made any kind of request, it would look unprofessional or be taken as a lack of commitment. But my boss’s reaction was nothing like that.

It made me realize something. Growing up, my mom always taught me "don't cause trouble," "be good," and "don't be a burden to others." I realize now that I might have internalized this to mean not just avoiding trouble at home, but never voicing any needs at work either. It’s as if the act of making a request itself was a breach of etiquette.

But this time, because it was for my father’s care, I felt like I had a "justifiable reason" to break the pattern. My request wasn't about my own ambition or comfort; it was about family responsibility. So, I just did it.

I feel a mix of regret and relief right now. Regret that I probably could have done this a long time ago—that I might have wasted so much time imagining obstacles that weren't even there. And the relief... I don't know how to describe it. It’s like I always thought workplace communication was this massive wall, but maybe it’s not actually that high.

At the same time, I’m worried. Is this a good start? Or did I only find my voice because I had a "valid excuse," and next time I'll just go back to my old ways? Have I actually changed something, or did I just make an exception for this specific situation?

I need your help to understand this. How does this tie into my personality, my family background, and the choices I’ve been making all this time?

By the way, my father is doing okay. The doctor said it can improve with physical therapy and daily exercise. I’ve already started logging his treatment plan and the doctor’s advice. I’ve got that part under control—I know how to organize and execute. But this "professional breakthrough" thing... I really need to talk to you about it.

Tuesday, August 16, 2022 | Night

My hands were shaking a little just now when I opened the diary app. I’m not sure why I feel the need to record this, but I feel like if I don’t write it down, I might forget what happened today. Or rather, I’ll find some way to convince myself that none of it actually happened.

Xiao Zhang came to see me this afternoon. She asked if I could give a talk on the recent risk model optimizations at next week’s department meeting. It’s a small meeting—just fifteen people. She said, "It’s just an internal sharing session, no pressure."

What was my first instinct? To refuse. Just like the countless times before. I already had the excuses lined up—too busy with work, haven't organized the materials yet, someone else would be better suited for it, and so on. My mouth was practically open, ready to say it.

But... I paused.

I asked a question: "Is it a small meeting or a large one?"

It sounds like a strange question. Why did I ask that? I already knew it was fifteen people. But I asked anyway. Xiao Zhang said it was just within the department, with people I know well.

And then I said, "Then... I’ll give it a try."

*I’ll give it a try.*

Now, sitting at home, I’m still thinking about those words.

I’m nervous, for sure. Starting tomorrow, I’ll have to begin preparing. I’ll have to figure out exactly how to present it, practice over and over, remind myself not to speak too fast, and... anyway, I shouldn't overthink the details.

But there’s another feeling there, too. I don’t know how to describe it. Maybe a little... excited? Or maybe it’s just that strange sensation that comes from making an unusual decision.

I’ve heard the term "automatic response" in my sessions with Dr. Wang. She said I have an automatic refusal response to many things, like a reflex. I’ve never really stopped to ask myself, "Is this a decision I actually want to make, or is my fear making the decision for me?"

Today, I stopped. I just paused for a moment and asked a question.

And then I said yes.

I’m not sure if it’s a good decision or a bad one. Next week might be miserable. I might lose sleep the night before the meeting. My voice might tremble while I’m speaking. All of that is possible.

But at least... at least I didn't automatically say no.

It sounds like it’s no big deal. But for me, it feels a little different.

Sister Li said the day before yesterday that change doesn't happen all at once. She said she was very introverted when she was young, and she still is, but she learned how to step up when necessary. She said, "You don’t need to become a completely different person; you just need to be a little gentler with yourself sometimes."

I didn't quite understand what she meant then. But today, I think I understand a little.

Back to work tomorrow. I won't think too much for now.

I’ll leave it at that.

# Wednesday, July 5, 2023 – Sunny

Just got back from the wet market. Still sweating a little, so I’m sitting on the sofa having a glass of cold water. Sister Li should be home by now, too.

Today was strange. Or rather, not strange, but hard to put into words.

At the market, I saw these shellfish called "clams," and Sister Li asked me how they should be prepared. I just casually rattled off a few ideas—clear soup, stir-fry, or maybe a sauce for pasta. After listening, Sister Li said, "You’re a real thinker."

Just that one sentence.

I paused for a moment, then said, "I’ve always thought this way; it’s just that before, no one ever asked for my opinion."

I surprised even myself when I said that. Because it felt like the truth. I had never realized it before, but looking back, it really is true.

In my old job, I would analyze data and identify problems, but that was because the job required it. In my personal life, I think about a lot of things too—how to coordinate outfits, how to plan the weekend, how to cook a dish—but since no one ever asked, I just kept those thoughts to myself, overthinking things alone. Eventually, I’d either give up or just do things someone else’s way.

Sister Li is different. She asks, "What do you think?" and then she actually listens.

On the way back, I was thinking about my plans for July. I had made a list earlier to try ten new recipes this month. At the time, I was a bit nervous, worried I wouldn't stick with it. But it feels different now. Now, I’m wondering what new combinations I want to try. Maybe something with clams?

The shift is subtle. But it feels important.

I’m no longer just "completing a task"; I’m "looking forward" to it.

I’m a bit tired, but it’s that good kind of tired.

# Japanese Tempura | My First Batch of Failures

Another disaster in the kitchen today.

I tried making tempura at home this afternoon. I didn't control the oil temperature well for the first batch—the batter charred the moment it hit the pan, while the shrimp and vegetables inside were still raw. I took photos, burnt bits and all, without trying to find the "perfect angle" like I did with the tonkatsu last time. I just posted them as they were.

I’m a bit tired.

Later, I lowered the heat, and the second batch turned out much better. The shrimp had a nice crunch, and the batter was golden. This temperature seems right—around 160°C. I’ve noted it down.

Actually, when it comes to cooking, I’ve realized I overthink things. I used to obsess over plating just for a single photo, feeling like everything had to be perfect to be worth recording. But today, looking at those blackened pieces of tempura, I just snapped the picture and shared it. It didn't feel like such a big deal after all.

Maybe next time will be better. Or rather, next time will be another chance to learn.

I’ve been reading a book on Japanese cuisine lately. It mentions *Ichi-go Ichi-e*—the idea that every encounter is unique; the ingredients, the weather, and one's mood are never the same twice. That failed batch is just as much a part of the process.

I’m starting to embrace this feeling of imperfection.

---

*Update: Oil temperature is key. Will try using a thermometer next time.*

Mom said it again: "Starting another new hobby, I see."

That tone... I know it all too well. It’s the same one she used for all those books and courses I bought before. She didn’t say it outright, but I could feel that "we all know how this ends" look in her eyes. I snapped at her immediately, telling her I’ve already stuck with it for two weeks and that this time is different. Even as the words came out, I could hear the desperation in my own voice—it sounded like I was pleading my case.

She ended with, "I hope you can keep it up." Mom, that’s the part that stings the most.

It’s not a reproach, which makes it even harder to take. Because behind it lies... uncertainty. Doubt. Or rather, she’s just grown used to my "three-minute passions." I thought of all the things I never finished—the yoga classes, the photography tutorials, that sociology book... all gathering dust in the corners of my bookshelf.

I looked at my notes again: "One new dish every week for three months." I used three exclamation marks. Now, they just look... glaring.

Can I really stick with it?

No, I can. I’ve already done two weeks. Tamagoyaki on Monday, miso soup on Wednesday, and a beef bowl last weekend. I logged everything on Douban in great detail—every step, the temperatures, the exact amount of salt. I’m not wasting time; I’m taking this seriously. My colleagues even asked about my lunchbox; they said it looked professional. I was so happy in that moment because someone actually saw my progress.

But what Mom said... it’s making me scared. Scared that I really will give up in two months just like before. Scared that she’ll give me that "I knew it" look.

But what I fear most is that I’ll start looking at myself that way, too.

What am I doing right now? I’m writing in my diary instead of being in the kitchen. I should be prepping tomorrow’s ingredients, yet here I am, overthinking whether I can "stick with it." That in itself is a problem, isn't it?

Whatever. Tomorrow goes on. I’ll make the next dish. I’ll document the process. I’ll prove it to Mom, and I’ll prove it to myself.

This time really is different.

I have to make it different.

# February 22, 2022, 11:50 PM

Insomnia again.

I spent the last hour lying in bed scrolling through my phone, eventually clicking on a "Self-Assessment Scale for Anxiety." I don’t know why, but lately, I’ve been prone to falling into this... how should I put it? Compulsive self-diagnosis.

Checking off the items:
- Feeling irritable or keyed up ✓
- Fatiguing easily ✓
- Difficulty concentrating ✓
- Poor sleep ✓
- Ruminating on the same issues ✓✓✓
- Worrying about the future ✓

The final score came out: Moderate Anxiety.

Seeing that result gave me a strange feeling. On one hand, it was a "just as I thought" moment; on the other... I felt even more scared. It’s as if by framing these vague, heavy feelings with a medical diagnosis, they become more real. Like something is actually broken.

But do I really have an anxiety disorder? Or am I just over-interpreting normal reactions?

My mind has been running simulations for days. First, it was my father’s retirement, then the cost-of-living table for the elderly, then... ten years from now, twenty years from now. If I’m like this already, what about thirty years down the line? Will I end up like my father, with my health failing, and then everything just... stops?

I’ve been filling spreadsheets with numbers—insurance premiums, medical expenses, caregiving costs. These numbers make me feel safe because they give me the illusion of "control." But in reality? I can’t control anything. I can’t control my father’s health, and I can’t control what I’ll become in ten years.

Rational planning can’t solve this kind of fear.

I know that. I really do.

And yet, I’m still making spreadsheets. Still researching insurance products. Still double-checking my calculations for any omissions. It’s like a ritual—as long as I keep "doing" something, I can pretend I still have a grip on things.

Maybe I should see a therapist. Dr. Wang’s business card is still in my drawer. I’ve thought about it before, but I’ve never had the courage to make the call. How do I explain it... admitting I need help feels like admitting there really is "something wrong" with me. And once I say it out loud, it’s no longer just a vague anxiety in my head; it becomes a symptom that needs "treatment."

I don’t know if I’m ready.

Or rather, I don’t want to face it at all.

It’s almost midnight. I have work tomorrow. I should sleep. But I know I’ll just keep lying here, my mind going in circles, continuing to play out those "ten years later" scenarios.

Maybe tomorrow I’ll find the courage to call Dr. Wang.

Maybe.

But for now, I just want to turn off my phone, close my eyes, and pretend none of this is happening.

But over these last two months—through those meetups and things like the cooking gathering—I feel like I’ve started to believe I can do things differently. I’ve started to find the courage to express my ideas, and I’ve started to feel that my thoughts have value.

It might sound like an exaggeration, but really, the day you reached out and invited me meant a lot.

Now I need to give this opportunity some serious thought. Maybe we can chat when we do yoga together this weekend? I’d love to hear your thoughts, but more importantly, I need to figure out for myself what I truly want.

Thank you.

## Draft 3 — Key Points Outline

**What to say tomorrow (Face-to-face):**

1.  **The Opening** ✓
    *   "Thank you for the trust you’ve placed in my work."
    *   "I’d like to discuss the possibility of some work flexibility."
    *   ~~"Something happened at home."~~ → "There’s a situation at home recently that requires my care."

2.  **The Core Request** ✓
    *   My father was recently diagnosed with a lumbar disc herniation; he needs regular medical appointments and rehab.
    *   As an only child, I need to take on the caregiving responsibilities.
    *   Discuss whether we can flexibly adjust my hours (e.g., remote work on certain days, or shifting start/end times).
    *   ~~"I might need to take leave."~~ → "I’m hoping to find a balanced solution that allows me to care for my family while maintaining the quality of my work."

3.  **The Delivery** ✓
    *   Emphasize: This will not affect my performance.
    *   Emphasize: I’ve already been thinking about how to manage this; I’m not just winging it.
    *   Emphasize: I appreciate the support the company has always shown.
    *   ~~Don't say "I know this might be a hassle."~~ → State the need directly.

4.  **Anticipated Responses & My Rebuttals** ✓
    *   If he agrees: Thank him, confirm specific arrangements.
    *   If he says "I need to think about it": Ask when I can expect an answer; show understanding.
    *   If he says "This will affect your evaluation": Explain that this is necessary, but I will ensure work remains unaffected.
    *   If he refuses outright: ... (Deep breath) Say I understand, but I’ll have to think of other options.

This isn't about being lazy.

# Private Chat with Dr. Wang

Dr. Wang, I wanted to share something with you before our next session.

After yoga class today, Sister Li (the neighbor I mentioned before) came up and asked how I was doing. She said she’d noticed I seemed a bit distracted lately. Usually, I would have just given a polite, brief response, but for some reason... I just said it. I told her I’ve been thinking about making some adjustments at work and questioning whether I really want to move into a management role.

You know I’m not usually the type to open up to people like that.

After she listened, she said something that I can’t stop thinking about: "You deserve to have a balanced life."

I think that really struck a chord. I realized I’ve been measuring myself by a single standard—promotions, higher titles, proving my worth. But when she said that, it suddenly hit me: maybe... maybe my definition of "success" has been too narrow all along?

It reminded me of the question you asked last time—"Is a promotion really what you want?" I didn't have a clear answer then; I just felt confused. But today in the yoga group, that feeling of being relaxed, of not needing to prove anything... it felt like an answer in itself.

I’m starting to think that when you talked about "finding the right way to socialize," you weren't just talking about social interaction. Maybe it’s also about finding a lifestyle where I don’t constantly feel the need to "prove myself." In the yoga group, I don’t have to talk much or act extroverted, yet I feel accepted. It’s a completely different feeling from what I’m used to.

I even stayed to chat with Sister Li and the others for a bit after class. Even though I didn't say much, I realized I didn't have that usual urge to leave immediately. I just... stayed there, and it felt okay.

What I’m struggling with now is—is this real progress? Or is it just a fleeting emotional wave? I don’t want to overthink it, but these changes definitely feel different.

Also, I’m wondering if I should start making some adjustments at work too. Not giving up on my career, but redefining what actually matters to me. I’m just not sure exactly how to go about it yet.

What do you think?

I’ve only recently come to realize that sometimes, being seen is more important than being understood.

When my father asked, "How’s work going lately?" it hit me that he’s always been there. It’s just that neither of us is very good with words.

When my mother spoke about her dreams from when she was young, it was the first time I’d ever heard her talk about herself in that tone. In that moment, I finally understood that her "over-cautiousness" wasn't a constraint—it was all the warmth she knew how to give.

While home for dinner, I made a Japanese dish I’d recently learned. My father took a bite and didn't say anything, but the look in his eyes changed. When my mother asked, "When did you learn to make this?" I realized I’d never actually told them much about what I’ve been up to.

Maybe that’s just how my family is—we aren't great at expressing ourselves, but we never stop caring.

I don't feel quite so lonely anymore.

September 18, 2023. Evening.

I just got back from home; haven't even had a chance to change my clothes yet. When Mom saw me to the door, I suddenly felt like crying.

Dinner with my parents today was supposed to be just another routine end-of-the-month thing. But this time was different. I noticed my voice sounded completely different when I was talking about the yoga group. As I described how Sister Li invited me, how we practiced the poses together, and how someone complimented me on my progress... I could hear the passion and vibrancy in my own voice. It felt unfamiliar, yet so real.

What’s even stranger is that my parents’ reactions changed, too. Instead of asking the usual formulaic questions like "How's work going?", Mom actually wanted to listen. She asked me what kind of person Sister Li is, what I learned in cooking class, and even if I’ve been getting enough rest lately. Dad was listening intently, too, and he asked me one thing: "You look happier now than you used to."

That was the line.

I didn't answer right away; I just smiled. But my eyes welled up. I realized that I’ve always been focused on how to take care of them, how to shoulder responsibilities, and how to keep them from worrying. I never considered that taking care of myself and being happy is, in itself, the best way to give back to them. Seeing me like this puts their minds at ease more than any raise or promotion ever could.

Mom said, "Seeing you like this makes me feel relieved." It’s that simple.

On the way back, I was wondering: what exactly has changed in me over these past few months? I’m still that same introvert, still the same person who overthinks everything. But it feels like something has opened up. It’s not that I’ve become an extrovert; it’s just that... I’ve found a way to engage with life on my own terms. The yoga group, the cooking, chatting with Sister Li—none of these are "big" things, but they are real, and they are things I’ve stuck with.

I used to think I lacked follow-through because I could never finish the piles of books and courses I bought. But now I understand—maybe it wasn't me; maybe I was just choosing the wrong things. When I find the right thing, the right environment, and the right people, I actually can stick with it.

The question now is, how do I keep this feeling going? Or maybe I don’t need to "keep" anything at all, and I just need to keep doing what I’m doing?

I don’t know. But at least for today, I feel good.

January 22, 2022, 11:45 PM

I’ve closed all the tabs.

The Excel sheet is still there on my desktop. File name: "Retirement Cost Estimate_Ten Years." I’ve been staring at it all afternoon. From 2:00 PM until now, I haven’t done a single thing except drink a cup of tea that went cold ages ago.

Just staring at those numbers.

Medical insurance, nursing costs, daily expenses, emergency reserves... I listed everything I could think of. I even color-coded them: green for basic costs, red for risk factors. Very professional. Very rational. Just like building a risk assessment model at work.

But as I kept looking, I suddenly lost track of what I was actually doing.

What did those flickering numbers on the screen turn into? They turned into the money that gets deducted from my salary every month. They turned into a life where I might never be able to save a penny. They turned into... can I really take care of them?

What Mom said the last time I went home is still ringing in my ears. She was so calm, as if she were just talking about the weather: "You’ll have to look after us from now on."

"From now on."

I never truly thought about what that phrase meant. Before, I just saw it as... an obligation? A responsibility? But now it has become something concrete, something heavy. It’s not in the future; it’s right here, right now, pressing down on my chest.

I should have thought of this sooner. Why didn't I? It’s been five years since they retired, yet I’ve been acting like nothing has changed—going home for dinner on time every month, giving them money on time, and that was it. I haven’t even truly asked about their health. Has Dad’s pace slowed down? When did that slight tremor in Mom’s fingers start?

I don’t know.

I should know, but I don’t.

Now I’ve started scrutinizing their every move, as if looking for evidence—proof that they are aging, proof that my fears are justified. It’s a terrifying way to think. What am I afraid of? Am I afraid of them getting old? Or am I afraid that I won’t be able to handle it?

Both.

I closed the spreadsheet. I don’t want to look at those numbers anymore. But they’re already seared into my brain. Every month, every year, ten years... time has been sliced into units that need to be planned out.

I’ve never thought about life this way before.

Maybe I’m really not cut out to take care of anyone. I can’t even manage my own life—all those books I bought but never read, the courses I signed up for but couldn't stick with, five years into my career and I’m still in the same spot. How am I supposed to take care of my parents?

But I have to. There’s no one else.

I’m sitting here, looking out at the pitch-black night, and I suddenly feel so tired. Not physically tired, but a different kind of exhaustion. A sense of... powerlessness?

I have work tomorrow. My inbox is probably piling up. I should sleep.

But right now, I can’t do anything.

The other day, I was talking to my dad, and he told me he understands why I don't want the promotion. He said having someone present at home is more important than having someone move up the ladder in an office.

I never expected him to say that. I always thought my choice was a compromise, a retreat. It turns out, someone actually gets it.

Life has been coming into focus lately. Not because everything is resolved, but because I’m starting to embrace who I actually am. Being introverted or cautious isn't a flaw—it’s just me.

While doing yoga with Sister Li and the others, I realized that I don't need to change myself to fit in. As long as I speak my truth, there will be people who understand.

This realization is worth holding onto.

# May 8, 2023, 9:00 PM

Another night home alone. I just finished the dishes; my fingers are still a bit damp as I sit on the sofa and unlock my phone.

About that dish I made today… to be honest, I was a bit nervous at first. Ever since Sister Li’s invitation the other day, I’ve been debating whether I should actually join the cooking group. Truthfully, my first instinct was that familiar voice in my head: *Look at the Japanese cookbooks, the classes you took before… you never stuck with any of them. This time won't be any different.*

But I tried making it this afternoon anyway. I didn't follow the recipe to the letter; instead, I just looked at what was in the fridge and started improvising. I used the ingredients I had on hand and tweaked a few steps. As I was cooking, I thought to myself—this isn't procrastinating or being lazy; it’s actually optimizing, right? I was thinking, I was adjusting… that counts as a form of… creativity, doesn't it?

After I finished, I logged the whole process on Douban. Not to prove anything, and not for the sake of "checking off a task," but simply because I wanted to record those thoughts. While eating, I felt a strange sense of satisfaction—not the "I completed a plan" kind, but the "I enjoyed the process" kind.

Does this sound like I'm just lying to myself? Maybe. But I’m starting to wonder if the problem was never a lack of willpower… maybe I’ve just been putting too much pressure on myself? When Sister Li said "no pressure," I didn't really take it to heart at the time, but looking back now, those words seem to have struck a chord.

I’ve always defined success through "persistence" and "completion." If I can't do that, I've failed. But what if… the definition of success itself was wrong all along?

Still, I’m a little scared. Scared that this is just a one-time fluke. Scared that I’m fooling myself again, telling myself "this time is different," only to fall back into the same old patterns next month.

Whatever, I won't overthink it for now. At least today’s meal was genuinely delicious.

I'll worry about tomorrow when it comes.

# Pre-session Notes

Dr. Wang, I wanted to write down what’s been happening over the last few days before our next session. I’m afraid that once I’m sitting in front of you, I might get a bit nervous again and fail to express some of these thoughts clearly.

This trip home was indeed different. One day, while my mom was in the kitchen, I told her directly that I’m starting to understand why she’s always told me to be cautious—that it wasn't because she wanted to restrict me, but because she was protecting me in the only way she knew how. I even surprised myself by saying that, because in the past, I mostly just complained about her parenting style in my head. But this time, I truly felt grateful. My mom stopped what she was doing and just looked at me for a long time, her eyes welling up.

The most striking moment was with my dad. He’s always been a man of few words; our conversations are usually just brief greetings at the dinner table. But after dinner that day, he suddenly said, "You’ve always been a sensible child." Just that one sentence. I didn't quite know how to react at the moment, but I felt this overwhelming ache in my chest. I realized that all these years, I might have been waiting for him to say something like that—not necessarily to praise how well I’ve done, but just to feel truly seen by him.

On the high-speed train back, I wrote a long journal entry reflecting on these changes. I’ve been wondering: have I been repeating my mother’s patterns all these years? That cautiousness, that self-restraint, that feeling of not wanting to trouble anyone. But now I’m starting to see that it’s not all bad. These traits have also made me a reliable person. It’s just... I need to learn how to relax and express myself a bit more on top of that foundation.

But that’s where I’m most confused right now. I am changing; that much is true. Since starting therapy, I’ve definitely become more proactive and have found the courage to speak my mind at work. But... is this a real change? Or am I just trying to become who you expect me to be? Or who my mother expects me to be?

Also, I took the initiative to suggest to my dad that we go for a medical checkup together. I never would have brought that up before because I felt like their health was their own business. But this time I said it, and my dad actually agreed. We’ve set a date for next month. I’m wondering, is this growth? Or am I over-functioning and taking on too much responsibility again?

I’m also starting to worry about whether these changes will last. I know my pattern—I start with a lot of enthusiasm and then gradually slack off. Will it be the same this time? I’m afraid of slipping back into my old state, but I’m also afraid of this constant tension of trying to change.

Things at work are a bit complicated, too. After our last session, I spoke up during a meeting, and my boss went out of his way to praise me. It made me a little happy, but at the same time, I felt a sense of dread—fear that he now has new expectations of me, and fear that I’ll have to start proving myself all over again.

I’m not sure if I need to continue with the sessions. It’s not that therapy isn't helping, but I’m wondering: if I can maintain these changes on my own, does that mean I don’t actually need that much help? On the other hand, I feel like without the support of our sessions, I might just shrink back again.

It’s all a bit messy. I know there might not be standard answers to these questions, but I just want to sort through them properly during our session.

— Xiaoyu

## I. Symptom Self-Assessment

Based on the PHQ-9 and GAD-7 scales, my current situation is as follows:

| Symptom | Frequency | Severity | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Restlessness / Inability to focus | Almost daily | Moderate | Constantly checking emails; struggling to enter "deep work" mode. |
| Sleep issues | 3-4 times/week | Moderate | Difficulty falling asleep; waking up early; mind racing with insurance plans. |
| Muscle tension | Persistent | Mod-Severe | Neck, shoulders, and lower back; worsens in the afternoon. |
| Fatigue | Almost daily | Moderate | Feeling exhausted even after getting enough sleep. |
| Chest tightness / Palpitations | Occasional | Mild | Occurs during high stress; lasts for a few minutes. |
| Excessive worrying | Almost daily | Severe | Parents' health, retirement costs, my own career prospects. |
| Irritability | 1-2 times/week | Mild-Mod | Overreacting to colleagues' questions. |

**Self-Assessment Result**: Moderate Anxiety (GAD-7 score approx. 15)

---

## II. Trigger Analysis

- Financial and health responsibilities regarding parents' retirement.
- Career stagnation (no progress in 5 years); "Age 35" anxiety.
- Decision paralysis regarding insurance products (information overload).
- Social media comparisons (peers getting married, promoted, or having children).
- Frequent overtime; rest time being interrupted by work.
- Bearing the weight of all decisions alone; lack of someone to confide in.

---

## III. Action Plan

### Immediate Actions (This Week)
- [ ] Book an appointment with Dr. Wang (Counselor) for an initial assessment (non-medical).
- [ ] Schedule a physical check-up: Cardiology screening, cervical spine X-ray, and basic blood pressure/glucose tests.
- [ ] Maintain a symptom diary: Record the time, intensity, and triggers of each anxiety episode.

### Short-term Actions (2-4 Weeks)
- [ ] Consult a doctor regarding the necessity of medication.
- [ ] Learn anxiety management techniques (yoga, meditation, breathing exercises).
- [ ] Have an honest conversation with my parents to clarify mutual expectations and needs.
- [ ] Discuss workload and overtime issues with my supervisor.

### Long-term Actions (1-3 Months)
- [ ] Complete a course of counseling (recommended 10-15 sessions).
- [ ] Develop a realistic retirement plan (rather than a "perfect" one).
- [ ] Evaluate career direction: Is it time for a change, or should I accept the status quo?
- [ ] Establish consistent stress-relief habits (yoga, walking, cooking).

---

## IV. Resources

- PHQ-9 and GAD-7 Scales: Self-assessment completed via the DXY (DingXiang) App.
- Counselor: Dr. Wang (Need to confirm if they are accepting new patients).
- Medical Center: Company-partnered health check-up center.
- Reading Material: Purchased *The Anxiety Recovery Handbook* (pending reading).

---

## V. Progress Indicators

Evaluate once a week:
- Sleep Quality (1-10)
- Work Focus (1-10)
- Physical Tension (1-10)
- Overall Anxiety Level (1-10)

**Note**: This is not a formal medical diagnosis, but a self-assessment based on standardized scales. A formal diagnosis requires professional medical confirmation. However, I must acknowledge that this anxiety has begun to impact my work and quality of life; I can no longer afford to procrastinate.

April 3, 2023, 7:30 PM

Sitting on the sofa, teacup in hand, but I haven't taken a single sip. Dr. Wang’s words from today keep replaying in my head.

"We could consider moving our sessions from once a week to once every two weeks. You’ve made clear progress lately, and I feel you’ve built up enough self-awareness."

What was my first reaction when I heard that? Defensiveness. Almost instinctively, I asked her, "Does this mean I don't need as much help anymore? Or that I’m not making enough progress?"

Looking back now, I feel a bit embarrassed. Dr. Wang didn’t answer directly; she just said, in that gentle tone of hers, that this is exactly what progress looks like—that I’m becoming capable of independent thought.

But that’s exactly the problem.

I don’t know if I’m happy about my independence or terrified of what I’m losing. Reducing the sessions should, in theory, be a good thing. It means I’m less dependent on external support, that I can handle things on my own. It’s a sign of growth, right?

Then why, as I was leaving the office, did I whisper those words: "I’m going to miss these conversations"?

I haven't even figured out what I meant by that. Miss what? Miss Dr. Wang? Not exactly. Miss the feeling of being heard? It’s more than that. I think I miss having someone listen to the sound of me thinking. In that safe space, I can voice all the contradictions and confusion tangled up in my head without the fear of being judged.

Now, I have to do that work myself.

It’s supposed to sound empowering. But why does it feel more like being let go?

I know it sounds contradictory. I’ve been saying all along that I want independence, that I want to change, that I want to stop relying on others for validation. Now the opportunity is here, Dr. Wang believes I’m ready, and my first reaction is... fear?

Or rather, I’m questioning if I really am ready.

Is this shift to bi-weekly sessions the signal that she’s starting to let go? In a few months, will she suggest we stop altogether? And then I’ll really have to face all of this alone.

But I also know that this is exactly what I need to do.

I’ve started expressing myself more proactively at work. When Sister Li invited me to the yoga group, I went, and I’m going back. I even took the initiative to talk to my father about work during last week’s family dinner. These are things I couldn't have done before.

So maybe Dr. Wang is right. Maybe I really am making progress.

But progress and loss always seem to be two sides of the same coin. I gain something, and I lose something at the same time. I don’t know if the trade-off is worth it.

Or perhaps, I’m just not ready to admit that it is.

Tomorrow, I’ll keep working, keep living. In two weeks, I’ll see Dr. Wang again. Maybe by then, I’ll have a clearer answer. Maybe I’ll realize I don’t need those frequent conversations after all.

But right now, sitting here, I just feel a bit empty.

# February 16, 2022, 8:30 PM

The insomnia is back.

Ever since I got home today and saw Dad, the way he climbed the stairs has been replaying in my head. His pace was slower than last time; he paused midway, his hand gripping the railing. Mom said, "It’s normal at his age," but that phrase only made me more anxious. What does "normal" even mean? Does aging just happen this naturally, this inevitably?

I’m lying in bed, but my mind won't shut off. Walking slower → are his legs losing strength? → could it be a sign of a stroke? → or Parkinson’s... one thought after another keeps jumping out, and I can't stop them. I know I’m being irrational, but I just can’t help it.

All those spreadsheets I made, the insurance comparisons, the cost projections... they all feel like self-deception now. I can calculate how much their medical expenses might be, and I can list out every clause in an insurance policy, but I can’t calculate when Dad will actually get sick. Data can't protect them.

This is the first time I’ve realized so clearly—I have no control over their aging.

I’m reminded of what Dr. Wang said about the link between anxiety and the need for control. I always thought I was being "responsible," but in reality, I was trying to use logic to fight fear. But fear doesn't play by those rules.

It’s 8:30 PM. I’ve been lying here for two hours, eyes wide open. My phone is right next to me; for a moment, I wanted to open that insurance comparison sheet again, just to check if I missed anything. But I know doing that won't change a thing.

Am I overreacting? Or have I been avoiding what I actually need to face all along?

Whatever, I’ll just write it down for now. Maybe things will look clearer tomorrow. Or maybe they won't.

# April 3, 2024, Evening

Just got back from yoga group. I haven’t even changed yet, but I couldn't wait to write this down.

Something small happened today, but it feels significant.

Sister Li asked if I’d be interested in organizing a photo walk—inviting everyone from the yoga group to go out and take pictures around the city. My instinct was to just say "Sure" and leave it at that, passively following along like I usually do. But as the words were on the tip of my tongue, I paused. Instead, I said, "Let me think about how we can organize it."

The moment I said it, I surprised even myself.

It’s not a huge deal, but... I realized this is the first time I didn't just passively accept an invitation; I actually wanted to take the lead in planning. Auntie Wang immediately said, "That’s wonderful, we’re all looking forward to it," and Sister Li smiled, saying she trusted I’d do a great job. In that moment, I felt... what was it? Seen? Trusted?

Tonight, I pulled out the photos I’ve taken over the past few days. When I showed them to Sister Li, I was still a bit nervous, saying, "They’re just random shots, probably nothing special." But when she asked why I chose those specific angles and lighting, I found myself using so many more words than usual to explain. My eyes must have been glowing, because she was smiling as she looked at me.

I’m thinking about what Dr. Wang said before—it’s not that I’m "not cut out for socializing." I just need to find the right way and the people who truly understand me. Sister Li, Auntie Wang... they get me. In the yoga group, I don’t have to pretend to be an extrovert. I can just be myself—the quiet version of me—and that version is accepted.

Another thought has been circling in my head. I used to think I lacked "execution power" because I could never stick with all those books and courses I bought, and I felt so guilty about it. But now... I think I see it differently. It’s not that I lack the ability to get things done; it’s that I’ve been trying to do things that don’t belong to me. When I’m doing something I actually want to do—like photography, or organizing this walk—I actually have plenty of drive.

Tomorrow I’ll start making a list: the route, the best timing, maybe a theme... I’m actually looking forward to this?

One last thought before bed. I feel like I’ve been changing lately, bit by bit. It’s not a drastic or forced change, but... a natural progression in the right direction.

This is good.

August 12, 2023, 6:45 PM

I just finished washing my hands and sat down on the sofa, still a bit in disbelief over how I spent my day.

Two hours. I spent two whole hours cleaning the apartment, organizing the bookshelves, and wiping down the windows. I even went online to look up how to host guests and watched three Japanese cooking tutorials. Right now, the smell of kombu dashi is still lingering in the kitchen, and the fridge is stocked with the sushi and tempura I made myself.

This is so strange. I never imagined I’d be doing this.

In the past, when Sister Li invited me to gatherings at her place, I was passive. I would go, but I’d start getting nervous an hour beforehand, and once I arrived, I’d just sit in the corner and listen to others talk. And now? Now, I’m the one proactively inviting everyone to my home. I’m even wondering if I should prepare something extra for tomorrow.

The strangest part is that while I was doing all this, I wasn't thinking, "Will they think I’m not doing enough?" or "Will I say the wrong thing?" Instead, I was thinking—does Sister Li prefer lighter flavors? Does Sister Wang from the yoga group have any dietary restrictions? How can I make everyone feel relaxed in my home?

When did I start caring about these things?

Sitting here in my clean living room, looking at the tidy bookshelves and the polished coffee table, I suddenly feel like laughing. I did this. I did this for my friends. Not to prove anything, not to make people think I’m capable—just simply because I want them to be comfortable.

Maybe this is what change feels like. It’s not about becoming a different person; it’s about starting to care about different things.

To be honest, though, I’m a little nervous. I didn't sleep well. I keep running through tomorrow’s schedule in my head—when to serve the food, how to greet everyone, whether there will be any awkward silences. But this nervousness feels different. It’s not that "will I be judged" kind of fear; it’s more like... anticipation? Or maybe just the pressure of wanting things to go well?

I’m not entirely sure.

But I do know one thing—I feel fulfilled right now. Tired, but fulfilled.

See you tomorrow.

February 28, 2022, past 11:00 PM

Just finished showering and I’m lying in bed, but my head is still buzzing.

I talked with Zhang Xin for a long time today. We were in the office when she suddenly asked how my weekend was. My gut reaction was to say I stayed home and read. She said, "I thought you didn't have a life outside of work"—that sentence has been stuck in my head ever since. I actually opened up to her quite a bit, talking about my parents and my anxiety. It turns out she’s also taking care of her sick mother. We talked for over half an hour.

At the end, I said, "I need to focus more on my own life, not just work." The moment the words left my mouth, I stunned myself. Did I really just say that?

Then she invited me to go to yoga with her this weekend. To my surprise, I actually agreed. I even told her, "I'll reach out to you." It still feels a bit surreal now that I think about it.

But… I’m starting to feel scared again. Scared that it was just a momentary impulse. Scared that the weekend will actually come and I’ll have to do yoga with strangers. Scared that I’ll end up feeling exhausted and regretful afterwards, just like before.

"I thought you didn't have a life outside of work"—why did that sting so much? It’s as if she was saying I’ve been nothing but a work machine for the past five years. Wake up, go to work, leave work, go home, stay home on weekends, visit home once a month. Just a constant loop. I thought this was stability, that it was "safe." But looking at it now, it feels more like I’m running away from something.

Do I really need to change? Or was I just caught up in Zhang Xin’s enthusiasm and felt like I should try to have a "normal life" too?

My father has been coughing again lately. I’m wondering if I should convince him to go to the hospital for a check-up. But I know he won’t go. And I know that even if he does, there’s nothing I can change. People get old; people get sick. All I can do is… nothing.

I remember what Dr. Wang said last time—that I need to learn to accept powerlessness. But I don’t know how.

Am I really going this weekend? I’m still not sure. But I promised. I said, "I'll reach out to you."

I never break my word.

So… I guess I’m going.

But I’m still scared.

# Saturday Night, March 5, 2022

Bought a bunch of stuff again.

I spent nearly 45 minutes standing in the supermarket this afternoon, constantly glancing back and forth between the recipe photos on my phone and the seaweed on the shelves. When the clerk asked if I needed help, I felt a bit self-conscious asking about the quality of the imported brands. In the end, I bought the most expensive one—89 yuan a box. On the way home, I kept repeating to myself: "This is an investment, not a waste."

Even as I said it, I could hear the forced conviction in my own voice. It felt more like I was trying to convince myself rather than actually believing it.

Three cookbooks, an online course, imported ingredients... the bills are adding up to nearly 2,000 yuan. I wrote "One new dish every week, stick to it for three months" in my Notes app, followed by three exclamation marks. Looking at those three exclamation marks now, I don't know whether to laugh or cry.

Is it really different this time? Or am I just lying to myself again?

The yoga mat I bought last time is currently gathering dust under my bed. Those "how to build a habit" courses? I gave up by the third lesson. Five original English novels—I haven't finished a single one. It’s always the same: I start with a burst of passion, buy the best gear, make the most detailed plans, and then... nothing.

But today, when my colleague saw the Japanese-style bento in my lunchbox, her eyes lit up. She asked if I made it myself. In that moment, I felt seen. It felt so good to be seen. I said, "Yes, I've been learning Japanese cuisine lately," and I sounded like someone with taste, someone with follow-through, someone who is making progress.

I want to be that person all the time.

But... will finishing this three-month plan actually change anything? Will it really prove I have discipline? Or am I just using a new hobby to mask my self-doubt?

I'm 35 now. On WeChat, my old classmates are all posting about their kids, their promotions, their new houses. I’m posting my lunchbox. It sounds ridiculous.

When Mom asked me last time when I’d consider dating again, I didn't answer. What I wanted to say was—if I can't even stick to a simple plan, how am I supposed to maintain a relationship? But I didn't say it. I never say these things out loud.

Whatever. Let’s just put the seaweed away for now. First dish tomorrow.

Maybe this time really will be different. Maybe.

July 15, 2023, 7:30 PM

Something very strange happened today. I’m still replaying it in my head, trying to figure out what it means for me.

Xiao Wang asked me about the data visualization plan this afternoon. This has happened many times before, and usually, I’d just send an email or say something brief like, "I used a line chart and a heat map," and leave it at that. But today, for some reason, I stopped. I looked at him and started explaining—not just the conclusion, but the entire thought process. Why not a bar chart? Because the time dimension of this dataset is crucial; the fluctuations in user behavior need to be visible. Why the heat map? Because I wanted to show the differences between user segments simultaneously...

As I was speaking, I felt something. A strange sense of flow. It was as if I was finally allowed to let out what was in my head instead of keeping it all bottled up. Xiao Wang listened intently and even brought up a perspective I hadn't considered. We talked for about ten minutes. Ten whole minutes.

Then, during the afternoon meeting, my boss asked if I could share some of my recent thoughts. My first instinct was tension—that familiar urge to decline. But this time, I heard myself say, "Sure, let me organize my thoughts." I was a bit shocked at myself the moment the words left my mouth. I actually agreed.

Now I’m sitting at my desk, looking at a sticky note that says: "Next week’s team meeting—share new ideas on data visualization." Looking at it, I suddenly realized this is completely different from my usual notes like "Finish Q3 report" or "Fix data pipeline." Those are tasks. This is... an idea? My idea.

I’m thinking about Sister Li. Going to the wet market and cooking with her this month seems to have unlocked something. I’ve found that when I stop forcing myself to be "perfect" or to "persevere," I actually end up doing more. Maybe that same logic applies to work? Maybe I’ve always thought I lacked execution, when in reality, I was just approaching it the wrong way.

I’m still a little scared, though. Scared that next week’s presentation will be a disaster. Scared that I’ll be so nervous I won’t be able to speak. Scared of... well, I’m not even sure what.

But that ten-minute conversation with Xiao Wang and the moment I said "Sure"—it feels like something is slowly changing.

I need a good night's sleep. I'll keep thinking about it tomorrow.

July 12, 2023, 7:00 PM

It’s been an hour since my session ended, and I still haven’t settled down.

Dr. Wang’s words keep circling in my head: "Have you ever considered that your father’s silence might not be indifference, but his way of expressing love?"

I’m sitting in my office, staring at the computer screen, my eyes feeling a bit sore. I barely processed anything during this afternoon’s meeting.

I’ve always assumed my father didn’t care about me. This assumption has existed for so many years that I didn’t even realize it *was* an assumption. He rarely initiates conversation; when we see each other, it’s mostly silence. Occasionally he’ll ask, "How’s work?" and that’s the end of it. I interpreted this as coldness. I even felt resentful about it—feeling like an only child who never received enough care.

But... what if his silence isn't indifference?

Dr. Wang said it’s an issue of intergenerational communication. People from my father’s generation learned to express love through caution and silence. Just like my mother, who protects me by telling me to "be careful and stay out of trouble." It’s not that they don’t love me; they just don't know how to say it.

I started thinking back to the details. Whenever I go home, my father prepares my favorite dishes in advance. He asks if I’m working too hard. When I mention something about a colleague, he listens intently and then offers his thoughts. These have always been his ways.

My eyes are getting a little misty.

I realize I might owe them some understanding. Not an apology, but genuine understanding—that they are loving me in the only way they know how, while I’ve been waiting for a kind of love they don't know how to give.

Now, I want to tell them in a more direct way that I understand. I accept them. I also want them to know that I have actually felt their care all along; it just took me this long to see it clearly.

When I go home next week, I want to take the initiative to talk to my father. Not the usual silence over a meal, but a real conversation. I want to tell him that I hear his care.

It’s a strange feeling. I feel a sense of relief, but also a bit of heartache—why did it have to take me so many years to understand?

But I suppose it’s not too late to understand now.

I went back home this weekend to accompany my father for a check-up. It turns out he has a lumbar disc herniation, and the doctor said he’ll need some time for physical therapy and recovery.

Watching my father in the hospital corridor, it hit me that he really has grown old. My mother was constantly asking the doctor questions while I took notes—the doctor’s advice, medication names, follow-up dates. It felt like only by writing everything down could I feel a bit more at ease.

I might have to adjust my work schedule in the coming days. But that’s okay; some things are just more important than work.

I hope Dad gets better soon.

I looked at the time on my phone—11:30 PM. The office lights had been off for ages; only my screen was still glowing.

I hesitated for a long time, but in the end, I didn't post it.

I typed out a few openings, only to delete them one by one. "Lately, I’ve been thinking..." No, too blunt. "Five years into the job..." No, that’s just asking for people to follow up with "why."

What I actually wanted to say was that Dr. Wang’s question has been circling in my head: "If you weren't chasing a promotion, would your feelings about work change?"

I had never thought to ask myself that.

But now that I have, I can't stop.

In the end, I just opened the camera and took a photo of a corner of my desk—the cup of tea that had already gone cold, the sticky notes beside it, and the night view outside the window.

I spent a long time thinking about the caption, but eventually, I deleted that too.

Some things just can't be put into words yet.

Or rather, I’m not ready for others to see that I’m even thinking about them.

Let it be. Phone down. Tomorrow goes on.

August 5, 2023, past 11 PM

I decided to write this down after all. Otherwise, these thoughts will just keep spinning in my head, and I’ll be even more exhausted tomorrow.

My parents came over today. I’d been preparing since Wednesday—buying groceries, scrubbing every corner of the house, and watching Japanese cooking videos over and over. The first thing Mom said when she walked in was, "You put so much heart into this." There was a warmth in her voice I’d never heard before. I was in the kitchen at the time, holding a plate of freshly sautéed eggplant, and I suddenly felt like crying.

What struck me most was Dad. He stood at the kitchen doorway for a while, watching me plate the food with a strange softness in his eyes. I’ve never seen him look at me like that. He asked, "Is this something you’ve been learning lately?" and I said, "Yeah, I wanted you guys to try it." As I said those words, I realized I’d never shared my interests so openly before. I used to feel that these things—reading, cooking, photography—were just self-indulgent hobbies, not worth showing. But today, I just wanted them to see.

During dinner, Mom said some things about her own life choices and why she’s always been so cautious. She said, "I actually thought about taking other paths, but I didn't have the courage." Sitting there listening, it suddenly clicked—her constant lectures on being "careful" weren't meant to limit me; it was the only way she knew how to protect me. She hoped I wouldn't take the risks she never dared to take. It wasn't a lack of love; it was love itself.

I thought back to all those years of resentment—resenting her for always opposing me, for not supporting my risks. Now I realize she was always there. I just didn't see it.

The hardest part is realizing that my understanding of them has never been deep enough. I treated them as a responsibility—"I'm an only child, I have to take care of them"—rather than truly seeing them or understanding them as people. This afternoon, for the first time, I proactively created a space of my own and invited them in. It wasn't a passive "going home"; it was an active "come to my place." This is a huge shift for me.

Before leaving, Dad lingered at the door for a moment and patted me on the shoulder. He didn't say anything, but I could feel something.

I’m sitting on the sofa now. The kitchen is still a bit of a mess, but I don’t want to clean it up just yet. I want to let this afternoon linger a little longer.

I’m starting to believe that maybe we can interact in a more authentic way. Not that stiff, distant, duty-bound way, but truly seeing one another.

The thought makes me feel both guilty and relieved.

Sister Li, are you there?

I was a bit stunned, so she explained that all the demands she’s made of me over the years, all her cautious guidance, were just because she wanted me to be safe. She said she’s been waiting for me to understand that. We talked for nearly an hour; we’ve never done that before.

You know, I feel like this has something to do with you. Not that you did anything directly, but—you showed me a different way of connecting with people. You didn't demand that I change; you just invited me in and accepted me. And then I realized that my mom is actually the same way. She doesn't want me to become someone else; she just wants me to be okay.

I’m feeling a bit overwhelmed with emotion right now. It’s like so many things have suddenly clicked into place.

Thank you, Sister Li. Truly.

# February 25, 2022, 2:30 PM

Another afternoon. The office is quiet; everyone is busy with their own things. I opened the psychological counseling page.

I don’t know how many times I’ve opened it now.

Dr. Wang’s profile is just sitting there. I look at her photo; she looks very gentle. "Finding inner stability amidst uncertainty"—I read that sentence five times. Every time, it felt like she was talking about me. Talking about how I am right now.

But I didn't click "Book."

I closed the page, then opened it again. Closed it, opened it. I did this back and forth for about forty minutes. A colleague asked if I wanted to go downstairs for coffee, and I said no, I have work to do. Actually, I wasn't doing anything; I was just refreshing the screen.

In the end, I clicked it. The booking button. My finger hovered over the screen for a long time before pressing down.

The moment I pressed it, I regretted it.

What am I thinking? Do I really need counseling? Isn't this a bit too... dramatic? I’ve just been under a lot of pressure lately, not sleeping well, feeling a bit anxious. That’s normal. Everyone gets anxious. Why see a doctor?

But I also know this isn't just a "lately" thing.

Last night, I checked my pulse again. I used a phone app. Measured it over and over. My heart rate was a bit fast. I started wondering if there’s something wrong with my heart. Then I thought of my dad. He’s started having palpitations, too. Do we have a family history? Could it be...

I stopped that thought.

Now the request has been sent. I don’t know when I’ll get a reply. I’m scared she’ll reply, and I’m scared she won’t.

What am I doing? What am I actually doing?

It seems I’ve finally admitted it. I need help. Just the realization itself makes me feel exhausted. It’s as if all these years of self-control, self-planning, self-restraint... have all failed. All those plans, the insurance, the financial management... none of it changes anything. People still get old. Parents still get sick. And I’m still sitting here alone in a corner of the office, checking my heartbeat over and over.

I don’t even know when I became like this.

Or maybe I’ve always been like this, and I just can't stand to look at it anymore.

I closed the app. Closed the booking page. I looked out the window. The weather is nice, but I don’t feel like going for a walk. I just want to sit here.

Maybe Dr. Wang really can help me. Maybe she can tell me how I’m supposed to live in this world full of uncertainty.

Or, she might tell me that I can’t change anything at all, and I just need to learn to accept it.

I don’t know which is more terrifying.

July 13, 2022. Night.

When I was on the phone with Mom, she asked me how I’ve been lately. I told her I’m fine, just a little tired.

She could tell. I felt her pause for a second on the other end before she asked, "Are you just too busy with work?" I said no, it’s just how things are. She asked again, and my answers grew even shorter. Finally, she said, "Well, make sure you get some rest." I said okay, and then we hung up.

I didn't ask how Dad was doing like I usually do. I didn't mention my plans to go home next month. I didn't even stay on for one more sentence.

Sitting on the sofa now, I’m thinking—she will never understand me. Not because she doesn't want to, but because I simply have no way of making anyone understand. I can’t even voice the basic truth of "I’m really not okay right now"; I can only brush it off with the lie of being "a little tired." It’s another escape. Another failure.

The expired food is still sitting in the fridge. The book on my nightstand has been stuck on Chapter Three for over a month. The list of courses on my phone—the ones I bought, downloaded, and swore I’d finish—not a single one has been stuck with. These things feel like they’re mocking me—*Look at you, failing again. You’re always like this.*

If I can’t even manage a normal conversation with my own mother, how can I expect to change myself? How can I expect a breakthrough at work? How can I expect anyone to truly love me?

Maybe this is just who I am. Someone who can’t follow through, can’t change, and can’t be understood by anyone. Going five years without a promotion wasn't a coincidence. The failure of that relationship wasn't a coincidence. None of this is a coincidence. This is the real me.

I don’t even know how to explain it to Mom. She sensed something was wrong, but I have no way of telling her. Because I don’t even know how to say it myself. All I know is that I’m tired—so tired that I don’t want to move, don’t want to talk, don’t want to do anything at all.

But I still have to keep working. I still have to keep pretending everything is fine. I still have to keep failing.

A bit of dust has gathered on the corner of my yoga mat, and it just hit me—I’ve actually stuck with this for nearly two months.

I used to think I was someone who could never follow through on anything; every course, book, or plan I started always ended up abandoned halfway. But this time feels different. Maybe it wasn't me after all—I just hadn't found the right environment.

Sister Li was right: sometimes change isn't that hard. What’s hard is finding a place where you don't have to pretend.

How do they keep going?

And me? I can’t even stick to a meal plan.

It’s not just about the cooking. I suddenly realize this runs through my entire life. Five years without a promotion—because I lack initiative. A three-year relationship ending in a breakup—because I couldn't change myself. All those books and courses I bought—because I couldn't follow through. Even visiting my parents feels like fulfilling an obligation rather than a genuine desire.

Is this just who I am? A mediocre, unchangeable, destined-to-fail person?

Lying in bed, I wonder: if I can’t even commit to making a single meal, how am I supposed to take care of my parents as they age? How can I ever have a breakthrough at work? How can I find someone who truly understands me? All of these things require persistence, change, and doing difficult things. And I can’t even do the simplest ones.

Maybe I should just accept this reality. This is who I am. Incapable of change. Incapable of progress. Incapable of being loved.

That "Failure List" is still open on my phone. I read through it again. Every entry is glowing, as if mocking me.

Should I delete this note? Or should I just leave it there to remind me of who I am?

I don't know.

Right now, I just want to sleep. But I know I’ll have insomnia. I’ll wake up in the middle of the night with these scenes of failure playing on a loop in my head. And then tomorrow, I’ll go to work, keep pretending everything is fine, and keep playing the role of the "stable and reliable" data analyst.

When in reality, I can’t do anything right.

# May Bento Diary · Week 5

Tags: Japanese Cuisine / Home Cooking / Keeping at it

---

It’s Tuesday morning again. When the alarm went off, I was still debating whether I should remake that miso soup from last night.

Last week, I went with tamagoyaki, tonkatsu, and cabbage rolls... This week, I wanted to try something different, but I started hesitating the moment I opened my cookbook. Should I do tempura? Or try teriyaki chicken? In the end, I settled on teriyaki chicken with brown rice and pickled cucumbers. It took about an hour and a half, from prepping the ingredients to packing the box.

A colleague asked me yesterday, "Have you been making all your bentos yourself lately?" I said yes. She said, "That’s amazing, I could never stick with it." I just laughed and said it was nothing, that I just enjoy it. But honestly, I felt a bit... how should I put it? A bit watched. It’s like I can’t just throw a bento together anymore; I have to maintain that image of "persistence."

The teriyaki chicken in today’s box turned out with a nice glaze, so I took a photo and posted it on Douban. I wrote a bunch of notes about heat control and sauce ratios. Only after finishing did I realize I seemed to be repeating things I’ve written before. How many times have I written "simmer over low heat" now...

To be honest, choosing the menu has been a bit exhausting lately. Not the cooking itself, but the deciding. Every week I have to think, "What should I eat this week?" flipping through recipes, reading reviews, comparing difficulty levels... Sometimes I just want to stir-fry some greens and call it a day. But then I feel like that would be doing a disservice to my plan.

By the way, that third cookbook, *The Soul of Washoku*, is still sitting on the shelf. I’ve flipped through a page or two and haven't touched it since. I feel a bit guilty, but there’s not much I can do—I already have more recipes than I can handle.

Work was incredibly busy today. Two back-to-back meetings in the afternoon meant no time for lunch. My bento sat there until it wasn't fresh anymore, and I ended up just grabbing something from the convenience store to get by. It feels like a waste of my morning efforts, but also... whatever, I guess that’s just life.

I’ve kept this up for five weeks now. Seven weeks left to hit my three-month goal.

Can I keep going? I think so.

# Diary

May 28, 2024, 7:00 PM, On the subway

Just now on the subway, watching the city lights flash past the window, it suddenly hit me that I should write down how I’ve been feeling lately. I’m afraid if I wait a few more days, the feeling will slip away.

May is almost over. What actually happened this month? Nothing particularly major, it seems, yet it feels like everything is quietly shifting.

It started with buying the camera. At the time, I was still overthinking it, worried I was just starting another thing I "probably wouldn't stick with." But this time is different. Maybe it’s because I didn’t buy it to "change myself" this time; I truly just wanted to use it to see the world. When Sister Li and the others saw my photos, their reaction made me realize—I don’t need to become an extrovert to connect with people. I just need to find a way that fits who I am.

It’s not that my personality has changed. I’m still that quiet person who enjoys being alone. But I understand now that solitude and isolation are two different things.

I was a bit nervous when I organized those two photo walks. But surprisingly, I wasn't "forcing a smile." We walked together, looked together, and shot together. No awkward small talk, no forced socializing. Just a few people using their cameras to experience different angles of the same city. It felt right.

I’ve met some new friends, too. Not many, but they’re the right people.

Things at work are changing as well. I used to get hit with sudden anxiety in the middle of the night, wondering why I haven't been promoted after five years and if I’m truly not competitive enough. Now... I still think about it, but the urgency has faded. I’ve realized that my sense of achievement doesn't have to come solely from a promotion. What I’ve learned about photography this month, the breakthroughs in my social life—these things make me feel fulfilled. Work is still work, but it’s no longer my only stage.

Mom asked me what I’ve been up to lately, with that "are you messing around again?" tone in her voice. I didn't rush to explain myself or feel guilty like I used to. I just told her I’m learning photography and organizing some events. She was quiet for a moment and then said, "That’s good." I can feel her starting to understand that I’m not someone who "needs to be fixed."

Right now, I feel energized, but it’s not an anxious energy. It’s... a fulfilled energy? It’s hard to describe. It just feels like life has gained a new dimension.

The subway is almost at my stop. I have to get off now. But I want to remember this moment—this feeling of being both calm and excited. For the first time, I feel like maybe I don’t need to be who everyone expects me to be. I just need to be myself and find a way of living that fits.

It’s actually not that complicated. That’s all.
