# Washoku Notes | Third Attempt at Tamagoyaki

**Date:** 2022.04.15  
**Reference Recipes:** *The Soul of Washoku*, p. 47 + A YouTube video (Japanese Housewife series)

---

I made tamagoyaki today. I say it’s the "third attempt," but I never actually posted the records of my first two failures here because, at the time, I felt that failure itself wasn't worth documenting. Looking back now, though, maybe recording the failures is more important than recording the successes.

**The Process:**

Four eggs, 1/4 tsp salt, 1/2 tsp sugar, 50ml dashi. This time, I paid extra attention to the heat—medium-low, no rushing. My first two tries failed because the heat was too high; the edges of the egg mixture would set while the middle stayed liquid, causing everything to fall apart during the roll.

I took it slow this time. I poured the egg mixture into the heated square pan and waited until it was semi-set before rolling it from one end. I repeated this four times. The final product looked decent; the color was even when sliced, without those burnt yellow marks.

A colleague took a bite and said, "Huh, this one’s different." When I asked how, she said, "It looks more professional." I smiled, but inside I was thinking—professional? I just spent more time on something completely trivial.

---

**Musings:**

Lately, I’ve bought a lot of books on Washoku and even signed up for that online course. The passion I had two weeks ago is now... how should I put it? It’s not fading, exactly; I’m just a bit tired.

Every day when I get home from work and look at the kitchen, then at the course progress stuck at 50% on my phone, I sometimes wonder—why do I have to make a hobby so complicated? The obsession with perfection, the need to document, the urge to prove something.

When my mom asked what I’ve been up to lately, I told her I was learning to cook. She just gave me an "Oh" and didn't say anything else. I know what she was thinking—another three-minute passion. Maybe she’s right.

But I don’t want to admit she’s right. So I keep going. I keep cooking, I keep recording, and I keep telling myself, "This time is different."

The tamagoyaki turned out alright. Next time, I want to try adding some grated mountain yam; I heard it makes it fluffier. But honestly, I don't know when "next time" will be.

---

**Tips:** If you’re struggling with heat control, you can place the pan on a damp towel to cool it down. A perfect dish requires perfect timing, but I never seem to have enough time.

July 21, 2023, Night

My heart is still racing a little.

Around 2:00 PM, my boss messaged me saying he needed an extra data analysis report over the weekend. When I saw the message, my first instinct was to reply, "Sure, I’ll do it." My finger was hovering over the screen, ready to tap. But I stopped.

I thought about my father’s recent health issues and how I had planned to take him to the hospital for a follow-up this weekend. Then the internal struggle began—should I just say yes? Would saying no seem unprofessional? Would he think I’m not proactive enough?

I agonized over it for 15 minutes. Seriously, for 15 minutes, I kept rewriting the draft. Finally, I sent: "I have family commitments this weekend, but I can have the analysis finished by 8:00 AM Monday morning."

My fingers were actually shaking when I hit send.

Then came the refreshing. I kept refreshing the screen, waiting for his reply. I kept thinking: Will I get scolded? Does he secretly have a problem with me and just hasn't shown it? Will I never get promoted again because of this?

When I realized what I was thinking, I actually laughed at myself. These thoughts are so familiar. For five years, I’ve reflexively agreed to overtime, only to spend my weekends feeling anxious over work emails. I never truly asked myself—would he actually say no?

Two minutes later, my boss replied: "No problem. See you Monday morning."

It was that simple.

Sitting there in the office, I suddenly felt like crying. Not because I was sad, but because of... I don't know how to describe it. It was a sense of release. For the first time in five years, I took the initiative to say "no," and I wasn't punished. I wasn't rejected.

I always thought I was the kind of person who "wasn't cut out for change." Zhang Hao says I’m too stubborn; my mom taught me from a young age to be cautious and not cause trouble. And I really believed them—that this is just who I am, and I can't change.

But this afternoon, I did change. Just like that.

Now, sitting at home eating a bowl of homemade tomato and egg noodles, I’m thinking—maybe it wasn't a lack of willpower before. Maybe I was just constantly doing things I didn't want to do. Maybe when I’m truly clear about what I need, taking action isn't that hard.

I need to take care of my father. That is what I truly want. Not because I’m a "good daughter," but because I love him. That reason is clear enough and powerful enough.

I still have to work tomorrow. But it feels different now. Work is no longer this thing that swallows up all my time. It’s just a part of my life. An important part, but not the whole thing.

I also thought of Sister Li. If she hadn't invited me to the yoga group, I might still be at home overthinking these things, never finding the courage to act. Maybe change really does require some external push, but more than that, it requires your own decision.

I did it.

I think I’ll sleep very well tonight.

Sitting here now, as the sky darkens outside the window, I suddenly don't want to ask those questions anymore.

Listening to her ramble on about what he ate this week and how his walking is improving, I realized something: for years, I’ve been promising to take care of them, but I’ve rarely actually been there. These past two months have been different. I’ve gone home every week. I sit by my father’s side; we don’t necessarily talk much, I’m just there. He points at the news on TV, and we watch together. Mom busies herself in the kitchen, and I help her out. It’s simple, but it feels entirely different.

Last week, during a 1-on-1 with my boss, I said things I’d never dared to say before. I told him I didn’t want to work until 10 PM anymore, and that I needed time to care for my family. My palms were sweating as I spoke; I was so nervous. But he just nodded and said, "No problem. You do great work, and these adjustments are perfectly fine." That was it. No dire consequences, no accusations of being "unprofessional." Instead, I felt understood.

Sister Li invited me to a yoga group dinner again recently. My instinct was to decline, but this time I told the truth: "I really enjoy the yoga classes, but social events drain me a bit." She just laughed and said, "Then just come for a little while. If you get tired, head home early—no one will mind." It was that simple. I don’t have to pretend to be a social butterfly; I just have to be honest about who I am.

I’ve been thinking about one question lately: I always thought I needed to change. Change my personality, my work style, my pace of life. But maybe the problem isn't that I need to change; it's that I’ve been busy rejecting myself just to meet other people’s expectations. Being cautious, introverted, and a quiet doer—these aren't flaws; they are who I am. I can use this version of myself to care for my parents, to do my job well, and to build genuine connections with people like Sister Li.

The "career ceiling"? Maybe it’s not a ceiling at all. Maybe I’ve just been using the wrong yardstick to measure my life. Promotions, high salaries, social circles... those aren't what I truly want. What I want is to be able to care for my family, to speak my needs openly, and to find a balance between work and life. And I’m already doing those things.

I no longer need to change myself to prove my worth. I am already enough.

This realization came a bit late, but at least it’s here.

# November 25, 2023, 9:00 PM

It’s been a week since I bought this new diary, but I haven't dared to write in it until now. I kept feeling like I had to write something "important" to be worthy of these pages. Today, I finally decided to just start, right here and now.

This past month, it feels like I’ve been going through a long process of sorting things out. It started as a vague sense of unease, but now, I feel like I’m finally beginning to see things clearly.

I am an introvert. That isn't a flaw. Growing up, my mother taught me to be cautious and to stay out of trouble; for a long time, I thought of this as a limitation. But now I understand—this is who I am. My caution allows me to notice details that others miss, and my introversion allows me to think deeply. Zhang Hao once wanted me to be more extroverted, and I tried, but it wasn't me. I don't need to change my essence to fit someone else’s expectations.

I’ve also thought a lot about my lack of "follow-through." I used to blame myself constantly—why did I buy so many books but never finish them? Why did I sign up for courses only to quit halfway? Yet, I’ve managed to stick with the yoga group. When Sister Li invited me, I joined without a second thought. I realize now that the problem wasn't incompetence; it was the environment. When I have companionship, a stable setting, and don't have to rely solely on willpower to fight against inertia, I can actually do it. This discovery is such a relief. Maybe what I need isn't more willpower, but the right system.

I am a caregiver. As an only child, I carry the responsibility for my parents. It feels heavy, but it also gives me a sense of purpose. I’m starting to understand my father’s introversion and my mother’s cautiousness—they were just living life the only way they knew how. I don’t resent their upbringing as much anymore. Instead, I’m starting to feel grateful that they gave me the capacity for reflection and an independent character.

Regarding work, I’m not as anxious as I used to be. Not getting a promotion for five years once made me feel like a failure. But now I think: maybe a promotion isn't the only measure of a person's worth. My job provides stability; it gives me time to think, to read, and to experience life. That is also a good thing.

And another important discovery—I’m not that lonely. Even though the people in the yoga group aren't close friends, I can connect with them naturally in that environment. I’ve realized I’m not "bad" at socializing; I just need to find the right way to do it. Not all social interactions require me to force a smile or go against my own rhythm.

Sitting here by the window, writing this while looking at the night view outside, I feel very peaceful. It’s not the peace of escapism, but the peace that comes from truly understanding myself.

I accept myself. I believe in myself. I am grateful for every part of who I am.

Dr. Wang, I wanted to say thank you.

Today’s session helped me a lot. When I first sat down, I was actually prepared to be judged, or at least to be told, "This is what you should do." But you didn't do that. You just listened and said, "You can keep going."

That simple sentence made me feel... heard. I don’t know how else to describe it. It was just the feeling of someone actually listening to me instead of evaluating me.

I used to think the problem was simple—that I just lacked execution, that I had no willpower, that I was a failure. But today you helped me see that it’s not that simple. It’s my family background, my self-perception, those external expectations... all these things are tangled together. I had never thought about it that way before.

My state of mind has been really bad these past few days. I haven't been sleeping well, and I can't find any energy for work. But after I got back today, as I was lying in bed, my mind wasn't looping those negative thoughts like it usually does. Instead, I was thinking about the things you said.

Thank you. Truly.

I’ve only recently realized that change doesn’t have to be loud. Sometimes it’s just a random afternoon when a friend says, "Give it a try," and you take a deep breath and say, "Okay." Then you realize the world looks completely different from another perspective.

Grateful for those warm invitations that gave me the courage to try something new. And grateful to myself for finally being willing to say yes.

# April 8, 2024, 11:15 PM

I can’t sleep.

I’ve been lying in bed for half an hour, but my mind is still racing. Sister Li’s reply keeps flashing before my eyes—she said Sunday at 2:00 PM works great, and she even asked if she should invite the others from the yoga group. When I saw that message, I was honestly a bit stunned.

When I sent that invitation earlier, I sat in front of my phone and re-read it over and over. "Is this too formal?" "Will it seem like I’m forcing an event?" "Is the phrasing natural enough?"... I scrutinized it word by word. But this time, I didn't change it again. I just read it once and hit send. No second or third rounds of editing. It was a strange feeling—as if something inside me had finally loosened.

Thinking back now, that tiny detail feels even more significant than the event itself.

I sent Sister Li a list of suggested shooting locations—a few streets in the Old Town, the light and shadows at Riverside Park, and that old wet market. I even wrote down some thematic directions, things like "Urban Details," "Light and Shadow," "The Poetry of the Everyday." I’ve had these ideas before, but I’ve never organized them so systematically, let alone proactively shared them with anyone.

Sister Li said okay, and that she’s looking forward to it.

And now, I can’t sleep.

It’s a bit strange. In the past, I couldn’t sleep because of anxiety—worrying that I wasn’t doing enough or obsessing over some minor detail. But this is different. Is it... excitement? Anticipation? It’s hard to put into words. It’s just this feeling that something good is about to happen, a sort of light, airy sensation in my body.

I remember Zhang Hao once saying I wasn't extroverted enough, that I should be more proactive about joining activities and talking to people. I was really hurt at the time because I felt like I was already trying so hard—why was it still not enough? But now I understand—the "extroversion" he was talking about isn't what I need. I don't need to become someone who loves parties or small talk. I just need to find people like me and connect in a way that feels comfortable.

Sister Li is that kind of person. The yoga group is, too.

Sunday afternoon, we’ll walk through the city together, taking photos and chatting. I’ll see the world through their eyes, and they’ll see what I see. It sounds so simple, but to me, it feels like something has finally clicked into place.

I can do this now. I can make a plan, I can invite people, and I can turn thoughts into action. Not because I’ve suddenly become a different person, but because I’ve finally allowed myself to do things my own way.

It’s quiet outside. Occasionally, a car passes by below. I still can’t sleep, but I’m in no rush. I’ll just let this anticipation keep me company for a while.

I have work tomorrow, but I don’t care. This feeling is worth it.

October 25, 2022, 11:47 PM

I was lying in bed scrolling through my phone when Mom called. She asked how I was doing and if work had been too exhausting lately. Hearing her voice, I suddenly found myself with nothing to say. I told her I was fine, then quickly changed the subject, asking how she and Dad were feeling and if they were eating well.

I haven’t been able to stop thinking about it since I hung up.

Mom said, "Your voice doesn't sound right." I didn't push back because I knew she was right. I could hear how stiff my own voice was, like I was reading a work report. I didn't even have the energy to pretend—to be the person a "good daughter" should be: warm, caring, and full of life. I couldn't do any of it.

She was reaching out to me, and I was dodging her. I didn't even have the courage to tell her how tired and powerless I feel right now. Instead, I was in such a rush to turn the conversation back to her and Dad, as if to say, "Don't worry about me, I'm fine." But we both knew that was a lie.

Now I’m lying here, replaying that conversation over and over in my head. Am I an unfilial daughter? I should have told her the truth. She was worried about me, and I chose to run away. I didn't even give her a sincere response.

But I really don't know how to respond. How am I supposed to tell her that her daughter can't seem to get anything right? No promotion at work, no relationship in sight, I can't even stick to a simple meal plan. Now that she’s retired, I should be the one she can lean on, but I’m on the verge of breaking down myself. How could I possibly take care of them?

I’m an only child. This identity feels like a stone that keeps getting heavier, pressing down on me. I need to be responsible for their future; I need to save money, buy insurance, and plan for their old age. But I haven't even figured out my own life. I don't even know where I’ll be, what I’ll be doing, or who I’ll be with five years from now.

Mom cares about me, yet I keep changing the subject. I ask if she’s eaten, if she’s gone for her walks—anything to avoid talking about myself. Because the moment I open my mouth, I’d have to admit how miserable things are right now. And I don't have the courage to let her down.

But I’ve already let her down. Through my silence, through my avoidance, and through that terrifyingly cold voice of mine.

I am so selfish.

It’s past midnight now. I still can’t sleep.

Maybe it was this afternoon at the office when I got another WeChat from Mom asking when I’m coming home, or maybe it was last night after I finished looking at that estimate for elderly care costs. The numbers in the spreadsheet were clear—annual health insurance for my parents, living expenses, potential nursing costs. I calculated everything meticulously, down to every single item. But after doing the math? I just sat there, feeling like nothing had been solved.

So, I started browsing through therapists.

Dr. Wang’s case studies mentioned career bottlenecks, family pressure, and anxiety symptoms. I read through them one by one, feeling like he was talking about me. It was a strange sensation—feeling seen, yet terrified at the same time.

I clicked on his booking page. There’s an opening on Wednesday night. 450 yuan an hour. If I go twice a month... I opened my bank balance again. I can afford it, but...

Maybe I’m just being too sensitive.

The moment that thought surfaced, I knew I was lying to myself. But I kept the lie going anyway—maybe in a few days, things will get better. Spring is coming, the weather will get warmer, maybe my neck pain will go away, maybe the work pressure will ease up, maybe I won’t worry so much about my parents. Maybe...

I moved my finger away from the screen.

Now I’m sitting in a dark room. Outside the window are the streetlights of the residential complex. I can hear Sister Li and the others laughing downstairs—the yoga group chatting. I didn't go down. I told myself I was too tired, but the truth is, I just didn't want to see people who are happy.

I know this mindset can’t sustain me anymore. I can feel it. Like a dam about to burst, the layer of rationality is getting thinner and thinner. I’ve held on for so long using data, plans, and the lie that "I have everything under control," but now even that is falling apart.

My phone is still beside me. Dr. Wang’s page is still open.

I don’t have the courage to click it. Nor do I have the courage to close it.

Let it be. I’ll deal with it when it’s light out.

# May 15, 2024, 8:15 PM

Something happened today that I still find a little hard to believe.

After yoga class this afternoon, I actually announced my plan for next week’s photo walk in front of everyone. Just like that, I said it out loud. "Urban Botany"—I even gave the event a theme. My heart was racing the moment the words left my mouth, but it wasn't that "bad" kind of nervous. It was... a bit of a thrill?

As soon as I finished, Sister Li said, "That’s wonderful!" and then everyone else started showing interest too. I was going to leave it at that, but then Sister Li suggested I should invite even more people. My gut reaction was, "Oh... I'm not sure..." Just like the old me, I was ready to say no. But I didn't. I paused, looked her in the eye, and said, "Okay, I'll try."

The moment I said those words, I could feel myself crossing a threshold. It wasn't a giant leap, but for me... it was enough.

The thing that struck me most was when a friend from the yoga group said to me, "I'm so glad you're organizing these activities; it makes the group so much more fun."

I was a bit stunned. That feeling of being needed. For so many years, I always felt like the passive one, the person who just got dragged along. But it turns out... I actually have something to offer.

I thought about this the whole way home. I haven't turned into a different person; I’m still the same introverted, overthinking Lin Xiaoyu. But somewhere along the line, I stopped just thinking. I’m doing. I’m taking photos, I’m documenting things, and I’m inviting others to see the world through my eyes.

I used to feel like I lacked the drive to get things done, but now I understand—it wasn't that I couldn't do it, I just needed to find something worth doing, and I needed people who believed I could do it. Sister Li has been doing that all along. So has the yoga group.

I’ll still get nervous. Tomorrow I have to start reaching out to people to invite them for next week, which makes me a little anxious. But this anxiety feels different from before. Back then, I was afraid of failing; now, I’m... looking forward to succeeding?

I can hardly believe I’m writing these words. But that’s exactly how I feel right now.

Onward to tomorrow.

# May 2023: Redefining Success — Checking Progress

**Date:** June 10, 2023  
**Reminder Frequency:** 10th of every month  
**Importance:** ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐

---

## Monthly Self-Reflection

This month flew by. When I wrote this title last time, I was still spiraling over whether my boss would notice my dip in productivity. Looking back now, it doesn't seem that important anymore.

**Three questions I need to ask myself:**

1. **Am I prioritizing my family?**
   - Dad stuck with his physical therapy; this week he said his legs hurt less.
   - Mom’s blood pressure has stabilized. I organized a medication calendar for her, and she’s finding it very easy to use.
   - Going home twice a month has turned into three times. It doesn't feel like a burden; it feels... necessary.

2. **Am I speaking up for myself?**
   - When I mentioned my father’s situation to Sister Li, she didn’t judge me like I thought she would. Instead, she listened intently.
   - During yoga class, I took the initiative to say something to the person next to me. It was just "It’s so hot," but I said it.
   - It’s still hard to speak up in meetings, but I no longer view it as a flaw that must be "fixed."

3. **Do I feel more authentic and accepted?**
   - Yes. When Dad said "thank you," I felt a sense of fulfillment that professional achievements have never given me.
   - Sister Li invited me to go grocery shopping, and I didn't make an excuse to decline. We talked for an hour.
   - I’m starting to understand that maybe I don’t need to become an extrovert to be accepted.

---

## Observations

The work is still getting done, the reports are still being filed, but I no longer see a promotion as the sole marker of success. It sounds like self-consolation, but it really isn't.

I still haven't finished those courses I bought, which makes me feel a bit guilty. But my time is being spent on more urgent matters now. Maybe this isn't failure—just a shift in priorities.

My body is still tired. My neck hurts, and so does my lower back. But I’m starting to seriously consider seeing a doctor instead of procrastinating. I suppose that’s a change, too.

---

## Small Goals for Next Month

- Say at least one thing in a work meeting (without being prompted).
- Do something with Sister Li again.
- Book that appointment for my cervical spine.
- Don’t be so hard on myself.

---

**Notes:** I chose the title of this document intentionally. I am redefining what success means. Perhaps it’s not a promotion or finding the perfect partner, but feeling needed while caring for others, and feeling authentic while being understood.

I’ll review this again on the 10th of next month.

# September Realizations

**Date:** September 28, 2023, 9:30 PM

---

## Redefining Career and Life

Over the past month, I’ve been constantly turning one question over in my mind: What does success actually mean?

The fact that I haven't been promoted in five years used to frustrate me deeply. But I want to write this down now to prevent myself from falling back into that same anxiety during future low points.

**A New Definition of Career Success:**
- It’s not about promotions or titles.
- It’s about stability—being able to support myself and take care of my parents.
- It’s about flexibility—having the time to do what matters instead of being swallowed by work.
- It’s about supporting life—working to live better, not living for the work.

I am content right now. Not a passive kind of contentment, but a clear, rational one. My job provides me with these things.

---

## On the Meaning of Life

I used to always be chasing a finish line—a promotion, getting married, becoming a "certain type" of person. But lately, I’ve started to understand:

**The meaning of life isn't about reaching a specific goal; it’s about the process itself.**

It’s found in:
- Those quiet moments sharing a meal with my father.
- The feeling of being accepted when I share my thoughts in the yoga group.
- The focus and joy of experimenting with a new recipe alone in the kitchen.
- Connections with people who truly understand me.

These things don't have a "completion" date, but they are real.

---

## To My Future Self

If there comes a day when I start feeling anxious again, blaming myself for not getting promoted, I need to come back and read this:

You’ve already figured this out. You already understand what truly matters to you. Career success doesn't have a single definition. For you, it means stability, flexibility, and supporting your life. That is good. That is enough.

You are not a failure. You are consciously choosing your own life.

---

## Recent Optimizations

- Work boundaries are actually working. Emails can wait until tomorrow.
- The "quality time" model with my father is effective. Fixed monthly times, but higher quality.
- The yoga group has given me a space where I can just be myself.
- Still need to think about: What is my long-term career plan? Not about promotions, but what do I truly want?

---

# Weekend Email Check Experiment - Observation Log

**Date**: Friday Evening, July 14, 2023  
**Subject**: Rethinking the Definition of "Urgent"

---

## Experiment Setup
Intentionally avoided checking work emails over the weekend (July 8-9) to observe if any actual consequences would arise.

## Results & Data

| Metric | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Weekend email checks | 0 |
| Emails in inbox Monday morning | 2 |
| Urgent emails among them | 0 |
| Tasks requiring immediate action | 0 |

## Email Breakdown

**Email 1**: Discussion regarding next month's data report format → Can be completed by Tuesday.  
**Email 2**: Colleague inquiring about historical data → Existing analysis documents are already available.

## Observations & Reflections

I used to feel that failing to check emails promptly would lead to major issues. However, this experiment proves that my definition of "urgent" has been far too broad.

Much of the time, I have been **imposing pressure on myself**. Weekends are supposed to be recovery time, yet I found myself constantly refreshing my inbox for fear of missing something. In reality, most work matters can wait until Monday.

## Weekend Highlights

- Watched a full documentary on Saturday (without email interruptions).
- Tried a new Japanese recipe on Sunday and took detailed notes.
- Revived my reading habit; finished a chapter of *Sapiens*.
- Noticed a significant decrease in overall fatigue throughout the weekend.

## Proposed Adjustments

**Trial Period**: Continue execution next week.

1. **Fixed Email Check Windows**
   - 10:00 AM, 3:00 PM, and 4:00 PM (before EOD).
   - Turn off email notifications outside these windows.

2. **Email Prioritization Standards** (To be refined)
   - **Red**: Same-day response required (from supervisors, client complaints).
   - **Yellow**: Response within 48 hours (internal discussions, data requests).
   - **Green**: Process within a week (info sharing, meeting notices).

3. **Weekend Rules**
   - Zero email checking.
   - If a true emergency occurs, my supervisor will call me directly.

## Hypotheses to Validate

- [ ] Can productivity be maintained with fixed check-in times?
- [ ] Are there truly no "urgent" emails being missed?
- [ ] Is this rhythm adjustment sustainable in the long run?

---

**Note**: The sense of recovery this weekend made me realize that perhaps the problem isn't a lack of competence, but rather that I’ve been filling my life with an unnecessary "sense of urgency." Will continue observations next week.

# Thinking is Not a Delay, But a Form of Action

Lately, while reading *Sapiens*, one particular idea has been circling in my mind.

The book explains that humans are able to cooperate and innovate because we can imagine things that don't exist. This ability sounds abstract, but I think it’s actually quite concrete—it’s our capacity to "run through" a scenario in our heads first to see what might happen.

A small thing happened during a meeting a few days ago. My boss asked a question about a data model, and several ideas flashed through my mind instantly. My gut reaction was that familiar habit—to pause, listen to others, and overthink it internally. But this time, I didn't let that habit win. I paused for three seconds, and then I spoke up.

What I said isn't the point. What matters is that while I was speaking, I realized those three seconds of silence weren't "procrastination"—they were thinking. I was rapidly scanning the logic, weighing different ways to phrase it, and imagining how others might react. That process wasn't a waste of time; it was an optimization of what was about to be said.

I’ve always felt I lacked "execution power" because I overthink everything. But lately, I’ve started to doubt that conclusion. Maybe the problem isn't that I think too much, but that I haven't found the answer to "why I'm doing it" in the first place.

It’s just like cooking. When I’m home alone, I procrastinate; I think, "It’s just for me, I’ll just throw something together." But when I’m cooking for friends, my brain goes into overdrive—thinking about ingredient pairings, heat control, and how to present the dish so everyone enjoys it. That "thinking" isn't a delay; it’s creativity.

Maybe I’ve had it backwards all along. I don’t need to become a "do first, think later" person. I just need to find a goal that is worth my thinking.

I recently saw a discussion on Douban about "finding your voice." I used to think that sounded a bit airy-fairy. But now I feel that perhaps your "voice" is simply your unique way of thinking. Some people’s voices are fast and direct; others are slow and deliberate. Neither is a problem. The real question is whether you dare to let your voice be heard.

In that meeting the other day, I dared to do it once.

As I write this, I’m thinking that maybe the next question isn't "How should I change myself?" but rather "How can I accept myself, and then act from that foundation?"

That thought makes me feel a little lighter.

March 22, 2023, Night

Just got off the phone with Mom. I originally intended to just give her a quick update on my life, but we ended up talking for nearly forty minutes.

I’m not sure why I brought up Sister Li and the yoga group on my own. Maybe it’s because I went to two more classes this week and felt pretty good about it? I described the people in the group in detail, told her how Sister Li took the initiative to invite me, and how it felt to practice yoga together that day. Mom listened intently and asked a lot of questions—since when did she become so interested?

Then she said that line I’ve heard a thousand times: "You should socialize more."

But this time, I didn't feel that familiar pressure. There was no sense of being judged or being told to change. Instead... I think I understood what she meant. She wasn't saying there’s something wrong with me; she just doesn't want me to be so lonely.

I told her, "Mom, I’m doing that right now." She laughed.

After hanging up, I sat for a long while thinking about our conversation. For five years, every time she mentioned socializing, friends, or how I should get out more, I would instinctively get defensive. It felt like she was accusing me of not being good enough. But maybe... maybe she was just expressing care?

What’s even stranger is the sudden realization that the changes I’m making now aren't to meet her expectations. I actually *want* to go to yoga. I actually *enjoy* chatting with Sister Li. I’m not forcing myself to become an extrovert; I’m just doing what I truly want to do.

So maybe the gap in understanding between my mother and me isn't actually that wide. I am changing, but not in the way she demanded. It’s the change I needed for myself. And she saw that.

It’s a strange feeling. Almost like... being seen?

Back to work tomorrow. Yoga class on Wednesday.

Sometimes, understanding hits you all at once.

It was something my mom said today that I’ve been replaying in my head all afternoon. "You seem more like an adult now than you ever did before." Such a simple sentence, yet it made me want to cry.

These past two months have been spent shuttling between home and the hospital—managing my dad’s medication schedule, accompanying him to physical therapy, and carrying some of my mom’s worries. I thought I was just doing "what needed to be done." But perhaps, simply being seen has already changed something.

There’s still some of the Japanese soup I made yesterday in the kitchen. Tomorrow, we keep going.

A thought just crossed my mind here in the office. I’m jotting it down quickly before I get buried in emails.

Sticking with the yoga group over the past few weeks has made me wonder: **Is it really that I’m incapable of change, or was my previous environment simply the wrong fit?**

Looking back at my past self—all those online courses I signed up for and the stacks of books I bought, only to abandon them halfway. At the time, I blamed it on a lack of willpower and felt so guilty about it. But now, in this yoga group, I’ve actually kept it up. It’s not because I’ve suddenly become a person of iron will; it’s because:

- No one is judging me.
- There is no atmosphere of competition.
- Sister Li’s invitation made me feel welcomed rather than coerced.
- The physical improvements have given me a reason to keep going.

Over these three months, I’ve gone from participating passively to actually looking forward to Tuesdays and Thursdays. The shift is subtle, but it’s real.

**Points for Reflection:**
- What is the fundamental difference between the work environment and the yoga group?
  - Work: Target-driven pressure, evaluations, and comparisons.
  - Yoga: Purely personal improvement and a sense of being accepted.
  
- Is my "stagnation" at work truly a lack of ability? Or does the environment itself fail to motivate me?
  - My boss trusts my professional skills.
  - But no one has ever asked me what I actually want.
  - And I’ve never spoken up about it, either.

- If the key to change is the environment, should I consider changing my work environment? Or should I change my mindset toward work?

This is a big thought. I need to talk it over with Dr. Wang. But at least now I know that maybe the problem isn't that I’m "flawed"—it’s just that I’ve been pushing in the wrong direction.

July 25th, 9:00 PM

I dug out a notebook I haven't used in ages; the fountain pen nib was a bit dry. On the first page, I wrote: "Records of July."

I’m not quite sure where to start. I sat for a while; the night view outside the window is very quiet.

What has happened this past month? Or rather, what has happened to me?

Sister Li said I’ve changed. Not my appearance or the way I dress, but the look in my eyes. She said I look "alive" now. I felt a bit awkward at the time, but after turning that sentence over in my mind, I realized it struck a chord.

I’ve stuck with the yoga group for almost two months now. For me, this is a miracle. I’ve always seen myself as someone lacking follow-through—books I buy never get finished, and courses I sign up for are abandoned halfway. But this time is different. Every Wednesday night, I proactively change into my workout gear, not because I "should" go, but because I want to. This feeling is very unfamiliar.

What’s even stranger is that on the yoga mat, I’m not as tense anymore. When I can’t nail a certain pose, I don’t beat myself up; instead, I laugh. Once, I messed up a posture and the person next to me laughed too. We caught each other's eye and both relaxed. In that moment, I realized that maybe I’ve always made social interaction out to be more complicated than it is.

I stop writing and look out the window.

If I can be this relaxed and confident in the yoga group, why can’t I be like this elsewhere?

This question has been circling in my head. At work, I still stay silent during meetings. But recently, when a colleague asked for my opinion on a data model, I didn't hesitate as usual—I just said it. It was only after I finished that I realized what I said wasn't particularly special, but I said it. That little thing stayed on my mind for a long time.

Maybe the problem isn't that I’m incapable of change, but that I’ve been in the wrong environments. Or rather, I need to find the "right" environment for me. In the yoga group, no one expects me to be someone else. Sister Li never says things like "you should be more outgoing." She just accepts me, the same way she accepts herself.

I think back to the things Zhang Hao used to say. Back then, I felt like there was something wrong with me, that my personality was what limited us. But now I think, maybe the problem wasn't who I was, but that both he and I were trying to change each other.

I’ve discovered that I’m not someone who is bad at socializing. I just need to find the right environment and people who truly accept me.

I need to write that down.

My neck has been feeling much better lately. Maybe it’s the yoga, or maybe it’s just because I’m in a better mood. The connection between the body and the mind is tighter than I imagined.

I’m going home for a visit next week. I want to cook something for my parents. I’ve been learning Japanese cuisine lately and want to see if I can pull it off. Mom will probably say, "Why are you fussing over something new again?" but I’m not afraid of her saying that anymore. Because this time, I know I’ll stick with it.

I’ve proven to myself that I am capable of persistence.

I put the pen down and look at these lines. I’m a bit tired, but it’s a grounded, solid kind of tired.

To be continued tomorrow.

January 16, 2023. Late night, 23:47.

Another sleepless night.

I’ve been lying in bed for an hour now. The light from my phone screen is making my eyes sting, but I still can’t put it down. There are seven or eight tabs open in my browser—all profiles of therapists. Dr. Wang, Dr. Li, Dr. Zhang… I’ve even screenshotted their introduction pages and made a comparison chart. How ridiculous. What am I doing? Choosing a therapist like I’m shopping for a product?

But I just can’t stop.

Ever since Sister Li asked me last week, "You look really tired lately," it felt like something inside me was punctured. She said it so casually, but that sentence has been playing on a loop in my head. Yes, I am tired. I haven’t had a good night’s sleep in a long time. Work, parents, age, relationships… everything is piling up, pressing down on me until I can’t breathe.

I never thought about going to therapy. It feels like admitting defeat—admitting that I can’t handle my own problems. I’ve always felt it was a bit shameful. Who goes to a stranger to pour their heart out about these things? I should be able to fix this myself.

But… clearly, I haven’t.

I’ve bought so many books, signed up for so many courses, and written countless plans. And in the end? Nothing was accomplished. I don’t even know what I actually want. A promotion? Not sure. Marriage? I’m scared. Change? I crave it, but at the same time, I’m terrified of it.

So I started searching. First, "how to deal with career stagnation," then "anxiety of unmarried women at 35," and then finally, "psychological counseling." I spent two hours comparing different therapists. Looking at their educational backgrounds, counseling styles, user reviews… I even looked at their photos, trying to judge from a face whether a person is trustworthy. How foolish.

There was a sentence in Dr. Wang’s profile that struck me: "I believe everyone has the capacity for change; sometimes, they just need a safe space to explore."

A safe space. How long has it been since I felt something like that?

I listed the questions I want to ask him in my Notes app. The first is, "Why do I always feel so exhausted?" The second is, "How can I become more extroverted?" The third is, "Do I really need help?"

Then, I changed the question mark of that last one into an exclamation point.

I don’t know why I did it. Maybe it’s because, in the middle of the night, my defenses have dropped. Maybe it’s because I’m too tired to keep lying to myself. Maybe it’s because… I really do need help.

Admitting this feels like crying.

I’m not crying. I’m just… tired. So tired that I want someone to listen to me. Not the "you should do this" kind of advice, but real listening. The way Sister Li did.

I’ll make an appointment tomorrow. I’ve decided. Maybe it’s a waste of time and money, maybe it won’t change anything. But at least… at least I’m trying. At least I’m admitting that I might need help.

For me, that’s already a huge step.

Time to put the phone down. Work tomorrow.

I was a bit surprised at myself after I said it. Usually, I’m not the type to offer help so proactively. But in that moment, I just felt like... I can do this, and I want to do it.

I was chatting with a friend at a cafe this afternoon when she suddenly started opening up about some work troubles. Without thinking much, I just applied my usual data-analysis mindset to help her organize her thoughts—breaking down the problems, mapping out a few potential directions, and weighing which one was the most feasible.

After listening, she said, "It’s so much clearer now that you’ve put it that way."

Sitting there, it suddenly hit me that maybe I’ve been underestimating what I’m capable of. You don’t have to be standing on a stage or acting as a leader to have value. Sometimes, just listening quietly and speaking your truth is enough.

In that moment, I think I finally understood what it meant.

January 25, 2022, 11:00 PM

I’ve been lying in bed for nearly an hour, but my mind is still a mess.

What happened in the breakroom this afternoon keeps playing on a loop. I honestly don’t know how I let it slip—"I might have to cover my parents' medical expenses." The moment the words left my mouth, I felt it: that cold, sinking regret spreading from my chest. My colleague just gave a flat "Oh" and went back to talking about her kid, but I could feel something behind that "Oh." Is she thinking there’s something wrong with my family? Is she going to tell people behind my back that I’m being dragged down by my home life?

Why did I say it? I know better than to talk about personal stuff at the office. My mother taught me from a young age to be cautious, to keep family matters private, and to never be a burden to others. I’ve managed to keep my guard up at work for years; why did I lose it today?

Now I’m starting to worry about how this might affect my boss’s perception of me. He’s always trusted my professionalism, but what if he hears I’m distracted by family issues? I haven't been promoted in five years; will this "negative information" make him think I’m unstable? I was already feeling insecure about the lack of promotion, and now this just makes it worse.

But honestly, I don’t even know what I’m afraid of. Being judged? Or the fear that I really can’t handle it?

Ever since my parents retired, this responsibility has felt like a sudden weight dropped on my shoulders. I’ve started obsessing over insurance, financial planning, and cost data—trying to use numbers to control the fear. But the more I learn, the more terrified I get. What if something really goes wrong with my father’s leg? What about that tremor in my mother’s finger? I am fundamentally unprepared to face a sick parent.

I haven’t even had a real conversation with them about any of this. Every time I go home for dinner, I just observe them in silence—my father’s gait, my mother’s hands, the speed at which they eat—but I never actually ask, "How are you?" It’s like I’m gathering evidence to prove they are aging, to prove how heavy my burden is.

It’s so selfish.

And now the guilt is setting in again. Why am I always like this? I blame myself when I do something wrong, and I blame myself even when I do something right. I’m guilty because I said something I shouldn't have, and I’m guilty because I don’t care for my parents enough. Both things are eating me alive.

I have work tomorrow. I’ll sit at my desk just like any other day, pretending nothing happened. But I know I’ll keep thinking about that one sentence, replay every possible consequence in my head over and over. Is this my fate? To always overthink, to always live in regret.

Whatever. I need to stop thinking. Tomorrow is another day.

March 1, 2023, 7:30 PM

I just got out of the shower. My neck still feels a bit sore and tender, but it’s not that painful kind of ache—it’s more like the relaxation you feel after being massaged out. Sitting here on the edge of the bed, I’m thinking back to those moments on the yoga mat two hours ago, and I still feel a bit embarrassed.

Sister Li was right; there really is a yoga group in the neighborhood. For the 3:30 PM class, I arrived ten minutes early. There were already five or six people spreading out their mats. I pretended to be very focused on tying my shoelaces, but I was actually observing everyone else. One older lady looked even more nervous than I was, which made me relax a little.

But once we actually started the poses, that sense of powerlessness hit me again. When the instructor said, "Fold forward and bring your forehead to your knees," my body simply wouldn't cooperate. I could feel the muscles in the back of my legs as tight as steel bars, and my spine was protesting. The lady next to me did it effortlessly, and I wondered if she was watching me... I know, no one was actually looking, but I couldn't help feeling that way.

The most awkward moment was during a twisting pose when my neck let out a very audible "crack." At that moment, I really wanted to just walk out and make an excuse about not feeling well. But I didn't. For some reason, I pushed through and finished the whole class. Maybe it was because I was already there, maybe I didn't want to let Sister Li down, or maybe it was just that in that moment, I decided to stop being ashamed of my body—at least while on this yoga mat.

The strangest thing is that the neck pain that has been bothering me for weeks has noticeably eased up since the class ended. I can turn my head; I can feel my shoulders relaxing. It’s not psychological; it’s a real, physical improvement.

But I wonder, is this just temporary? Will the pain come back tomorrow?

And... will I keep going? Sister Li mentioned there’s another class next week and asked if I wanted to go together. I didn’t give her a straight "yes," I just said, "Maybe, we’ll see." That’s my usual move—leaving myself an out. But this time, I think I actually want to go again. The physical improvement is one reason, but not the only one. There’s something else, though I can’t quite put my finger on it yet.

I just... I hope I can stick with it this time. I don't want it to be like those books I bought or the courses I signed up for that ended up half-finished. But I’m also afraid to expect too much of myself, because the feeling of disappointment is all too familiar.

Whatever, I shouldn't overthink it. For now, my neck feels better, and that’s enough.

I’ve been reading *Sapiens* lately, and one passage has really stayed with me:

"The tragedy of the human condition is not our limitations, but our constant attempt to transcend where we were never meant to."

A couple of days ago, my boss pulled me aside to talk about leading a team for a new project. Instead of feeling an immediate rush of excitement, I felt calm. I started asking myself a lot of questions: Do I really want this? What are my actual strengths? What are my limitations?

In the end, I told him I’d rather stay in my current role. He seemed a bit surprised, but he understood.

This isn't about giving up. I think I finally realize that accepting yourself isn't a compromise—it’s honesty. I don’t need to be everything to everyone; I just need to do what I’m good at, and do it well.

Sometimes the best change isn't about changing who you are, but about finally stopping the self-denial.

July 21, 2022 Afternoon

After Sister Li left, I sat on the sofa for a long time without moving.

The apartment is still a mess. I tried to tidy up earlier, but eventually gave up. Books are piled in the corner, and the ingredients from yesterday’s failed attempt at Japanese cooking are still in the kitchen bin—that fish... I didn't even have the courage to properly dispose of it. Then there are those course notebooks; I bought them with such high hopes, and now they just feel like they’re mocking me.

The hardest part to accept is that Sister Li saw all of this.

When she knocked, I thought about not opening the door. But I knew she had already heard me crying. I remember the moment she walked in—the way her eyes swept across the room and then settled on me. I could feel her looking at me—really looking at me, not that polite, forgettable kind of glance. My face must have looked terrible. I don’t even dare to look at myself in the mirror.

I don’t know how I started talking. Maybe I was just too tired—too exhausted to keep up the act. I poured everything out: the failed cooking, the lack of progress at work, the unfinished courses, being thirty-five and still alone, feeling like a completely useless person... Towards the end, I even found myself wondering if everyone would be better off if I just disappeared.

I felt a pang of fear when I said that. Fear that Sister Li would think I was crazy, fear that such a thought might actually turn into something real. But she didn't. She just sat there, listening intently. She said, "I hear you."

Such a simple sentence, yet it made me cry even harder.

Now that she’s gone, I’m sitting here alone, feeling even more empty. It’s that post-confession emptiness. It feels like I’ve hollowed myself out, and now there’s nothing left but an empty shell. I wonder, is this a release? Or am I just further confirming what a failure I am?

Dr. Wang mentioned before that I should consider counseling. At the time, I thought it was a sign of weakness. Now, I’m not so sure. Maybe it’s not weakness. Maybe it’s... I don’t know how to put it. Courage?

But courage feels so foreign to me.

My head is a mess right now. Part of me feels seen, a bit understood. The other part of me is blaming myself—why did I let someone see me in such a pathetic state? Why couldn't I just keep hiding? Why can't I do anything right?

I want to take a shower, but I don't even have the strength to stand up.

I have work tomorrow.

Sister Li, I’ve been thinking about what we discussed this afternoon. 😅

To be honest, when you said "I'll help you," I was actually a bit startled. Not in a bad way, just... it suddenly felt like this might actually happen? I thought I was just talking out loud at first, and I didn't expect you to take it so seriously.

I might need a bit more time to figure out the specific schedule and route, but I think I’m serious about this. I really appreciate it, Sister Li. Not just for the help, but more for the fact that you believe I can actually do it.

Sister Li, are you there?

I... wanted to tell you something. It might sound a bit silly, but my head is still spinning a little right now.

A colleague came up to me this afternoon and asked if I could present a data analysis case study at next week’s team sharing session. My first instinct was to say no—you know me, I’ve never been brave enough to speak in front of everyone. But this time... I didn’t immediately turn it down. I asked about the details, how long it would be, and how many people would be attending.

And then... I said yes.

Now, sitting here in the office, I’m feeling a mix of regret and... I don’t even know how to describe it. Nervous, definitely. But it’s not entirely a bad feeling? It’s almost like a bit of excitement? I’m not sure.

I keep thinking this might have been a really stupid decision. I’m certain my voice is going to shake from nerves. But Sister Li, I remembered what you said before—that sometimes you just have to say yes first, and only then can you see what you’re truly capable of. The fact that I didn’t automatically refuse this time... it feels like a big deal for me.

Do you think I did the right thing? Was I being too impulsive?

It’s next Tuesday afternoon. Oh my god, just thinking about it makes me want to run away.

But... thank you for being there for me lately. Truly. If it weren't for our yoga classes and our chats, I probably would have already messaged my colleague saying, "Sorry, I can't do it."

These choices might look like "missing out" on something, but they are actually about "possessing" something.

Possessing my own rhythm. Possessing authentic relationships. Possessing inner peace.

Courage isn't always about changing yourself to fit the world. Sometimes, courage is simply accepting who you are and having the conviction to live that way.

—I need to remember this. Next time I start blaming myself, I’ll come back and read this.

I just got back from the office, and my heart is still racing a little.

I even said it clearly: I don’t need a promotion; I just want to do this job well.

My colleagues looked at me a bit differently. After the meeting, someone asked me what’s been going on lately, saying I seemed different. I just smiled and told her I’ve started to understand myself and accept who I am. She nodded and didn't pry, but I could tell she understood something.

Sitting on the subway, I caught myself thinking—what would the old me have done? The old me would have agonized over it, worrying if I was being "unambitious," or if I should be fighting for a leadership role just to prove a point. Or, I would have said nothing at all, remaining that reliable but invisible person.

But now I know that all that anxiety wasn't real. The real me just wants to do good data analysis, have time to spend with my parents, cook with Sister Li, keep up with yoga, and learn photography. I don’t need to be "successful" in other people's eyes. I just need to be someone who lives authentically in my own.

It might sound simple, but it took me so long to realize this.

Dr. Wang once asked me: "If you didn't have to prove yourself to anyone, what kind of life would you want?" I didn't have an answer then, but the question stayed with me. This afternoon, I feel like I finally gave an answer. Not with words, but with action.

I’ve found a way to contribute while staying true to myself.

It feels really good.

# Tempura | A Little Surprise in Week 3

**Date:** April 8, 2022
**Reference:** *The Complete Guide to Japanese Home Cooking*, Page 47
**Difficulty:** ★★★☆☆

---

During lunch today, my colleague Li Xiao took the initiative to ask me, "What new dish did you make this week?" To be honest, I was a bit surprised at that moment—I never thought anyone would actually care about what I was cooking.

This week, I chose Tempura. I’d been wanting to try it for the past two weeks, but I kept overthinking the size of the shrimp and the consistency of the batter. Yesterday, I finally made up my mind and bought the ingredients.

**Ingredients:**
- 8 large shrimp (shelled and deveined—this step is crucial)
- Pumpkin, eggplant, and green pepper (as needed)
- 100g low-gluten flour
- 1 egg
- 120ml ice water
- A pinch of salt and black pepper

**Cooking Notes:**

The batter is the part that requires the most attention. The book says to use ice water, so I specifically put some in the fridge the night before. You shouldn't overmix the batter; it needs to keep a slightly lumpy, coarse texture to ensure it turns out crispy. I read that section three times before I finally started.

The shrimp must be completely dried; I spent quite a while pressing them with paper towels. I kept the oil temperature around 170°C, which I verified with a thermometer. I made sure to space out the timing of each shrimp going into the pot so they could be taken out at the same time.

The vegetables were cut a bit too thick; I’ll need to slice them thinner next time. I managed the heat for the pumpkin well, though—it was soft to the bite but still held its shape.

**I took a few photos. Even though phone pictures aren't as good as a professional camera, they capture how it looked at the time.**

---

It’s a bit embarrassing to admit, but I’m already on the third lesson of that online course I bought. Usually, I struggle to stick with things, but this time feels different. Maybe it’s because knowing someone cares gives me more motivation to keep going.

Next week, I want to try Tendon (Tempura Rice Bowl). I’ve already added the recipe to my favorites.

# 2024.04.18 10:30 PM

Sitting at my desk, my hands are still shaking a little.

The email is out. I hit send three hours ago. There’s no turning back now.

I don’t know how to describe this feeling. It’s a bit like standing on the edge of a cliff, knowing you have to jump, but your feet keep instinctively pulling back. In the end, I jumped anyway. Or rather, I pushed myself off.

During those three hours, I rewrote that one sentence five times. "I’d like to invite everyone to a photo walk"—was there anything wrong with that? No. But I changed it anyway. I changed it to "We could try using our cameras to capture the beauty around us," but that felt too subjective. I changed it to "If anyone is interested, I’d like to organize a photo walk," but that felt too passive, like I was begging for permission.

My mother’s voice echoed in my head: "Be careful, don't be too forward." I know it’s actually my own voice, just wearing her clothes.

But I sent it anyway. The final version was: "I’ve been learning photography lately and really want to share this new discovery with you all. If anyone is interested next weekend, I can organize a little photo walk. No pressure, just a chance to see the world together."

The moment I clicked send, my heart was racing. It felt like I was doing something dangerous. But there’s no real danger, is there? It’s just inviting a few friends from the yoga group for a walk. Sister Li has been telling me for ages that I should share more of my own things.

Now the email is just sitting there. Maybe someone will reply, maybe no one will. But at least I did it. I finally did something I’ve been avoiding for a long time.

I used to think I just had poor execution—I’ve bought so many courses and never finished them. But now I’m wondering if it wasn't an execution problem after all. Maybe I just hadn't found something I truly wanted to do. Photography is different. I really want to do this. I really want to share it.

Even though I’m still feeling a bit of regret and anxiety in my chest, I also feel a little... proud? It’s a strange word to use, but that’s the feeling.

I did it.

I’ll check for replies tomorrow. For tonight, I’ll just let this feeling sit with me for a while.

November 16, 2022, 9:45 PM

I just did something very strange.

I searched for "psychological counselors." Then I sat there staring at my phone screen, clicking in, backing out, and clicking in again. I kept asking myself: Do I really need this? I’ve just been a bit tired and irritable lately; it’s not that big of a deal. I can handle it on my own.

But I haven't handled it. It’s been months.

I looked through a lot of profiles. Some counselors are so expensive—hundreds of yuan for just one hour. I thought, how could I spend that much money just to talk about my problems? It feels so self-indulgent. What would my mom think? What if my colleagues found out? Would they think I’m "mentally ill"?

I lay in bed, staring at the ceiling, asking myself these questions over and over.

Then I saw Dr. Wang’s profile. Her bio mentioned things like "acceptance-based therapy" and "non-judgmental." I don’t know why, but when I saw those words, my eyes suddenly welled up. I didn't even realize I was crying.

I thought, just this once. Just this once, I’ll try.

My finger hovered over the call button for a long time. A really long time. I could feel my heart racing, and there was a lump in my throat. I wondered, if she picks up, what am I even going to say? How do I explain the state I’m in? I can’t put into words why everything feels so meaningless.

I pressed call.

The phone rang three times. I almost hung up.

But I didn’t.

When Dr. Wang answered, my voice was very quiet. I told her I wanted to book a session. She asked when I was free, and I said the weekend. She said okay, she had an opening. Just like that, I was booked.

After hanging up, I lay in bed feeling strange. I don’t know if this was a good decision. I don’t know if it will change anything. But at least... at least I did something. I’m no longer just lying here, constantly tearing myself down.

I finally took a step.

Even if that step was just making a phone call.

Even if I’m still terrified.

# Work Log

**Date:** Wednesday, March 15, 2023  
**Weather:** Sunny  
**Status:** Productive

---

## Morning Meeting Minutes

Attended the quarterly review meeting for the Data Risk Control team. The discussion primarily focused on the recent progress of updates to the user behavior risk model.

During the meeting, I took the initiative to point out a logical flaw in our current data processing workflow. Specifically, in the outlier handling stage, the team has been using a simple 3σ (three-sigma) rule. However, this method isn't sensitive enough for the long-tail distributions typical of financial data. I suggested switching to the **Interquartile Range (IQR) method** (based on the 1.5x IQR rule). This would allow us to identify abnormal transaction behaviors more accurately while reducing the risk of "false positives" for legitimate users.

I also prepared a brief data comparison: after processing samples from the past three months using both methods, the new approach improved the anomaly detection rate by 12% while preserving valid transactions.

This was the first time I’ve ever spoken up in a large meeting. To be honest, I was nervous for quite a while before opening my mouth, even double-checking my logic repeatedly for any loopholes. But once I started talking, I realized everyone was listening intently; no one interrupted or questioned me. The room was quiet.

My supervisor pulled me aside for a private chat after the meeting. He said it was a great suggestion and that he’s been impressed with my recent performance. He asked if I’d always had these ideas and why I hadn't brought them up in meetings before. I wasn't quite sure how to answer, so I just said I might have lacked the confidence. He smiled and told me I should participate in discussions more often, as my professional expertise is clear to everyone.

---

## Reflections

It’s been five years, and I’ve never been pulled aside and recognized for a suggestion like this. The feeling is a bit foreign, but not as scary as I imagined.

Perhaps I have been consistently underestimating my own value. It’s not that I’ve suddenly become incredibly confident, but this experience has at least shown me that I can do better—and that my voice carries weight.

There’s another group discussion next week, and I’m wondering if I should keep sharing more ideas. I still need to be cautious, though, and ensure my logic is airtight before I speak up.

---

**To-Do:** Draft a detailed technical proposal for the IQR method and prepare for next week’s design review.

Dr. Wang, I wanted to share something that just happened with you.

Yesterday afternoon, I finally worked up the courage to have a talk with my boss. To be honest, I’ve been wanting to say something for a long time, but I never dared to. Do you remember? We discussed my concerns about taking care of my parents and how exhausted work has been making me feel.

So yesterday, I just laid it all out for him. I told him I’ve been thinking about how to balance work and family because my parents are retired now and I need to go home regularly. I also mentioned that I sometimes feel very drained by the overtime and constant work emails. I was actually really nervous while saying all this, terrified that he’d think I wasn’t dedicated enough or that I wasn't committed to the job.

But his reaction completely caught me off guard. He said he understood, and then he asked if I needed a more flexible work arrangement. He even mentioned the possibility of doing some remote work.

Dr. Wang, I’m still in a bit of disbelief. I always assumed that if I expressed my real needs, I’d be seen as unprofessional or uncompetitive. But that wasn't the case at all. What he heard was a real human being, not just a "perfect employee."

It reminds me of what you said—"Everything changes when you find someone who truly understands you." I always thought that was just a theory, but now I’ve actually felt it.

Now I’m wondering if I could communicate with my parents the same way? I’ve been hiding my true thoughts from them, afraid they’ll worry or be disappointed. But maybe they want to hear from the real me, too?

I’d like to keep discussing this. What do you think?

February 28, 2023. Past 11 PM.

Insomnia again. I’ve been lying in bed for half an hour, but my mind is still racing.

What happened today... Nothing special, really. Just a typical workday. And yet, it didn't feel quite ordinary.

This morning, Sister Li sent me a WeChat message reminding me about tomorrow afternoon’s yoga class, adding a smiley face. It was such a simple message, but I found myself reading it over and over. Being remembered... it really feels different. I don’t know how to describe it; I just suddenly felt a little less... invisible. Then, during the afternoon meeting, I actually spoke up and suggested that idea for data optimization. Usually, I just listen to others; I rarely take the initiative to speak.

My boss said it sounded good and that I should give it a try.

Just that one sentence, and I actually felt a bit happy.

But...

I’ve been thinking, what if I were this proactive in my work all the time? It’s been five years, and I’m still in the same position. Maybe I should push for a promotion? Maybe I should take the initiative to talk to my boss about career development?

And then I started talking myself out of it. Don't overthink it. Now isn't the time. The company has so many projects right now; I should just focus on getting my current tasks done first. There’s no rush anyway...

But the truth is, I’m scared, aren't I? I admit it. I’m scared.

Scared that standing out too much in meetings will draw unwanted attention. Scared that if I ask for something, I’ll be rejected. Scared that change will bring more pressure.

But when it came to yoga, why was I brave enough to say yes to Sister Li?

I’m pondering this. One was passive—I was invited—so it didn't feel as terrifying. The other requires me to actively strive, to change, and so I shrink back. This logic seems a bit... ridiculous?

Or maybe I’m just lying to myself. The feeling of being accepted gave me the courage to try something new, but that courage only seems to apply to things where I’m "passively accepted." As soon as it involves me taking the lead to change or taking a risk, I still hide.

"Maybe later." I’ve said that phrase to myself so many times. Maybe later I’ll talk to my boss about my career path. Maybe later I’ll be more proactive. Maybe later...

Maybe "later" will always be "later."

Still, I’m going to that yoga class tomorrow. Sister Li said to just let things happen naturally and not to be nervous. I’ll see if I can stick with it this time. If I can’t even commit to this, then I really have no right to complain about my life.

Let’s just do this one thing well first. As for the rest... I’ll stop thinking about it for now.

It’s late. Work tomorrow.

Go to sleep.

Dr. Wang, I wanted to share something that happened recently.

Last week, I took the initiative to have a talk with my boss. I told him that my father has been having some health issues lately and needs me to go home regularly to care for him, so I asked if we could discuss a more flexible work arrangement. To be honest, I revised the wording of that email countless times before sending it, and I even rehearsed in my head over and over what I would do if he rejected me.

But his reaction was completely unexpected. Not only did he agree to my leave requests, but he also proactively suggested that I could work remotely. He said it wouldn't affect my performance evaluations and even mentioned that we could negotiate deadlines if needed.

I’m only now realizing that for the past five years, I’ve been living under an assumption—the assumption that if I expressed my own needs, I’d be seen as not dedicated enough, and it would hurt my career. But in reality, that assumption might never have been true. I’ve been carrying this invisible weight all this time, as if I had something to prove.

It reminds me of what my mother taught me growing up—to be cautious, don’t cause trouble, and be "good." I seem to have internalized this as a professional strategy, thinking that as long as I stay low-profile and reliable enough, I can protect myself. But actually, I was just protecting myself from a threat that didn't exist.

The strangest thing is that I feel a sense of release now. I no longer feel like I have to choose between work and family, or change who I am just to get a promotion. I’m starting to accept that I am simply an introverted, cautious person; it’s not a flaw, it’s just who I am.

You mentioned before that change isn’t necessarily about changing your essence, but about changing your understanding of yourself. I think that might be what I’m experiencing right now.

Still, I’m a bit uncertain—is this a real change? Or is it just a temporary sense of relief? I’m worried about whether I’ll slip back into my old patterns. What do you think?

May 31, 2022, past 11:00 PM

Mom called to ask how my Japanese cooking was going. I told her I hadn't done it lately.

She said, "That’s okay, take your time. As long as you enjoy it, that’s all that matters." It was meant to be comforting, but instead, it sounded like: *See, you can’t even stick with something this simple.* I could sense the lack of blame in her voice, and ironically, that understanding made me feel even worse.

After hanging up, I sat at my desk staring at the sticky notes. Monday’s plan, Wednesday’s goals, next month’s schedule. They were everywhere. Now, they just look like a pile of failed promises.

*The Soul of Washoku* is still on the shelf, the bookmark stuck at Chapter 3. The progress bar for my online course has been stalled at 42% for two weeks. I bought *Atomic Habits* to fix my executive dysfunction, but I haven't even finished the book. The irony is painful. I’m using a book about consistency to prove just how inconsistent I am.

I wonder, if I can’t even follow through on a simple plan like learning to cook, how can I ever expect a breakthrough at work? Five years in, and I’m still in the same position. How can I expect anything in my personal life? 35 years old, and still alone.

Mom’s words—"as long as you enjoy it"—keep playing on a loop in my head. She was genuinely trying to encourage me, but what I heard was: *This is just who you are. We all know you can’t stick to anything, so we don’t expect anything from you anymore.*

I don’t know if it’s because work is too exhausting or if I truly just lack discipline. I can’t even tell the difference anymore.

I’ve been so tired every day this past month. I just want to lie down the moment I get home, and I have no energy to do anything on the weekends. The books I buy keep piling up, while the plans I finish keep dwindling. I’m like someone who constantly accumulates promises but can never settle the debt.

Maybe I should just accept it—I’m not the kind of person who can persevere. I’m not the kind of person who can change.

But thinking that makes me feel utterly hopeless.

# August 15, 2023, Evening

Sitting on the sofa, the faint scent of dashi still lingers in the living room. Everyone from the group has left, and the house is quiet again. I’m sitting here wondering what exactly happened today.

I was a bit nervous this afternoon—hosting people at my place isn't something I do often. But the moment Sister Li and the others walked in, that tension quickly dissipated. Maybe it was because I’d spent the whole weekend preparing, from cleaning to testing recipes; everything was under my control. Or perhaps, this is my territory, and I know exactly what to do here.

The interesting thing was that I didn’t feel judged like I usually do. They looked at my home, the flowers I’d arranged, and the Japanese dishes I’d prepared with genuine appreciation. Xiao Li said my home was "warm and tasteful," and when another auntie asked about the food, I softly said yes, and then... I actually felt a bit of pride? Not the showy kind, but a quiet, internal sense of validation.

I used to never feel proud of my interests. Japanese cuisine, photography, the things I study alone at home—I always felt a bit... embarrassed? Like I was apologizing for my hobbies. But today was different. When they asked how I learned, I just told them naturally—no explanations, no self-deprecation, just sharing.

What surprised me most was that I played the role of the listener. I didn’t say much all afternoon, but I was listening, asking questions, and laughing. I didn’t feel like an outsider. Instead, there was this sense of... being accepted? Not in a forced way, but very naturally.

As she was leaving, Sister Li said, "Xiaoyu, you really are a special person." I didn’t deny it. Before, I definitely would have said "No, no, not at all" and changed the subject. But today, I just accepted it.

Sitting here now, I’m thinking—maybe I’ve had it wrong all along. It’s not that I’m bad at socializing. It’s just that... I need the right environment. In the office, I’m tense. At parties with strangers, I shrink back. But in my own home, on my yoga mat, with Sister Li, I’m relaxed. I can be myself.

This realization seems simple, but it feels significant to me.

Also, I realized that I’ve actually stuck with it. The yoga, the cooking classes, the friendship with Sister Li... none of these were just passing whims. I’m doing it. I’m changing. It’s not the big, dramatic change I once imagined, but it’s real.

I’m a bit tired now, but it’s that good kind of tired. A satisfied tired.

I’m going home to see my parents tomorrow. I’m not sure how to tell them about today. Maybe I don’t need to. Maybe it’s enough for them just to see the way I am now.
