She listened so intently and even gave me a hug afterward. At that moment, I realized that the confusion and reflections I’ve gone through might actually be meaningful to others. It’s not because I’m anyone special; it’s just because I’ve truly walked that path myself.

To be honest, I never expected I could help someone. I’ve always felt like I’m not good at socializing and don’t have much to teach. But this small moment today made me feel... maybe I do have my own value.

This is all because of you. If you hadn't invited me to the yoga group, I’d probably still be at home overthinking everything, stuck in my own head. You’ve created a space where we can all be our authentic selves and help one another. I wanted you to know how much this means to me.

Thank you, Sister Li.

February 3rd, Sunny

Sister Li ran into me downstairs again today. I was planning to just give a quick greeting and head up, but she caught me and mentioned there’s a yoga group in the neighborhood, asking if I wanted to join.

My first instinct was to say no. Truly. I could feel the word "no" right on the tip of my tongue. But when I saw the look in her eyes... how should I put it? She seemed like she was genuinely inviting me, not just out of politeness or something. It was just so sincere. I don’t think anyone has ever looked at me like that before.

So I said, "Maybe, I’ll see."

Now, lying in bed, I’m still thinking about that phrase. "Maybe"—that’s just so me. Never daring to give a straight yes or no. Always lingering in the gray area. My mom taught me from a young age to be cautious and stay out of trouble, so I learned this routine. But sometimes, I really hate myself for it.

I’ve noted "February 28th, Yoga Class" in my memos. The moment I wrote it down, it felt real. But I also know this is just me trying to mentally prepare myself. Because I’m scared.

Scared of what? Scared that it’ll be awkward once I’m there. Scared of how others will see me. Scared that I’ll give up after a few sessions, just like all those other classes. And then I’ll fall back into self-blame—"See, I just have no follow-through."

But this time feels a bit different?

Sister Li said, "Just let nature take its course." That sentence keeps spinning in my head. Maybe the problem isn't my lack of execution; maybe it’s that I’m always forcing myself to do things I don’t want to do. I was forced to sign up for those other classes, forced to stick with them. But this time... I was invited. It feels completely different.

Still, I don’t dare to be too optimistic. When I broke up with Zhang Hao last time, I had hope too, thinking maybe I could change, maybe I could become more extroverted. And the result? Another failure. Maybe "change" just isn't meant for people like me.

During my walk, I started looking up yoga info. What should I wear? Will I look like a total amateur? What are the other people like? I keep rehearsing tomorrow’s scenarios in my head, trying to predict every possible awkward situation. This habit is so annoying, but I can't control it.

Maybe... maybe this time really will be different?

But I’m still so nervous.

**Saturday Afternoon, November 18, 2023**

**Conversation with Mom · Kitchen**

---

**Title:** Understanding and Gratitude

**Time:** November 18, 2:00 PM – 4:00 PM

**Location:** Parents' House, Kitchen

**Participants:** Wang Xiulan (Mother)

---

**Event Log:**

Today in the kitchen, my mother and I had a conversation that felt different.

We were preparing dinner; I was chopping green onions while Mom was boiling water. As usual, she started reminding me to be careful and not to cut my hand. I was about to give a perfunctory nod like I always do, but this time I stopped and turned to look at her.

I said to her: "I understand now. All those years you told me to 'be cautious' or 'stay out of trouble,' you weren't trying to limit me—you were protecting me. I used to feel suffocated by it, but now I get it. That was the best way you knew how to give."

Mom’s hands stopped moving. She didn't say anything, she just looked at me. Then, her eyes welled up.

She said: "I was always worried you thought I was being too harsh."

Neither of us spoke for a moment after that; we just went back to what we were doing. But that silence was no longer awkward—it was an understanding.

I felt a sense of relief. I also felt a deep gratitude toward her—not just for her protection, but for helping me become the thoughtful, discerning person I am today.

This is a day I want to remember.

---

# Saturday, July 8, 2023, 10:45 AM

I just stepped out of the kitchen, the warmth of the clear broth still lingering on my hands.

I spent all of last night thinking about what my father said. "How has work been lately?"—such a simple question, yet I’ve replayed it in my head a thousand times. Dr. Wang was right: I have been judging my parents instead of trying to understand them.

This realization came somewhat abruptly, yet it feels like something I’ve known all along but never truly admitted.

My father’s silence isn't indifference. I finally understand now that he cares for me in the only way he knows how—through silent company and those seemingly mundane greetings. He isn't good at expressing himself, but that doesn't mean he doesn't care. The same goes for my mother. Her nagging about "being careful" or "not causing trouble" isn't meant to restrict me; it’s her way of protecting me. She even confessed that she, too, had wanted more choices when she was young... My eyes welled up when I heard that.

I’ve always felt guilty about being their daughter—guilty for not being extroverted enough, for not living up to their expectations (or what I *thought* were their expectations). But now I think, maybe they never expected those things of me. Maybe I’ve just been arguing with my own imagination.

So today, I decided to cook lunch for them. Clear noodle soup and steamed fish—very simple dishes. But while I was slicing the fish and simmering the broth, I kept thinking: this is the way I can give back to them right now. It doesn't need to be perfect or extraordinary; it’s just honest companionship and care.

The process of cooking brought me a sense of calm. It wasn't that "I must do this well" kind of tension, but rather... a feeling of being allowed to love.

I don’t know how to describe it. The problems are still there—I’m still 35 and unmarried, still in the same job, still overthinking the little things. But these problems feel lighter now. Because I’ve started to accept them, and I’ve started to accept my parents, and... myself.

I’ve been sleeping exceptionally well these past few days. I don't feel that same surge of anxiety when I wake up in the morning. Maybe this is what peace feels like.

It’s not because everything is resolved, but because I’ve finally stopped trying so hard to change things.

When they come over tonight, I want to really look at their faces. Truly look at them.

Made Japanese food with Sister Li today. She showed me how to roll sushi—it’s much more of an art form than I imagined. As the steam rose in the kitchen, I suddenly felt how precious moments like these are. We’ve already made a date for next time; she says we’re trying tempura.

[Image: Sunlight filtering through leaves onto a cobblestone path, with a few figures strolling in the distance]

The third time already.

I still remember how nervous I was when I first suggested organizing a photo walk. I kept thinking no one would show up, or that it would just be awkward if they did. But Sister Li told me, "Your photos are so beautiful, you should share them with everyone," so I actually gave it a shot.

New faces joined us again today. We walked together, stopped together to watch the play of light and shadow, and discussed compositions for certain angles. When someone asked for my take on this street, the words just came out naturally—none of that overthinking I used to do before speaking.

The most interesting part was when someone suggested organizing other activities in the future. My mind immediately went to—how about a cooking meetup? I’ve been researching Japanese cuisine lately and could teach everyone some simple dishes. I was actually a bit surprised at myself for saying it out loud.

On the way home, it suddenly hit me that I’m no longer just "participating" in something; I’m "creating" something. It’s a wonderful feeling.

Maybe change isn't that hard. Maybe I just hadn't found the right way until now.

# June 15, 2022, 9:30 PM

Another failed night.

The ingredients in the fridge have been sitting there for nearly three weeks. The tomatoes, the onions, and that box of imported Japanese seasoning—I can still remember the rush of excitement when I bought them. "This time, I’m definitely going to master Japanese home cooking," I told myself then. Now, they’re all expired. I don't even have the courage to open the fridge and look.

The book on my nightstand is still stuck on Chapter 3. I was so full of anticipation when I bought it; I even wrote three notes on Douban, documenting my thoughts on Japanese culinary culture. Today, I deleted all of them. Seeing those "records of failure" sitting there in my account made me feel a wave of nauseating shame. It felt like a public announcement: "Look, here is a person with zero execution, someone who can't even stick to a hobby."

I opened the Notes app on my phone and looked at that list. I titled it "All My Failures":
- A three-year relationship, because I wasn't extroverted enough, wasn't good enough.
- Five years without a promotion, because I’m not competitive.
- Every course I ever bought, all abandoned.
- The Japanese cooking plan, shelved again.

I added a new line at the end: **I am a useless person who can't even stick to a simple cooking plan.**

Looking at those words, I wanted to delete them, but I didn't. Because it’s the truth.

Why can I never follow through? Why can others do it while I can't? It’s not the environment; it’s not a lack of time. It’s me. This is my essence—a person who cannot change, cannot persist, and cannot grow.

I’m even starting to wonder if this "inability to persist" will infect every aspect of my life. Work, relationships, self-growth... maybe I’ll never change. Maybe this is just my fate.

As I lie in bed, these questions play on a loop in my head. Like a broken tape recorder. I know what I’m thinking, I know these thoughts are negative, but I can’t stop them.

I have work tomorrow. I have to pretend everything is fine. I have to watch my colleagues moving forward while I’m still stuck in the same place.

I’m so tired.

July 28, 2023, 7:00 PM

Something small happened today, but I can’t stop thinking about it.

Around 3:00 PM, Li Ming suddenly rushed over to my desk. He said there was some data that needed urgent processing and asked if I could work over the weekend to get it done. My gut reaction was to say "okay," just like I always do. But this time, I paused.

I asked him, "Is this truly urgent? Or can it wait until Monday?"

He froze for a second, looking a bit surprised. Then he said that, actually, Monday would be fine.

That was it. One simple sentence. But Li Ming’s reaction made me realize what kind of person I must have been—the "always says yes" type. It hadn't even crossed his mind that I might ask a question like that.

I sat in the office afterward, replaying the moment over and over. It wasn't that I felt guilty for refusing to work overtime; instead, it was... a strange sense of satisfaction?

It reminded me of the last conversation I had with my boss. That time, I gathered the courage to speak my mind, telling him I needed a better work-life balance. He listened—he actually listened. And then he agreed. Since then, my weekends have been quieter, and the emails have thinned out.

Now, it’s my turn to take the initiative and evaluate the urgency of a request instead of just going on autopilot. It feels... different.

But I’m also asking myself: is this a real change? Or was it just a one-time stroke of luck? Li Ming might ask again next time, and other colleagues will surely come to me with "urgent" projects. Will I be able to ask that question every time? Or will I slip back into my old patterns?

Still... Li Ming’s surprise today showed me something. It showed me just how "compliant" I used to be. It showed me that I don't have to be that way.

On the subway home tonight, I sent a message to Sister Li, telling her I’d be at yoga class on time this weekend. She replied almost instantly with a smiley face.

Maybe this is how change happens. It’s not a sudden, earth-shattering transformation, but a small question, a casual remark, and the surprised look on someone’s face that makes you realize you can actually be different.

I still get nervous, and I still overthink things. But at least... I’m starting to believe that I can make different choices.

Dr. Wang, I wanted to talk to you about some things that have been happening lately.

My father’s test results came back last week, and his herniated disc is more severe than the doctor initially thought. He’s having a bit of trouble walking now, and my mother is very worried. I’ve been taking notes constantly over the past couple of days—recording every word the doctor says, organizing the physical therapy schedule, and researching everything I can about spinal disc herniation. I know this is just my habit, but I find that doing these things makes me feel a bit more grounded. And yet… I’m still so uncertain.

What makes me most anxious is that I don’t know if I can truly take good care of him. I’m an only child; this was always supposed to be my responsibility. But now that this responsibility is right in front of me, I’ve started to doubt myself. Can I keep this up? What if his condition gets worse? Should I take time off work to stay home with him?

I sent an email to my boss yesterday saying I might need some flexible work arrangements to care for my father. I revised it so many times before finally hitting send—I originally wrote "need," but then changed it to "might need." I know it sounds strange, but I just couldn't bring myself to say it directly. I checked my leave records for the past five years, and I’ve only taken 8 days off. Eight days. I’ve always put work first, and now that I suddenly have to shift that priority, I feel so guilty, as if I’m betraying something.

There’s another thing I’ve been thinking about. I’ve always carried stress on my own; I’ve never been good at asking for help. But this time, I’m not sure if that’s enough anymore. There’s a neighbor in our complex, Sister Li, who is very warm-hearted. She invited me to join a yoga group before. I never really integrated into that circle, but lately, I’ve been wondering if I should tell her what’s going on. Maybe I need some support? But then again, I don’t want to be a burden to others, and I don’t even know how to bring it up.

I know there might not be standard answers to these questions. But I really need to talk to you right now. I need to know if I’m doing the right thing. Should I adjust my work to care for my father? Should I try to seek help from those around me? Am I truly capable of handling this role?

I’m sorry for such a long message. My head has just been full of these things lately; it’s all a bit of a mess.

August 7, 2022, past 11 PM

I’m still playing back what Mom said in my head.

I went home for dinner this afternoon, and for some reason, it just came out—I told her I’ve been seeing a therapist. I was actually prepared for her reaction, for all those things I’ve heard a thousand times growing up. But she didn't say them. She just paused for a moment and said, "It’s good that you’re seeing a therapist. It’s good to have professional help."

Just that one sentence.

I was a bit stunned, and I even felt like crying. I always thought she’d find it embarrassing, or tell me I’m overthinking things, or ask me why on earth I needed a doctor—but she didn't. She just... accepted it.

Then she added, "Maybe we all need to take good care of ourselves." I remember it so clearly because as I processed those words, I felt my whole body go stiff. Taking care of oneself? Isn't that... selfish? Growing up, she always taught me to be cautious, to not cause trouble, and to be considerate of others. But now she’s saying maybe we all need to take good care of ourselves.

Dad said something too. He rarely speaks, but he said, "You need to take care of yourself." His tone was calm, like he was just stating a simple fact.

I’m sitting in my room now, alone. It’s pitch black outside. I’m wondering if I’ve had it wrong all along. If all my expectations of Mom were just things I’d imagined. Maybe she was never judging me the way I thought she was. Maybe... maybe I’m not a total failure.

But I’m also afraid to think this way. Afraid of what happens if I believe this and then get disappointed again. Afraid that this was just a momentary lapse into kindness, not a real change.

I don’t know.

I only know how I felt when Mom said those words. That feeling of being seen, of being accepted. Even if it was only for a split second.

I need to write this down. I need to confirm that it actually happened, that it wasn't just my imagination.

# MEMO

**Date:** Friday Afternoon, July 21, 2023

**Subject:** Re-evaluating the Fear of Rejection

---

**The Incident:**
This afternoon, I finally emailed my boss to request Monday morning off to take care of my father. I had been agonizing over it for ages—worrying if the phrasing was right, if I’d seem unprofessional, or if it would change his opinion of me. Eventually, I just hit send.

His reply: "No problem. See you Monday morning."

It was that simple.

---

**Key Takeaways:**

1. **Rejection is not a catastrophe**
   - I had imagined a hundred different scenarios where I was turned down.
   - What actually happened: a simple "No problem."
   - My fear ≠ reality.

2. **My boss respects my boundaries**
   - There was no questioning, no making things difficult.
   - This proves that my professional standing is strong enough to support such a request.
   - I don’t need to sacrifice my own needs just to "look" dedicated.

3. **It’s okay to say no, and it’s okay to state my needs**
   - This isn't being difficult; it’s a basic workplace right.
   - Taking care of my father isn't a "personal problem"—it’s a part of my life.
   - I have the right to make work fit my life, not the other way around.

---

**Note to Self:**

The next time you get stuck in a "what if I'm rejected" loop, read this.

You’ve already proven yourself.

---

*P.S. My sleep quality has definitely improved over the last few days.*

October 22, 2022, past 11:00 PM

Another sleepless night. I’ve been lying in bed for an hour now, and my mind is still replaying that scene from this afternoon over and over again.

Xiao Wang asked if I wanted to grab dinner—just a simple question. I didn't even look up at her; I just mumbled, "No, thanks." My voice was so quiet I could barely hear it myself. She paused there for two seconds. I could feel she wanted to say something, but in the end, she just turned and walked away.

Now I’m lying here, picturing her expression again and again. She was genuinely looking out for me. I know that. These past few days, she’s been asking if work is too exhausting or if I’m feeling under the weather. And me? I acted like a cold, heartless person, failing at even the most basic courtesy.

Why did I treat her like that?

She didn't do anything wrong. She just wanted to invite me to dinner. It’s the most normal thing in the world. But I just... I don’t have the energy to deal with anything. Even the idea of eating feels exhausting. I didn't even have the strength to explain, to say, "Thanks for the invite, I'm just a bit tired today." All I could do was reject her in the coldest way possible.

And then the guilt sets in. This guilt makes me feel even more powerless. I start wondering, why am I so pathetic? I can’t even handle a kind invitation from a colleague. I didn't even look her in the eye. I’m like a machine with no emotions.

If I can’t even handle a small thing like this, how am I ever going to change? How can I ever make a breakthrough at work? How can I ever find a real relationship? This is just who I am. Incapable of change. Incapable of progress.

Those Japanese recipes are still sitting on the bookshelf. I watched the first two lesson videos and never opened them again. Every time I see them, I’m reminded of my own incompetence. And now, I’ve added today’s incident to the pile. Just more evidence that I’m useless.

Should I apologize to Xiao Wang? But I don’t even know what to say. "Sorry for my attitude"? That sounds so hollow. Besides, seeing her tomorrow will be even more awkward. I’ll probably just end up avoiding her again.

I’m so tired. It’s not just physical; it’s a total, nameless exhaustion. It feels like my battery is completely dead, yet I have to keep pretending everything is fine.

It’s quiet outside. The lights at Sister Li’s place next door are out. The whole world is asleep, and I’m the only one still awake, chewing on these thoughts in the dark.

Maybe I really do need someone to talk to. But I don’t even have the energy to make a phone call.

- Lightweight—I walk a lot and want something I can carry everywhere.
- Compact form factor.

**3. Learning Resources**
- Abundant online tutorials.
- Active secondhand market (in case I want to upgrade or sell later).

- Avoid getting locked into a niche system.

I know this probably sounds like an overreaction, but I just can’t stop playing it over and over in my head. I keep replaying that conversation, wondering if I came across as too anxious or if I exposed something I shouldn't have.

Do you think this is a symptom of my anxiety? Lately, I seem to be constantly worrying about things like this—saying the wrong thing, acting inappropriately, or being "seen through." Sometimes I’ll rewrite an email a dozen times before sending it, just out of fear that a poor choice of words might cause trouble for someone else.

I think I might need to manage my emotions better... or rather, manage my mouth better.

July 27, 2022 – Past 11:00 PM

I just stepped out of the consulting room. I sat there for a while before driving home. I’m still shaking a little.

I don’t know how to start writing this. It feels like something very important happened today, but I can’t quite put my finger on what it was.

Dr. Wang asked me why I feel like a failure. I started talking about my cooking disasters, my stagnant career, being single… and as I talked, I started to cry. I tried to hold it back because crying in front of someone else is embarrassing, but Dr. Wang said, "It’s okay to cry. This is a safe space." That one sentence was all it took for my defenses to completely crumble. I couldn't stop the tears.

She handed me some tissues. They were just room temperature, but for some reason, I felt cared for.

Then Dr. Wang used a term—"internal stable attribution." She said I’m explaining failures in completely different areas of my life with the same reason: "I am fundamentally flawed." My cooking fails because I lack execution; my career is stuck because I lack execution; I’m single because I lack execution… It’s as if everything boils down to "that’s just who I am."

I had never thought about it that way. I always assumed the problem was me—that there was something wrong with my essence. But Dr. Wang said… maybe the problem isn't who I am, but how I think.

It’s a strange shift. I can’t describe the feeling. It’s like something in my head just "clicked" into a new position.

Moving from "I am flawed" to "my way of thinking is flawed"—it sounds like a minor distinction, but it feels entirely different. A flaw is permanent; it’s something I can’t change. But a way of thinking… maybe that *can* be changed?

I sat in the car for a long time, turning this over in my mind. Maybe change isn’t impossible. Maybe I’ve just been negating myself in the wrong way all along.

I’m tired, but it’s not that hopeless kind of tired. It’s different. It’s the kind of exhaustion that comes after a long cry.

I’ll think more about it tomorrow. Right now, I just want to sleep.

*That day at the office, my body betrayed me. I was sitting at my desk when suddenly my heart started racing and my fingers began to shake. I pretended to go to the restroom and stood in the stall for ten minutes, trying to calm myself down. No one knew what happened. I didn't even know how to explain it.*

*So I started buying things. It’s as if as long as I’m "doing something," it proves I still have some control. But after every purchase comes an even deeper sense of powerlessness. I’m lying to myself.*

*The TCM doctor asked me online if I’ve been under a lot of pressure lately. I said, "It’s okay." I can’t even tell the truth to a stranger.*

*I might not go to the appointment tomorrow. Right now, even facing a doctor feels exhausting.*

I’ve been reading *Norwegian Wood* lately, and one quote keeps echoing in my mind:

"No matter what anyone in the world says, I think their judgment is wrong. In my own way, I can say 2+2=5; in my own way, I can say God is within me."

It’s been a long time since I last wondered whose standards I’m actually living by.

Perhaps change isn't about becoming someone else, but about allowing myself to slow down, and allowing myself to be imperfect.

Goodnight.

Sometimes, our understanding of ourselves might be wrong to begin with.

Lately, I’ve been reflecting on something—I’ve always felt like I have poor execution, and I feel guilty whenever I can’t finish everything. But a friend told me, maybe the problem isn't that I can't do it, but how I perceive what I do. That thought has been circling in my head for a long time.

After yoga class today, Sister Li said to me, "You don't need to be perfect, Xiaoyu. You’re already doing great." I didn't quite know how to respond at the moment, but later, as I was walking alone, I repeated it to myself several times.

It felt unfamiliar. But perhaps that very unfamiliarity is proof that I’ve never treated myself this way before.

I’ve been reading lately and came across a quote: "Choosing itself is a form of power; success doesn't have to mean finishing everything." I think maybe I need to learn to embrace "imperfect" choices instead of viewing them as failures.

Take it slow. No rush.

[Image: Afternoon sunlight spilling across a yoga mat]

March 6, 2023, Night

I just finished my shower and I’m sitting on my bed, playing that scene over and over in my head.

During the meeting this afternoon, I raised my hand. I actually raised my hand. For a split second, a voice in my head flashed, "What are you doing?" but I did it anyway. I pointed out that logical flaw in the data model, used specific numbers, and suggested a way to fix it. My voice was a bit quiet, but it didn't shake.

Then my boss walked over and said, "That correction was very valuable."

I’m still thinking about those words.

Five years. For five years, I’ve sat in that same spot, doing the same work. My colleagues never hear a word from me, and I’ve gotten used to it. But today... I don't know why I spoke up. Maybe it’s because I’d been thinking about that problem for a long time and I was certain. Maybe because... I don't know.

The strangest thing is, I didn't feel tired when I left work. Usually, I’m exhausted and just want to go home and do nothing. But today on the subway, I was thinking about Wednesday’s yoga class. Sister Li said, "Just let nature take its course," and I’ve been dwelling on that.

I’m wondering if I’ve been misunderstanding something all along.

Since I was a kid, Mom always taught me to be careful and stay out of trouble. I took it to heart—too much to heart. I thought being safe meant being invisible, staying silent, and making no mistakes. But the feeling of being seen by my boss today... it actually wasn't bad.

Still, I wonder if this is just a one-off. Will I shrink back again at the next meeting? I’m always like this—I have one good experience, and then I start doubting myself, feeling like that wasn't the "real" me.

Was that the real me?

I’m thinking that maybe my problem with follow-through isn't that I can't persist, but that I’ve been forcing myself to do things I don't want to do. All those courses, all those plans—they were just things I felt I "should" do. But this yoga class... I’m actually looking forward to it. Being invited by Sister Li, rather than being forced into it, feels different.

Wednesday is two days away. I’m wondering what to wear. I’m wondering if it’ll be awkward. I’m wondering if, maybe, this time will truly be different.

But I’m also afraid. Afraid of giving up halfway again, afraid of the self-blame that follows.

Whatever, I’ll stop overthinking for now. Back to work tomorrow. But I want to remember this feeling—the moment I raised my hand, and the moment my boss walked over.

Maybe... maybe I’m not as bad as I thought.

# Saturday Afternoon, August 5, 2023

I’m still shaking a little.

Yoga class just ended, and we were all sitting on our mats chatting. Sister Li was talking about a community event the neighborhood is planning. Then, I don’t know how it happened, but I heard myself say, "Why don't we watch a documentary at my place? I just finished a series about the polar regions, and it was incredible."

The moment the words left my mouth, I regretted it. I sat there listening to my own voice as if I were watching someone else speak. I’ve never done this before—inviting friends to my home. My home is a private sanctuary to me; I don’t even like having people over.

But Sister Li’s reaction made it impossible to take it back. Her eyes lit up, and she said, "That’s a wonderful idea! We’ve all been wanting to get to know you better." Everyone else chimed in. I counted—there are about eight of them. Eight people. Coming to my house.

Now, I’m making lists.

Cleaning—that’s the first thing. I need to do a deep clean. Not just the surfaces; I need to wash the windows, organize the bookshelves, and make sure the bathroom is spotless. Do I have too many teacups? Should I buy new ones?

Then there’s the food. I can’t serve nothing. But I don’t want it to be too complicated either. Maybe Japanese food? No, that takes too much time. Keep it simple: fruit, nuts, homemade cookies? I have been looking at cookie recipes lately...

The sofa. Is there enough seating? I might need extra cushions. And the lighting? Is it too bright? I should dim it to make it softer.

I’m wondering—is this really me?

Two months ago, I was still anxious about whether I had truly changed or if it was just temporary. Now, it feels like I’m answering that question through my actions. I’m not just talking a bit more in yoga class; I’m doing something I never dared to do. I’m opening up my private space.

But... what if the gathering is awkward? What if what I prepare isn't good enough? What if I still don't know what to say?

No, wait. I’ve stuck with this yoga group for so long. Sister Li and I are friends now. I know these people like me—or at least, they don’t dislike me. This won't be a disaster.

I remember what Dr. Wang said: "Change isn't a single moment; it's the accumulation of a series of small decisions." Today’s decision is one of those. A small, crazy, slightly terrifying decision.

I’m still a bit nervous. But... am I also a little excited?

It’s a strange feeling. I’m not sure if I like it yet. But I know I don’t regret saying those words.

Alright, time to start cleaning.

Sitting in the cafe downstairs after yoga class. Sister Li said, "Taking care of your family isn't easy; you must have been carrying so much."

Suddenly, I felt like crying. Not out of sadness, but because—someone finally heard me.

All these years, it’s been just me: facing data alone in the office, tidying the house alone, planning insurance and finances alone. I got so used to carrying the weight myself that I forgot I could actually be seen.

I’ve only recently realized that perhaps changing oneself isn't about becoming who others expect you to be, but about living more authentically. I don’t need to be more extroverted, and I don’t need a promotion to prove my worth. I am who I am—someone who thinks deeply and persists in doing every little thing well. That is enough.

The feeling of being understood is truly warm.

—

📍 Neighborhood Cafe

September 24, 2022 – Past 11 PM

Sister Li’s WeChat message is still sitting there.

"Xiaoyu, there’s a new yoga class tomorrow. Are you coming? We’re all really looking forward to seeing you."

I read it over and over again, and finally replied: "Work has been so busy lately, I probably can't make it for now. So sorry." Then I stopped looking at it.

The truth is, it’s not that I’m too busy with work.

When I looked in the mirror this afternoon, I gave myself a fright. Dark circles under my eyes, a dull, grey complexion, messy hair, and these rumpled pajamas. I just... didn't want Sister Li to see me like this. I didn't want to stand on a yoga mat and face this version of myself in the mirror. It’s just too painful.

It’s not so much a lack of time as it is... a lack of courage.

I’ve been fixing that bug all week, and I didn't get any rest over the weekend. My neck hurts terribly, and I struggled with insomnia again last night. When I woke up this morning, I felt a sense of despair, like I can't get anything right. My cooking plans have stalled again—the Japanese cookbook I bought is still sitting on the shelf, completely untouched. Just another example of "I’ll definitely stick to it this time" followed by failure.

I saw a post from a college classmate on my feed; someone else got promoted. She posted a celebratory photo with a mountain of likes underneath. I gave it a like, and then... I had nothing left to say. Five years, and I’m still in the same spot. My boss trusts my ability, but he’s never once thought about promoting me. Maybe he saw through me long ago—that I’m just someone stable but going nowhere.

Last time I went home, Mom asked when I was going to find someone. I didn't really answer. Zhang Hao used to say I wasn't outgoing enough, and thinking about it now, he was right. I don't even have the courage to meet someone like Sister Li who reaches out to me. I don't even have the courage to look at the exhausted person in the mirror.

Is it really because work is busy, or is this just who I am? Someone with no follow-through, no ability to change, who can only hide at home and feel guilty about her own incompetence?

Sister Li is a truly kind person. She invited me so warmly, and yet here I am, avoiding her. I don't even know what I’m running from—is it social interaction, or is it seeing myself?

Tomorrow, I have to keep fixing that bug. The day after, I have to go home to see my parents. They are getting older; I have to be responsible for their future. I have to earn money, I have to plan, I have to... persevere.

But right now, I can't even stick to a single yoga class.

Forget it, I’m done thinking. It’s too late.

May 3, 2023, 9:45 PM

Sister Li’s words have been looping in my head all night.

"No pressure"—how can those two words sting so much?

When she knocked on my door this afternoon, I actually had my excuses all ready. I was going to say I was too busy with work, or that I hadn't been feeling well lately. I’ve become quite an expert at saying no over the years. But when she said, "No pressure, we’re just doing it for fun. Come if you want, and if you don't, that’s fine too," the rejection just got stuck in my throat.

I agreed.

And then I immediately started regretting it.

Sitting here on the sofa now, I keep asking myself—is this going to be just like all the other times? Those Japanese cookbooks are still gathering dust on the shelf. Then there was that online course I signed up for last year; I paid the tuition, watched three lessons, and never looked at it again. And the photography tutorials from the year before... never mind, I don't want to count anymore. The more I count, the more hopeless I feel.

But... maybe this time is different?

I’m wondering if my past failures weren't actually due to a lack of willpower. Maybe it’s because I put too much pressure on myself. Every time I start something, I automatically switch into "must persist" and "must do it well" mode. When Sister Li said "no pressure," it suddenly hit me—maybe the problem isn't me. Or at least, not entirely.

I don't know if this thought is right. But it gives me this strange sense of release. It’s also a bit scary—because if the problem isn't my lack of execution, then how am I supposed to redefine myself?

I promised to go on May 20th. That’s a little over two weeks away.

In these two weeks, will I start putting pressure on myself again? Will I start obsessing over whether I’ll fail again? Will I find an excuse to cancel when the time comes?

I don’t know. I really don’t know.

But... maybe this time will be different. Maybe Sister Li’s "no pressure" really can help me break this cycle. Maybe I really can just *do* something, rather than trying to complete a "mission."

Maybe.

But I don’t dare to believe it too much.

Friday, December 1, 2023 | Night

Something strange happened today. I’m still processing it.

My boss called me in for a meeting this afternoon. I thought it was just another urgent project, but instead, he asked how I was doing and if anything was bothering me. I was guarded at first and reflexively said, "Everything’s fine"—a survival instinct I’ve had since I was a kid. But he didn't just accept that. He said, "Xiaoyu, your recent reports have been high quality, but I’ve noticed you’ve been staying very late lately."

And then, for some reason, I just let it out.

I told him that both my parents retired this year and I’ve been worried about their medical expenses and daily costs. I said I wanted more time to take care of them, but at the same time, I didn't want my work to suffer. I even talked about my anxiety regarding my own performance—though I didn't use such blunt terms.

As I heard myself saying these things, my heart was trembling. It felt like I was taking a massive risk.

But my boss’s reaction was completely unexpected. He didn't frown or show any disappointment. He just nodded, said he understood, and then asked if I’d be interested in a more flexible work arrangement—like working from home once or twice a week, or adjusting my hours. He said the quality of my work is consistently stable, and what matters most is that I feel comfortable and supported.

I was a bit stunned.

Sitting at home now, I’m still replaying the conversation in my head. I realized I’ve been living under an assumption—that expressing my real needs would be seen as unprofessional or would damage my career prospects. But in reality, when I spoke up, I was met with understanding.

It’s strange. Or maybe it’s not that strange; it’s just that I’ve never tried it before.

I thought about what Dr. Wang said: "When you find the right person and the right way, change happens." I always thought that sounded a bit hollow, but now I get it. My boss isn't the kind of person who would dismiss me just because I have personal needs. He’s been watching my work all along, and he cares about how I’m doing. I just never gave him the chance to see who I really am.

I need to think carefully about this flexible arrangement tomorrow. But regardless, today I did something the old me would never have done—I chose to be seen.

I feel a bit tired, but also a bit lighter.

I finally opened this new notebook. Sitting here on the sofa, it’s pitch black outside the window, with only the occasional sound of someone walking by downstairs. I decided to write something down.

I’ve been thinking about Mom lately. When I went home for dinner today, she started nagging again—telling me that no matter how busy work gets, I have to take care of myself; that I shouldn't push myself as hard as she did when she was young, or I’ll regret it. As I sat across the table listening to her, a thought suddenly became crystal clear: she isn’t trying to limit me; she’s trying to protect me.

It sounds like such a simple realization, but for me, it feels like something has opened up. I always felt that her teaching me to be "cautious and stay out of trouble" since I was a child was her way of setting boundaries for me. But now I understand—it wasn’t a restriction; it was love, expressed in the only way she knew how. She didn't have the means to give me more, but she wanted to make sure I wouldn't get hurt. I even feel a bit of heartache for her—she was trapped by that same caution herself, yet she still wanted to pass it on to me as a gift.

I no longer want to change her, nor do I want to change myself to fit her. I just want to understand her.

During yoga class yesterday, Sister Li asked me how I’ve been. I said I was doing okay, and she smiled and said, "I can tell. You look much more relaxed now." It made me realize that in this group, no one demands that I become a certain way. No one says I should be more extroverted, more lively, or more like someone else. Sister Li just accepts me as I am. I don't have to perform there.

I thought back to the things Zhang Hao used to say—how I should socialize more and participate in more activities. At the time, I felt so wronged, as if my very nature was a mistake. I don’t think that way anymore. The problem wasn't me; the problem was that the lifestyles we wanted were fundamentally different. He wanted a different person, while I just wanted to be myself.

Dr. Wang said last time, "Acceptance isn't compromise; it's the end of internal friction." I can truly feel that difference now.

I don't need to change myself into an extrovert. I don't need to force myself to go to parties that drain me. I don't need to take leadership courses I have no interest in just for a promotion. I don't need to still be living to meet other people's expectations at thirty-five.

I only need to understand: why I chose this path. Why I enjoy cooking, reading, and thinking by myself at home. Why I find a sense of stability in my work rather than a thrill. Why I need deep connections instead of broad social circles.

None of these are flaws. This is just who I am.

As I write this, I feel a strange sense of lightness. It’s as if something has finally been set down. Not given up, but truly let go.

To be continued tomorrow.

# Maybe Execution Isn't the Problem

I had a sudden realization today while I was at the wet market.

Sister Li said I "look like a real housewife now." My gut reaction was to correct her: "Not a housewife—I'm just doing what I love." After the words left my mouth, even I was a bit stunned.

This past month, I’ve spent more time in the kitchen than I have in years. It started with an interest in Japanese cuisine, and now I can handle ingredients with some level of skill. There were countless failures along the way—rice that turned out too hard, knife work that’s still clumsy, and one time I completely botched the timing on a dashi stock. But this time, I didn't give up after failing like I used to.

I’ve been thinking that maybe the change this month isn't because my "execution" suddenly got better, but because... the framework is different.

When Sister Li mentioned there being "no pressure," I finally understood how I had been defining "persistence." I used to always tell myself I had to "complete" things, "not give up," and "see results." These words felt like tiny shackles. Every time I started something, a judging voice would automatically play in my head: *Can you stick with it this time? Are you going to quit halfway again? Are you disciplined enough?*

But while haggling at the market, I realized I was doing this not to "achieve" anything, but simply because... I wanted to. I wanted to know how much the tomatoes cost; I wanted to see if there was fresher tofu; I wanted to chat with the lady at the vegetable stall. There was no pressure, no judgment—just the act of doing.

Maybe that’s the difference.

I’ve always felt I had poor execution, but now I’m wondering: Is execution really the problem? Or have I just been defining it the wrong way? The things I used to "stick with" were often forced, fueled by guilt, and heavy with self-surveillance. This past month, even though there were failures, I didn't stop because of them. Not because I "had to" persist, but because... I wanted to keep going.

I’m still exploring this idea. I’m not sure if it’s the real answer, but at least now, when I walk into the kitchen, I no longer ask myself, "Can I stick with this?" Instead, I ask, "What do I want to make today?"

It feels different.

---

Sometimes I look back at the books I’ve bought, the courses I’ve signed up for, and all those unfinished plans. I used to feel guilty about them, thinking I lacked self-discipline. But now I wonder if the plans themselves were the problem. Maybe I don’t need stronger execution; maybe I just need to ask myself more honestly: *Do I really want this? Or do I just feel like I "should" want it?*

I don’t know where this thought will lead me. But for now, I’m allowing myself to "waste" time in the kitchen. I’m allowing myself to do something simply because I enjoy it, not to tick off a box.

Maybe this is the beginning.

I’m just someone with no willpower.

Someone who can’t stick to anything.

——————

I drew three red lines in my diary. I drew them so hard the tip of the pen nearly tore through the paper.

Then I sat there for a long time, trying to find more accurate words to describe this feeling. Is "no willpower" too absolute? But "poor execution" sounds too mild, like I’m making excuses for myself. I changed it to "I lack basic self-discipline," but then felt "lack" wasn't strong enough. I crossed it out and rewrote: "I simply don't have any."

I spent an hour on these few words. Seriously, a whole hour.

After all that back and forth, I ended up right back at "I’m just someone with no willpower." Because it’s the truth.

It’s been two months. I told myself this was a three-month plan. We’re only two-thirds of the way through, and I’ve already started giving up. My updates on Douban are getting rarer; the people in that group probably haven't even noticed I’m slowly vanishing. I’ve looked at other people's notes—how they persist, how they update several times a week, how they can actually finish a whole book. It only makes me more certain that I’m a failure.

It’s true that work is busy. But other people are busy too.

I just can’t do it.

Or rather, I don’t want to do it. And yet, I’m berating myself for not doing it. What kind of logic is that? I even bought *Atomic Habits* to try and understand why I can’t follow through, and now that book is gathering dust on the shelf too. Ironic, isn't it?

I’m wondering if I should just scrap the plan entirely. But I can't let it go. It’s exactly what Zhang Hao said back then—I’m just this kind of person: someone who wants to change but fears the uncertainty that comes with it, so in the end, nothing ever changes.

So I’m stuck here. I can’t move forward, and I can’t quit.

Just like right now. I should have gone to bed early; I have work tomorrow. Instead, I’m here writing in my diary, overthinking "why I can't stick to things," when this very act of thinking is a waste of time. I know this, but I can’t stop.

I think too much.

But I can't stop thinking.

# On Tomato Sauce, and the Afternoon I Finally Saw Myself

Lately, I’ve been wondering: when do we truly start to believe in ourselves?

Last week, I cooked a meal for my friends. The main course was pasta bolognese. It sounds simple enough, but I spent a lot of time thinking about the "simplicity" of it—how to balance the acidity of the tomatoes with the richness of the meat, when to add the onions, how high the heat should be, and exactly what consistency the sauce needed to reach to achieve that "depth of flavor."

I shared these details with Chen Si while I was dicing the tomatoes. In the past, I would have had these same thoughts but kept them to myself—I used to think this kind of "rambling" was annoying, that people just wanted to eat and didn't care about my process. But that day, I just spoke up. I explained it all, in great detail.

The most interesting part was their reaction. It wasn't a dismissive "oh, okay"; they were actually listening. They asked "why do it this way?" and tried to understand my logic. When they finally took a bite, Sister Li said, "You’ve become so much more cheerful and confident."

My response at the time was: "I haven't changed. I’ve just started to see myself."

I stunned even myself a little when I said that. But it’s true.

The things I always considered "flaws"—my tendency to overthink, my obsession with details, the way I deliberate for a long time before acting—it turns out they were never flaws at all. I had just been hiding them, as if I were doing something wrong. My mother taught me from a young age "not to cause trouble," so I suppressed my own thoughts. Later, in a relationship, my partner told me I was too introverted, and I began to wonder if there was something genuinely wrong with me.

But in the kitchen, there is no hiding. How long it takes for tomatoes to break down into juice is a question that requires thought. If the temperature is off, the sauce separates—that requires my attention. Here, my way of thinking isn't "procrastination"; it’s "optimization."

I recorded this process on Douban—how to build flavors in layers, how to make every bite a discovery. It was only while writing it down that I realized I have always been using my own unique way to understand the world. It’s not the way of a "doer," but the way of a "thinker." Both have value.

One last interesting detail: I’ve started proactively wanting to host another gathering. Not because I was invited, not because I was talked into it, but because I want to. Because I’ve recognized my own ability in this, and I’ve seen that my friends genuinely enjoy it. This feeling is so foreign, yet so real.

Perhaps change isn't about becoming someone else; it’s about finally giving yourself permission to be who you are.

---

*The kitchen in August is sweltering, but I love the feeling.*

But then I thought of her warm, enthusiastic face and how she genuinely invited me... and I just couldn't lie. Besides, I know if I flake, I’ll feel even guiltier afterward. That guilt would haunt me for days. I might as well just suck it up and go.

But... why is Sister Li being so nice to me? We aren't even that close. Does she really want to be my friend, or is it just that neighborly politeness? Am I overthinking again? That’s just who I am—always playing things over and over in my head.

Maybe tomorrow won't be that bad. Maybe everyone else is a beginner too. Maybe... maybe this time I can actually stick with it, instead of quitting halfway like I did with those other classes.

But what if I can't stick with it? What if I’m still the same as before?

Whatever, stop thinking. Eyes closed, go to sleep. I’ll wear those black leggings tomorrow; they’re more slimming.

Oh my god, now I’m thinking about what to wear again. I’m truly hopeless.

Perhaps what we are pursuing is not a higher position, but a more authentic connection.

I’ve only recently come to realize that understanding holds more power than change. It’s not about reshaping yourself to fit others' expectations, but about finding those who truly see you. In that moment, you’ll discover that there was never anything wrong with you to begin with.

Life isn’t a race. Sometimes, simply doing your own thing quietly and feeling the world around you with your heart is enough.

Good night.

Saturday morning, June 22, 2024

I dug up my old journals and came across that line: "I’m afraid of change, but I’m even more afraid of doing nothing." I almost wanted to laugh. When did I write that? Three months ago? Four? Reading it now, it feels like it was written by a different person. It’s not that she was wrong, it’s just that... the "me" now doesn't need that kind of self-persuasion anymore.

While organizing my photography work, I stumbled upon the photos from my very first photo walk. Those blurry leaves, the shaky light and shadows, the compositions I kept over-adjusting... they all seem to glow now. Not because the technique was good, but because in that moment, I was truly doing something I wanted to do.

Spending the morning sorting through my journals and notes made me realize I’ve completed a full psychological cycle. I’ve moved from the anxiety of "Am I really just not cut out for change?" to this current sense of peace. It’s not a numb peace, but a peace of certainty.

Dr. Wang said last time, "You don't need to become someone else; you just need to find the way that works for you." I finally get it now. It’s not about forcing myself to go to parties I hate or faking a smile to be an extrovert. It’s about connecting with people on my wavelength through photography, through walks, and through cooking. People like Sister Li, everyone in the yoga group, and those friends who are happy to just sit and watch the light and shadows with me on a quiet afternoon.

The funniest thing is that work doesn't make me as anxious anymore. I used to constantly obsess over "Why haven't I been promoted?" or "Is it because I'm not good enough?" Now... my life isn't just about work. I have my photography, my friends, the Japanese cooking classes, and my weekly yoga. These things make me feel whole. Work is still work—I do it seriously and I do it well—but it’s no longer the sum total of my self-worth. It’s a strange feeling, but incredibly freeing.

That quote I saved in my notes is still there on the screen: "Life doesn't need to be grand; it just needs to be real, fulfilling, and meaningful." Looking at those words, I don't have a single doubt. This isn't self-consolation; it’s something I’ve actually experienced.

I have yoga class this afternoon. Sister Li mentioned a newcomer might join this week and asked if I’d mind introducing the walking group. I said sure.

I never expected I could say "sure" so naturally.

3:47 AM

Dr. Wang, I’m sorry to bother you so late. I know you’re probably asleep.

I... I don’t really know how to start this.

My sleep has been terrible lately. Last night, I spent hours browsing sleeping pills again. I didn’t buy anything, but it scares me just thinking about it. It’s like something is slowly consuming me, and I’m powerless to stop it.

Dr. Wang, I wanted to share something that happened today.

During the major project review meeting yesterday afternoon, I made a decision—I stood up on my own initiative to share my optimization plan. It might not sound like a big deal, but for me, it felt like crossing a threshold.

It’s been five years. For five years, I’ve just listened to others in meetings, sitting there quietly unless I was directly asked something. Yesterday, I suddenly realized that I’ve been waiting for a "perfect moment"—waiting until I felt good enough, qualified enough, and certain enough that I wouldn't make a mistake. But that moment was never going to come.

When I stood up, I was actually still a bit nervous. But as I spoke, I noticed my voice was steady. It wasn’t a forced calmness; it was real—I knew what I was talking about, and I knew my plan had value.

Afterward, I wrote a sentence in my work log: "Today, I didn't speak up for a promotion; I spoke up because I actually had something to share." I paused for a long time after writing that. I realized that this is exactly what we’ve been discussing lately—not changing yourself to fit others' expectations, but simply doing what is right.

I thought about what you said before: "Self-acceptance isn't about giving up on change; it’s about stopping the use of 'not being good enough' as a way to negate yourself." I feel like what happened yesterday was the result of that acceptance. I haven't turned into some incredibly extroverted person; I’m still the same person who is prone to reflection and caution. But I know now that these traits are actually my strengths, not my flaws.

Strangely enough, I didn't fall into that mindset of "one success must change my entire life." I just felt, "Yeah, this is good. I can keep doing this." Not because I have anything to prove, but because it’s the right thing to do.

Also, my recent conversations with my mother have changed. When I went home last week, she asked how work was going, and I told her about the project plan. After listening, she said, "You’ve always had great ideas; you just weren't very willing to speak up before." Hearing her say that made me want to cry. It wasn't sadness; it was just... the feeling of being seen.

My state of mind is very stable right now. I’m sleeping well, and my mood is steady. I’m no longer so easily caught up in the loop of overthinking small things. Of course, I still think a lot, and I still feel some anxiety, but those are no longer signals that "something is wrong with me"—they’re just... a normal part of the thinking process.

I want to thank you. These conversations have truly helped me see things clearly.

# Personal Change Tracker - January 2024

**Timestamp**: 2024.01.10, 8:30 PM

---

## Comparison & Reference
I dug out my diary from a month ago (early December) to objectively see if I’ve actually changed. The results were a bit surprising—change is indeed happening.

---

## Specific Metric Tracking

**Social Engagement Frequency**
- December: Attended yoga group twice; missed once because I "didn't want to trouble others."
- January (to date): Attended 4 times; zero absences.
- Interaction with Sister Li: From passively accepting invitations → Now proactively asking her to cook Japanese food together.
- Assessment: Frequency has increased significantly, and psychological resistance is lowering.

**Work Performance**
- December: Completed assigned tasks; rarely proposed new ideas.
- January: Proactively offered 3 optimization suggestions during last week’s risk assessment meeting; 2 were adopted.
- Assessment: Though the sample size is small, this is uncommon for me in the last 5 years of my career.

**Communication with Parents**
- December: Visited home once or twice a month; topics revolved around meals, work, and the weather.
- January: During last week’s visit, I talked with my father about his retirement plans and discussed Spring Festival arrangements with my mother.
- Assessment: Conversations have evolved from "checking in" to "genuine exchange." It’s still a bit stiff, but it feels more authentic.

**Self-Care Behaviors**
- Yoga: Consistently 2-3 times a week; no interruptions despite a busy work schedule.
- Cooking: From only cooking for myself → Now cooking with Sister Li; have completed 4 Japanese recipes.
- Reading: Finished *Sapiens*; currently reading a book on social psychology.
- Assessment: Consistency has risen to over 80%. The key factor is the support of "companionship."

---

## Psychological State Comparison

**Feelings in December**:
- Anxiety: 7/10 (Regarding promotion, age, and loneliness)
- Proactivity: 3/10 (Wanted to do many things, but always gave up halfway)
- Self-Evaluation: Self-blame, doubt

**Feelings in January**:
- Anxiety: 2/10 (Occasional flares, but no longer dictates my decisions)
- Proactivity: 7/10 (Acting on ideas immediately; no longer overthinking)
- Self-Evaluation: Acceptance, appreciation

---

## Most Surprising Discovery

I’ve realized that change didn't happen because I suddenly became "more extroverted" or "more ambitious." It happened because I found the right **environment** and **companionship**.

- Sister Li didn’t demand that I change; she simply invited me.
- The yoga group offers no pressure, only acceptance.
- Dr. Wang didn't give me "should" instructions; he just helped me understand myself.
- My parents are slowly changing, too; they’ve started listening to me instead of just giving advice.

So, it’s not "me" as a person that changed, but the **system** around me. When the system is right, everything becomes easy.

---

## Small Goals for the Next Phase

- [ ] Continue the cooking plan with Sister Li (Goal: 2-3 times a month)
- [ ] Propose 2-3 more ideas at work
- [ ] Plan family finances and elderly care arrangements with parents before Spring Festival
- [ ] Finish my current book, then buy the next one
- [ ] Chat more with other members of the yoga group

---

**Final Thought**: You don't need to "optimize" yourself; you just need to find where you fit.

April 15, 2023. Night.

Another mistake.

This morning, Xiao Wang asked why I’ve been taking leave so frequently lately. I gave a vague "just some family matters to handle" and hurried back to my desk. It wasn't until this afternoon that I realized I’d missed an entire section of data in my analysis report. In five years, I’ve never made such a rookie mistake.

My hands wouldn't stop shaking while I was fixing the report. It wasn't because I was afraid of my boss finding out—he ended up gently reminding me to pay more attention to detail—it was because I realized, with terrifying clarity, just how distracted I am. My mind is simultaneously occupied by my father’s herniated disc, next Monday’s physical therapy appointment, the way my mother tried to hide the worry in her voice when she called yesterday... and work.

I tried to quantify the problem with data. I tried to calculate the drop in my efficiency, using numbers to illustrate the conflict between work and caregiving, as if that would make the issue more rational, more controllable. But I gave up halfway through. Because this isn't a logical problem at all.

It’s a choice.

And I don’t know how to choose.

I’ve never been one to fight for myself. Not when it came to promotions, and not when Zhang Hao and I broke up. Now, facing my boss, I don’t even have the courage to say, "I need a flexible work arrangement to take care of my father." Instead, I use "family matters" as a brush-off, as if my family responsibilities are something shameful.

But what if I were honest? Would I be judged as "unprofessional"? Would it affect my standing in the company? I know these thoughts are absurd. But they keep looping in my head.

I’m running out of sticky notes on my desk. I’ve used three just to jot down my father’s medical orders, medication schedules, and the date of his next follow-up. I can do all of that. Concrete tasks, measurable goals—I’m good at those. But that invisible line between work and caregiving? I have no idea how to balance it.

Maybe the promotion doesn't matter at all. Maybe I’ve had it wrong all along. But I also don't want to be seen as someone who is "unprofessional."

I’m exhausted.

Tomorrow is another day.

# Memo

**Date: July 21, 2022, Afternoon**

---

I need to write down what Sister Li said. I’m afraid I’ll forget again.

**"I hear you."**

It sounds so simple. But as I sat there on my yoga mat, my eyes welled up. It’s been a long time since I heard something like that. Not "you should do this," not "why don't you do that," just... someone was listening. Really listening.

**"Asking for help is an act of courage, not weakness."**

I told her how I’ve been failing at everything lately—making mistakes at work, dropping out of my classes, even failing to stick to a simple meal plan. I told her I felt like a complete waste of space. She didn't try to comfort me by saying "no, you're not." Instead, she asked if I’d thought about seeing a therapist. I said... I didn't know. She told me that the act itself is a form of courage—the willingness to admit you need help.

I had never thought of it that way.

**"You don't have to carry it all alone."**

This was the hardest one to hear. Because the moment I heard it, I realized just how exhausted I am. Carrying my parents' expectations, carrying a stagnant career, carrying all this self-blame. I didn't even realize I was "carrying" it until she said it out loud.

And then I cried. In the middle of the yoga group, in front of a bunch of strangers. But Sister Li didn't make me feel embarrassed; she just handed me a tissue and said, "It’s good to let it out."

---

**Things I need to remember:**

- Dr. Wang’s number is in my phone. I can call it.
- Try to see if I can make an appointment for next Monday.
- Sister Li’s WeChat: I can message her when I’m struggling. She said, "You can reach out anytime."
- I am not alone.

---

*These words feel like a lifeline right now. I’m afraid of sinking back in, so I’m writing them here. Next time I feel like everything is meaningless, I can pull this out and read it again.*

March 6, 2022. Night.

I just spent nearly two hours standing in the kitchen. Tamagoyaki.

I was so excited at first. I printed out the recipe and laid it across the counter. But once I actually started... I found myself reading the same page over and over. Three times. It wasn't that I couldn't remember it; I just kept feeling like I might have misunderstood something. Medium heat or low heat? A "pinch" of salt or "1/4 teaspoon"? I even looked up the standard gram weight of a "pinch" in Japanese cooking on my phone.

While cooking, I checked the timer every 30 seconds, terrified the heat was too high or the time was up. Stop, check, resume, stop again. My finger hovered over the timer like I was performing some kind of precision surgery.

The final product... was okay? One corner was a bit scorched, but overall, it was edible. I took a photo, adjusting the angle five times, and then wrote a detailed post on Douban. The temperature, the timing, the brand of soy sauce, the freshness of the eggs, how I felt... I wrote it all down in excruciating detail. It was as if by documenting these minutiae, I could prove to myself that I was taking this seriously.

I know what I’m doing. I’m using this to tell myself—look, I’m not the kind of person who just has a three-minute passion. I’m serious. I’m sticking with it.

But deep down, I also know... I’m overthinking. Checking the recipe repeatedly isn't about quality; sometimes it’s just procrastination. It’s the fear of failing. It’s using "perfectionism" as a mask for my insecurity.

Three months. In my notes, I wrote: "One new dish every week, for three months!!!" I used three exclamation marks. I felt so certain when I wrote it, like this time would be different. This time, I’d definitely follow through. But sitting here now, looking at those words in my notebook, I wonder... will I give up in three weeks? Will this be like all those online courses—bought, started, and then forgotten?

I don’t want it to be like that. I really don't.

Maybe I should buy next week’s ingredients tomorrow. I can’t give myself too much time to think.

Dr. Wang, I wanted to share something that happened today. This might be a bit long, but I felt I needed to tell you.

This afternoon, while I was organizing files on my computer, I suddenly came across that "Failure List"—the one where I listed everything I felt I’d messed up. My initial instinct was to just delete it, but for some reason, I decided to open it and read through it first.

Then, I tried using the method you taught me—challenging those absolute thoughts. I went through it line by line:

"Dropped out of the online course" → I wrote: Actually, it was because the course design didn't suit my learning pace, not because I lacked discipline. I ended up learning some of the content in my own way; I just didn't finish that specific course.

"Three-year relationship failed" → I wrote: This was a choice made by two people; it wasn't entirely my fault. Maybe we just weren't a good match. This wasn't a failure; it was an honest decision we both made.

"No promotion in five years" → I thought about this one for a long time. I wrote: I actually did a good job in this position; I just didn't proactively fight for a promotion. This might have been my choice, not my failure.

...And so on. I re-interpreted every single item.

After that, I took a photo of the list and then deleted the file. I wrote a sentence in my journal: "I’ve decided to stop letting this list define me. These are not my failures; these are my experiences."

To be honest, my feelings are a bit complicated right now. On one hand, I feel a sense of relief, like I’ve put down something heavy. But on the other hand, I’m also doubting myself—does re-interpreting things like this really change anything? Or am I just lying to myself?

Still... maybe this is what you meant by "cognitive restructuring." It’s the first time I’ve actually tried to use the method you taught me, rather than just listening to it.

It feels a bit strange, but there’s also a bit of hope.

# Saturday, July 8, 2023 – Sunny

I just got back from cooking class. I’m still a bit sweaty, and my hands still smell like green onions. Sitting here on the sofa, I suddenly felt the urge to write down how I’m feeling today, because I think something shifted.

In this afternoon’s Japanese cooking class, the teacher taught us basic knife skills. I was nervous at first—having to chop vegetables in front of everyone, knowing my knife work isn't exactly pretty. In the past, I definitely would have practiced over and over at home until I felt "ready" before daring to do it in front of others. But today, for some reason, I just went for it.

My knife skills were indeed lacking. The scallions were sliced at odd angles, and the fish wasn't exactly even. The teacher came over, took a look, and offered some advice while the other students stood nearby. I waited for that familiar wave of embarrassment—that self-reproach I feel whenever I don't do something well.

But it didn't come.

Or rather, it flickered for a second and then vanished. I actually felt like smiling. I realized that this is exactly what learning is. No one expected me to be perfect on my first try. Not the teacher, not the other students. Only I have been expecting perfection from myself.

During the break, a girl sitting next to me—probably about ten years younger than me—asked if it was my first time. I said yes. She started asking why I wanted to learn Japanese cuisine, and I found myself chatting with her quite naturally—about the Japanese literature I’ve been reading lately and my curiosity about their food culture. We talked for nearly twenty minutes.

Looking back now, I didn't feel that "I’m talking to a stranger" anxiety at all. I was just sharing things I love. She listened intently and even said she wanted to read the book I recommended.

I don’t know when talking to strangers stopped being so terrifying.

Maybe it’s because of Sister Li. Or maybe it’s because, during these past two months in the yoga group, I’ve realized that no one cares if I’m the most extroverted person there. I can do my poses quietly and occasionally chat with the person next to me, and that’s enough. That version of me is also accepted.

Growing up, my mother always taught me to be cautious, not to make mistakes, and not to cause trouble. I internalized that into: I must be perfect to be worthy of being seen. But today, I sliced a fish imperfectly and said things that might not have been particularly clever, and I was still seen.

The girl even said, "Your passion for these things is really infectious."

I’m sitting here, still processing those words.

Maybe I don’t need to become someone else. Maybe I just need to allow myself to be imperfect.

I’ll call my parents tomorrow and tell them I’m learning Japanese cooking. I want them to know that I’m doing something that makes me happy.

October 10, 2022, 3:45 AM

Awake again.

This is the third time. Every time I close my eyes, my brain starts playing a movie—just a loop of all that messy, random stuff. I’m such a loser. Why can others do it and I can’t? Is this all my life is ever going to be?

These thoughts come one after another, like someone has pressed the repeat button in my head and it won't stop.

Lying here in the dark, I can hear the occasional car passing by downstairs. The curtains aren't closed tight, and a bit of light from the building opposite filters in. I just lie here with my eyes wide open, while my mind keeps racing.

The most annoying part is that I know thinking like this is useless, but I just can't stop.

I saw Xiao Wang’s WeChat Moments again yesterday. She said she’s learning a new programming language while pushing forward that big project. And me? I can't even stick to a simple meal plan. Those Japanese cookbooks are still gathering dust on the shelf, and I gave up on the video course after only two lessons. I’m so useless.

Why is it like this? Why can other people juggle so many things at once, while I can't do anything right?

I pulled out my phone and opened my diary app. I wanted to write something, but I didn't know what. My fingers hovered over the screen for a long time.

Finally, I typed it out: Maybe this is just who I am. I can't change.

Looking at that sentence after writing it, it really stung. I wanted to delete it, but I didn't. Then I thought, maybe this is just the truth. So I put it back.

Deleted.

Typed it again.

Deleted.

......

My neck is starting to ache again. Probably from lying in this position for too long. But I can't even be bothered to move.

Mom’s been asking when I’m going to find a boyfriend again. I told her work’s been too busy lately. It’s not really that I’m busy, it’s just... I’m tired. I don't want to socialize, I don't want to explain myself, and I don't want to hear all that "you should do this" advice. It’s easier to be alone. But when I’m alone, my head is filled with all this negative stuff.

The bugs at work still aren't fixed. I wanted to rest this weekend, but the emails kept pinging. My boss said it’s urgent. I said okay, and then kept working overtime. It’s just a cycle, never-ending.

Maybe work has just been too busy lately. It’ll get better once things settle down.

That’s what I tell myself.

But deep down, I know it’s not just about work.

The light from the phone screen is a bit blinding. I put the phone down and go back to staring at the ceiling.

I have work tomorrow.

# My First Tamagoyaki | Patience in the Details

Last night, I finally made the first dish from the three cookbooks I bought—Japanese Tamagoyaki.

To be honest, I read the same page of the recipe three times before I even started. The heat, the amount of salt, the sugar ratio... I double-checked every detail, terrified of making a mistake. Even while cooking, I kept pausing every few minutes to glance at the timer, worried the heat was too low or that I’d overcook it. A friend said I was "over-preparing," but I feel like that’s the essence of Japanese cuisine—the details are everything.

The final result wasn't actually perfect. The edges were a bit charred, and the layers weren't rolled neatly enough. Looking at it, I felt a pang of disappointment, thinking maybe I’m just not good at these things. But then, I spent over 20 minutes photographing it from different angles, adjusting the light and the background. Looking at those photos on my screen, I suddenly felt... well, it’s not perfect, but I finished it.

I wrote in my notes: "One new dish every week, stick to it for three months!!!" I used three exclamation marks because I really want this time to be different. Most of the courses and books I’ve bought before ended up abandoned, followed by a period of self-reproach. This time, I want to prove to myself that I’m not someone who lacks follow-through.

I had the tamagoyaki in my lunchbox today. A colleague asked if I made it myself, and I nodded. That feeling of being "seen" was actually quite nice.

Next week’s plan: Teriyaki Chicken. The grocery list is already done.
