September 1, 2022, 8:30 PM

Another night home alone.

I was scrolling through WeChat Moments just now, but I put my phone down. I don’t want to look anymore. Xiao Wang got promoted; he posted a photo of his new office, and the comments are flooded with congratulations. I stared at it for a long time, my finger hovering over the screen for several seconds, but in the end, I didn't hit "like." I didn't know what to say. "Congratulations"? Why should I congratulate him? It feels like I'd just be mocking myself.

Back in May, I posted a photo of something I cooked. Tomato and egg noodles. I remember it took me an hour to make that day, and I took several photos specifically to get the right shot. In the end, that post only got single-digit likes. Looking at that photo now feels incredibly ironic. I used to be so serious about my "one new dish a week" plan—I bought three cookbooks and signed up for an online course... And now? Now those books are just gathering dust by my bed, and I haven't opened that course in ages.

I’m 35. That fact feels especially vivid today.

My old classmates are all getting promoted, getting married, having kids. Their social media feeds are like documentaries, recording one milestone after another. And mine? A bowl of tomato and egg noodles from five months ago. And before that? I can’t even remember. Maybe the occasional shared article or a documentary I watched. Nothing worth sharing.

How do they all do it? How do they get promoted, keep learning new things, and have so much energy to curate their lives? I can’t even stick to a simple cooking plan. What does that say about me? It means I’m fundamentally incapable of change. It’s not about the environment, and it’s not about time. It’s just me.

This is just the kind of person I am.

Five years at this job, and I’m still in the same spot. Overtime, fixing bugs, submitting reports. Over and over again. My boss trusts my competence, but he’s never promoted me. Does he actually look down on me? Or does he just know I’m not cut out for management? I know it myself—I have no leadership skills, I can’t give a speech, I don't even dare to speak up in meetings. How could I possibly manage other people?

Sometimes I open recruitment sites to see if other companies are hiring data analysts. But I never send out my resume. Because I know that even if I switch jobs, I’ll face the same problems. This is who I am. Incapable of change.

My neck is starting to hurt again. My lower back, too. I was supposed to rest this weekend, but work emails kept interrupting. I didn’t say no to the overtime. I never say no.

Lying in bed, I keep asking myself: Why can others do it while I can’t? Why can’t I even stick to one plan? Is this how my life is going to be?

No answer.

I guess this is it.

Dr. Wang, I wanted to share something that happened recently.

When I went home for dinner last week, I took the initiative to talk to my parents about future care arrangements. To be honest, I never used to bring this up—I’d either avoid it entirely or feel like I was being forced into a burden. But this time was different.

I calmly told them that I’d looked into a few insurance products and consulted a financial advisor. I walked them through specific numbers and plans, not to prove a point, but just to... plan together. You know, I realized that as I was explaining everything, my voice was firm, but not tense.

What surprised me most was my father’s reaction. He asked me a lot of questions—about the coverage amounts, the claims process, even why I chose one product over another. It suddenly hit me that he was actually listening. He wasn't just passively accepting it; he was thinking. It made me feel... seen?

I used to feel like discussing these things was an obligation, a heavy responsibility. But that day, I realized that we were actually preparing for the future together. It wasn't me taking care of them; it was us facing it together.

My mother mentioned later that she was happy I had these thoughts and was taking action. She kept it brief, but I could feel her approval.

I think this is the shift you mentioned before—from "I have to do this" to "I want to be involved." I still feel some anxiety now, but it’s no longer that sense of powerlessness. Instead, there’s a sense of... fulfillment?

However, I do have a new question. Since I can feel this sense of agency and clarity in this matter, why do I still tend to be so passive at work? Or rather, what exactly is the difference between these two scenarios?

February 15, 2023, 8:00 PM

I had a phone call with Dad tonight. I originally just wanted to check in on how he was doing, but we ended up talking for nearly forty minutes.

That’s very unlike me.

I remember the first thing I said was, "Dad, how’s your back lately?"—Mom had mentioned before that his back was bothering him from sitting too much. I heard a hint of surprise in his voice when he replied, "Oh... it’s alright. What made you ask all of a sudden?" I didn't quite know how to answer, so I just kept going, asking about his blood pressure, his sleep, and even if he needed me to help him book a physical.

He said something that I’m still playing over in my head: "You seem different lately."

Different? How am I different? I didn't press him for an answer, but I felt this strange sensation—the feeling of being seen through, but not in an uncomfortable way. It was the feeling of being truly seen.

Toward the end of the call, he suggested I come home this weekend because Mom misses me. Instead of checking my schedule first and making excuses like I usually do, I just said yes. It wasn't until after I hung up that I realized what I’d done—I had agreed, and I hadn't even hesitated.

Sitting at my desk, I suddenly thought about all those wasted weekends. All that time I could have spent with them. They are getting older; I know this, but I’ve always handled that fact rationally—insurance, financial planning, logistics—as if I were preparing a work report. I never truly stopped to feel the reality that they are right there, and that I can simply choose to see them.

But this time, I chose to.

I don’t know when this shift started. Maybe it was that day Sister Li invited me to yoga? Maybe it was when I decided to go see Dr. Wang? I can’t say for sure. But this change brings me both a sense of relief and a pang of regret—why couldn't I have done this sooner?

When Dad said, "You’re different," I didn't argue. I just listened, feeling the warmth in those words. Maybe I really am changing. Maybe it’s not just my imagination.

I’m going home this weekend. I want to spend more time with them.

March 26, 2023, 7:00 PM

I just spent over two hours at the teahouse. Now that I’m back home, it all feels a bit surreal.

After class, Sister Li asked if I wanted to go for tea. My first instinct was to decline—those familiar excuses immediately popped into my head: I’m tired from work, I have emails to reply to at home. But just as the words were on the tip of my tongue, I paused. What was I thinking? Was I really that tired? Or was I just saying "no" out of habit?

Then I heard myself ask, "Which teahouse? What time?"

Sister Li looked a bit surprised; I was a bit surprised too. But I went anyway.

While we were sitting there, Sister Li started talking about her recent work. Xiao Mei was there, along with two others from the yoga group. They were discussing some data issues with a project, and I originally intended to just listen. But then Xiao Mei shared an idea, and I suddenly realized there was a flaw in her logic. I pointed it out quite naturally and even gave her some suggestions on how to reorganize her thoughts.

She listened intently and later said, "Xiaoyu, your analysis made everything so much clearer."

I don’t know why, but in that moment, I felt something different. It wasn’t just the feeling of being passively invited or accepted; it was... that I had contributed something. I was useful.

Sister Li also asked what I’d been researching lately. So I talked about Japanese tea and the characteristics of different regions. I even mentioned how I use Douban to keep my tasting notes. They listened so closely and asked a lot of questions.

I realized that while I was talking about these things, I didn't feel my usual anxiety. I wasn't thinking, "Am I talking too much?" or "Are they bored?" I was simply sharing something I love.

On the way back, a question crossed my mind: maybe I’ve always thought I was bad at socializing because I was always doing things I didn't want to do? Those forced gatherings and forced small talk—of course they were exhausting. But being with these people, discussing things I’m genuinely interested in, helping someone clear their head... isn't that socializing?

That *is* socializing.

It seems I’ve been underestimating myself all this time.

Sitting on the sofa now, my mind is still a bit of a mess. But there’s this warm feeling. Sister Li said we should go again next week and asked if I was interested. I said yes.

No hesitation. Just "yes."

The feeling of this "yes" is completely different from the "yes" I used to say when I was forcing myself.

# October 31, 2023, 10:00 PM

Sitting at my desk, it’s pitch black outside the window. I can hear the occasional pop of firecrackers from downstairs—someone must be celebrating Halloween. I opened my diary, wanting to record something, but I didn't know where to start.

In the end, I decided to write it down. Because today—or rather, during this recent period—I feel like I’ve come to understand something very important.

I was on the phone with Mom the day before yesterday, and she suddenly brought up my job. I thought she was going to urge me to push for a promotion again, to become more "outstanding." But she didn't. She said, "You’re doing great. Why do you feel like you have to go into management? You’re fine just the way you are." I was stunned for a moment. I’d never heard her say anything like that. She had always taught me to be cautious, to work hard, and to climb the ladder. But now, she was telling me that I am enough.

I realized that I’ve been running all these years. Running from what? Running away from the version of myself that is "not extroverted enough, not proactive enough, not like everyone else." But now, it’s as if Mom is saying—you don’t need to run. You are who you are, and that’s perfectly fine.

It sounds simple, but for me, it feels like it has overturned everything.

I am not a failure. I’ve just chosen a different path. My career might not be "glittering," but it is stable. My social circle isn't large, but it’s filled with sincere people. I haven't been promoted, but I also don't have to shoulder the kind of stress I never wanted. I’m not married, but I haven't compromised myself just for the sake of a wedding.

I’ve been doing yoga with Sister Li lately, and she mentioned that I seem much more relaxed. I smiled. It’s true, I am relaxed. Because I’ve finally stopped the war with myself.

I always viewed that relationship with Zhang Hao as my failure. He said I was too introverted, not social enough. For a long time, I thought that was a flaw of mine. But now I think, maybe we just weren't a good fit. He needed someone different, and I needed to find people who understand me. And I have found them—not just one person, but a group of people. Mom, Sister Li, the yoga group, Dr. Wang. They all see me, and they accept me.

As I write this, my pen is steady on the paper. Steadier than usual. Because I don't have as much doubt and anxiety anymore. I know what I want, and I know what I don't want.

I don’t need to become anyone else. I only need to be myself. And myself is already good enough.

Writing this, I pause and look at myself in the mirror. 35 years old, single, no promotion, no extraordinary achievements. But I am at peace. I am content. And it feels wonderful.

Dr. Wang, I wanted to share some thoughts I’ve been having lately. I’m not sure if now is the right time to say this, but I feel like I can’t wait any longer.

A lot has happened recently. My father’s recovery is progressing better than expected, and I’ve started going home an extra time each week to be with him. Interestingly, through this process, our communication has become much more natural. We watch the news together; sometimes he’ll take the initiative to ask about my work, and I’ve started opening up to him about my thoughts as well. This rarely happened before.

Also, I recently wrote a sentence in my diary: "I can be an introverted, cautious, and reliable person, while also taking care of my family, building meaningful connections, and living a fulfilling life." After I wrote that, I sat there in a bit of a daze. I realized that I had always assumed I needed to change who I am in order to have those things.

Regarding work, I had an honest conversation with my boss about my needs regarding the pace of work. His reaction was much more supportive than I imagined. It made me rethink something—why was I trying to change myself just for a promotion? Maybe a promotion isn't what I want most after all. I see things more clearly now: I want to be able to take good care of my parents, have time for the things I love, and be reliable and professional at work. I already have those things.

Then there’s Sister Li and my friends from the yoga group. I’ve started sharing my real thoughts with them instead of always keeping my distance. Their reactions have made me feel understood, which has broken my long-standing pattern of "carrying everything alone." I’ve discovered that I don’t need to change my fundamental nature to connect with others; I just need to express myself openly.

I think you might have been guiding me toward these realizations all along, but now I’m truly feeling it. It’s not just an intellectual understanding anymore—I can actually feel it.

I wonder if these changes are what our sessions have been working toward? It feels like something has suddenly clicked.

# Consultation Notes | 2023.10.02

**Keywords**: The Safe Path / Patterns of Choice / Authentic Needs

---

## Issues Surfacing Today

**Q1: Why do I always choose what's "safe"?**
- Major: Statistics (Clear logic, minimal interpersonal interaction)
- Career: Data Analysis (Independent work, low risk)
- Lifestyle: Routine, predictable, orderly

Dr. Wang asked me: "Is there anything inherently wrong with these choices?"

I said no. But I keep asking myself—is this my genuine preference, or am I hiding from something?

**A: It might be both.** I do genuinely enjoy structure and independent work. But I am also hiding—hiding from uncertainty, from judgment, and from failure.

---

## New Insights

There is no need to feel guilty for "choosing safety."

This isn't cowardice; it’s my understanding of myself. My nervous system is indeed more sensitive to uncertainty. My way of thinking is naturally more cautious. These aren't flaws; they are just traits.

**But the problem is**: I sometimes treat "safety" as the only definition of "correctness." Whether it’s about getting a promotion, getting married, or making a change—I judge every choice through the lens of safety, and then I fall into self-reproach because I know there are things I actually want, but lack the courage to pursue.

---

## To Reflect On

# Cooking Notes | December 3

**The Moment of Seasoning**

I was making dashi with Sister Li today. When it came time to season it, I hesitated for a moment. She asked if we should add some ponzu to balance the saltiness of the soy sauce. I had a thought, but I didn't say it right away.

After a pause of about two seconds, I finally spoke up: "I think if we add the vinegar now, the acidity might overpower the natural umami of the broth. Maybe we should let the soy sauce flavors fully develop first, then decide based on the final balance."

Sister Li listened and nodded earnestly. "You're right," she said. "I was thinking the same thing, but it just didn't click until you said it."

So that’s what we did. The final soup was delicious.

**It wasn't the decision itself that mattered. What mattered was—**

When I defended my idea, I used logic, not just a "feeling." I could clearly explain the "why" instead of being vague. More importantly, I didn't let my introversion make me feel like my thoughts weren't worth expressing.

In the past, I would have thought: *I’m probably wrong; Sister Li is more experienced, I should just listen to her.* And then I would have stayed silent.

But today, I didn't. I spoke up. And I spoke clearly.

Sister Li’s response wasn't a dismissive "Oh, okay." She genuinely acknowledged it. In that moment, what I felt wasn't the smugness of "being right," but rather—how should I put it—a sense of being understood?

My way of thinking isn't an obstacle. It’s simply how I make decisions. And sometimes, that way of thinking is valuable.

I need to remember this feeling.

---

*Later, in the yoga group chat, Sister Li mentioned the soup and said, "Xiaoyu’s suggestion really made sense." I felt a bit shy when I saw it, but also a little... proud? Maybe that's too strong a word. Let's just say, I felt seen.*

[Image: A yoga mat spread across a sun-drenched living room, with the city skyline visible in the distance.]

I’ve only recently come to realize that change doesn't always have to be drastic.

Sometimes, it’s just a quiet afternoon, a shift in posture, a different way of breathing. The body remembers.

I once read that "gentle power is often the most piercing." It wasn't until the other day that I truly felt what that meant.

Keep doing the right things, no matter how slow the progress. 🧘‍♀️

Friday, September 15, 2023
Evening

Sitting in the office, it’s already dark outside. My colleagues are leaving one by one, and I should probably pack up too. But I want to jot down some thoughts from today first.

While I was optimizing a risk assessment model this afternoon, a question suddenly popped into my head: How much longer do I want to dive deep into the field of data analysis? I’ve thought about this before, but back then, the question was fueled by anxiety—as if I were rushing myself to find an immediate answer. Today was different. I just asked myself calmly, then took out my notebook and listed a few related questions:

- Do I want to learn new skills? Like business analysis or product thinking?
- Is the intersection of data analysis and business operations actually interesting?
- What kind of work environment and pace do I feel comfortable with?

As I was writing, I realized something—I don’t need an answer right now. These questions can be explored slowly. I used to get trapped in the anxiety of "having to make a decision immediately," but now I seem to have found another way: letting these questions ferment in my mind while I observe and feel things out through my work.

Sister Li said something once that I finally truly understand: "Some things aren't figured out before you do them; they are figured out in the process of doing them." I used to feel guilty for a long time, thinking I was an "overthinker." But now I see that thinking itself isn't the problem; the problem was that I pitted thinking against action. Thinking helps me clarify my direction, while action helps me validate my ideas. They aren't enemies.

The most interesting part is that I’ve suddenly become curious about "the integration of data and business." I used to just mechanically perform analyses and churn out reports, then wait to be told what to do next. Now I’m starting to wonder: What if I understood the business logic more deeply? What if I didn’t just provide data, but provided insights? It sounds a bit ambitious, but it’s not something that requires a promotion to achieve.

By comparison, I remember myself six months ago, obsessing over not getting promoted and feeling like a failure. Looking back at that anxiety now, it feels very distant. It’s not that I’ve become less ambitious; it’s just that the shape of my ambition has changed. What I care about more now is whether my work supports the life I want—taking care of my father, having time for yoga, being able to read and cook. It seems professional success really has a new definition for me.

I’m a bit tired, but it’s that "fulfilled" kind of tired, not the "crushed" kind.

More tomorrow.

# Career Planning Reflection

**Date:** September 15, 2023, Afternoon
**Location:** Office

---

## Core Questions

During my recent sessions with Dr. Wang, I realized that I’ve been passively accepting the status quo rather than actively thinking about what I truly want. Sitting at my desk this afternoon, a few thoughts suddenly clicked, and I need to sort them out properly.

### Question 1: How much longer do I want to stay deep in the data analysis field?

This question has been bothering me for a long time. For five years, my work has been stable, but also very repetitive. I’m good at what I do, but being "good at it" and "loving it" don't seem to be the same thing.

**Initial Thoughts:**
- I don’t hate data analysis itself, but pure data processing is starting to wear me out.
- Maybe the problem isn’t the field, but the fact that I’ve been stuck in "technical execution" rather than "business thinking."
- If I could bridge the gap between data analysis and business insights, would I feel differently?

### Question 2: Do I really want to learn new skills?

I’ve bought so many books and signed up for plenty of courses, but I rarely finish them. I’ve been beating myself up over this. But lately, I’ve been wondering—maybe it’s not a lack of discipline, but rather that I haven't found a direction that truly captivates me.

**Initial Thoughts:**
- Short-term interests: Data visualization, business analysis methodologies.
- Long-term considerations: Do I need to pick up management or communication skills? (Even though that sounds terrifying.)
- The prerequisite, however, is that this learning must align with my actual career goals, rather than just learning for the sake of "should."

### Question 3: If I had the chance to switch careers, what would I consider?

I’ve never given this serious thought. Perhaps it’s exactly because I haven't thought about it that I feel this underlying sense of unease.

**Initial Thoughts:**
- I wouldn’t leave the data field entirely, but I could consider different applications.
- I have some interest in how data is used in social research and policy analysis.
- Or perhaps look for cross-departmental opportunities within my current company?
- But before any of that—I need to figure out what I actually want first.

---

## Next Steps

- [ ] Schedule a casual chat with my manager next week to see if there are any cross-departmental project opportunities.
- [ ] Curate a "Data Analysis + Business" learning list (don't overdo it; 3–5 resources are enough).
- [ ] Continue discussing these issues in depth with Dr. Wang during our next session.
- [ ] Consider talking to Sister Li or friends from the yoga group to hear their career perspectives (though this is still a bit out of my comfort zone).

---

## Reflection

I’ve realized something lately—I don’t hate change; I just need time to understand the meaning behind it. When I can see the "why" behind a change, I actually commit to it quite seriously. Maybe that’s just my process.

No need to rush. Think it through, take it slow.

Dr. Wang, I wanted to talk to you about what’s been happening lately.

When I went home last weekend, my mom asked if I was exhausted from work. I just told her that a recent project had been pretty intense, but actually... I don’t know how to explain it. During dinner, I realized the food had no taste in my mouth; I was just eating mechanically. My mom was watching me, and I could feel her worrying. Later, my dad said, "Young people need to learn how to balance work and life." Hearing that felt incredibly painful. He wasn't blaming me, but I just felt... like I can't even handle the most basic things.

I didn't tell them the truth. I chose to hide it, just like I always do.

Since I got back, I’ve been wondering: why am I like this? Work is busy, sure, but it’s not just about work. Lately, I always feel drained, and I can’t seem to get interested in anything. All those cookbooks I bought, the yoga classes... I can’t stick with any of them. When I see my former classmates' posts on social media—seeing them get promoted or start families—I fall into this strange state where I want to change, yet feel like I’m fundamentally incapable of it.

I keep asking myself: Why am I always like this? Why can’t I be like everyone else? Why can’t I even manage the most basic self-discipline?

I don’t know what’s wrong with me. These questions keep looping in my head, but I can’t find the answers. I thought maybe we could really talk this through during our next session.

# Saturday, October 1, 2022, 11:00 PM

I’m back again. Sitting alone in my room, the city lights are still glowing outside the window, but I don’t want to look at anything.

Mom asked me today if work has been too exhausting. The moment I saw that look in her eyes—that look of concern—I knew she could tell. But I still said, "A project has just been a bit tight lately." Why do I always do this? She’s clearly caring for me, yet I choose to hide. Maybe it’s because once I say it out loud, it becomes real. As long as I keep quiet, I can still pretend everything is fine.

During dinner, Mom made my favorite braised pork. I put a piece in my mouth and chewed for a long time, but I couldn't taste anything. Truly nothing. Watching Dad eat with such gusto across from me only made me more certain—the problem is with me. I pretended to be focused on eating, but I was just mechanically swallowing.

Dad said something that I’m still playing over and over in my head: "Young people need to learn how to balance work and life." He said it calmly, without a hint of blame in his voice, but all I heard was a reproach. He was saying I haven't balanced things well. And he’s right. I haven't.

I originally planned to stay home until tomorrow afternoon, but not long after dinner, I said I was a bit tired and left early. Mom’s expression changed; I saw it. She wanted to say something, but in the end, she said nothing. I’ve made them worry again.

On the subway back, I was wondering—why do I always hide my true self? Has it been like this since I was a kid? Or did I choose to be this way?

Zhang Hao once said I would never change. Maybe he was right.

Those cookbooks are still piled on the bookshelf; I haven't even flipped through them. I failed again. Just like everything else I’ve failed at. Why can others persist when I can't? Why do I always give up the moment the initial spark of passion fades?

Work again tomorrow. Another Monday. Another five days.

I’m tired. But I don’t even know what I’m tired of.

September 22, 2023, 8:00 PM

Sitting at my desk, it’s pitch black outside the window. I just finished the dishes and came back to my room, but what my father said is still echoing in my ears.

I always thought what they cared about was when I’d get married, when I’d get promoted, or when I’d have children like my old classmates. For years, I’ve been spinning in circles within these "expectations," getting more anxious with every turn. But today in the kitchen, when I finally plucked up the courage to say, "I’m not sure if I’ll ever get married... but I know I want a balanced life," my father didn't fall silent, nor did he look disappointed. He said, "I’m so grateful for your company lately. The time we spend cooking together is my favorite part of the day."

My eyes feel a bit hot, but the tears didn't fall.

I realized I’ve been living just to meet expectations. To prove I didn't waste the education they gave me, to avoid letting them down, to fit some template of "what a 35-year-old woman should look like." But that’s not what my father talked about. He talked about the time we spend together—that’s what he treasures most.

Mom said something too; I didn't quite catch it, but her tone wasn't as rushed as it used to be. She asked how work was going. I said it’s fine—no promotion, but I don’t need one. She didn't push me to "strive" for anything more.

I’m starting to accept my own choices. I was a bit surprised myself when those words popped out in the kitchen. But once they were out there, everything felt different.

The changes over the past month seem to be working. I’ve stopped replying to late-night work emails, and I’m actually resting on weekends. I took the initiative to chat with Sister Li in my yoga group and realized they’re all struggling with their own things too; no one’s life is perfect. I’m not that lonely, and I’m not that "unusual" after all.

But the biggest change is this—I no longer feel like a failure.

Not getting promoted isn't a failure. Not being married isn't a failure. Living alone isn't a failure. I’m just living life in my own way, and this way allows me to be there for my father, to have time for the things I love, and to turn strangers into friends on a yoga mat.

It’s worth it.

Back to work tomorrow. But it’ll be different.

Sister Li, I just wanted to say it to you one more time—thank you.

I’ve spent the whole day today still in a bit of disbelief over what happened. Lying in bed last night, I kept thinking to myself: I actually organized an event like that. And... it actually went well?

You know, when I was at the cafe introducing the concepts of light and shadow to everyone, I suddenly realized that I was talking. Really talking. It wasn’t that forced, nervous kind of speaking; it was because I truly wanted to share these things. Dr. Wang told me before that when you’re doing something you truly love, introversion is no longer an obstacle. I was skeptical at the time, but today, I felt it.

What moved me the most was your hug. I know it sounds a bit dramatic, but in that moment, I truly felt seen. When you said, "You organized this so well," I didn't immediately dismiss the compliment. Instead... I accepted it. That is a huge change for me.

And then there was that moment when everyone was discussing how to capture architectural details. I took the initiative to share my ideas and even discussed shutter speeds with Xiao Li. Hearing my own voice talk about these professional things felt strange, yet so real.

I’m already thinking about next month’s event. I was thinking maybe we could try a themed shoot—something like "Daily Life in the City" or "Stories of Light and Shadow"? I already have so many ideas swirling in my head. It’s a strange feeling; it’s excitement rather than anxiety.

Sister Li, thank you for constantly inviting me, and thank you for believing I could do this. Sometimes I think changing a person doesn't require a major event; sometimes you just need someone who truly accepts you and tells you, "Come on, let’s try it together."

You are that person for me.

❤️

Sister Li, thank you for what you said today.

I’ve been thinking about what you said regarding "no pressure." It feels like no one has ever said that to me before.

I realized that I’m always setting these must-achieve goals for myself, and then I get so hard on myself if I fall short. But when you put it that way, it suddenly occurred to me... maybe the problem isn't that I can't do it, but that I’ve been overcomplicating things from the start.

I’m going to go to the wet market this weekend. I’m a little nervous, but I’m also looking forward to it.

Thank you again.

April 20, 2023, Night

Sitting on the subway, I kept replaying this question in my mind: When did I become someone who could just say, "Okay, I’m in"?

Sister Li knocked on my door again this afternoon. When I heard the sound, my first reaction was that familiar pang of anxiety—*what excuse should I make up this time?* But this time was different. She told me there was a new session for the yoga group on Saturday and asked if I wanted to join. I opened my mouth, ready to say "Let me see" or "I might be busy this week," but when the words came out, I simply said, "Okay, I’m in."

I even stunned myself for a moment after saying it.

Sister Li’s eyes lit up instantly. "I knew you’d come," she said. In that moment, I felt something very strange—the feeling of being expected. Of being seen. It sounds dramatic, but that feeling is truly foreign to me. Usually, I’m used to people’s disappointment or indifference; I hadn’t even realized it had become a habit. But there was no disappointment in the way she looked at me, only a natural, matter-of-fact expectation—as if she had believed I would come from the very beginning.

I thought about it for a long time on the subway. I didn't say no. That fact itself seems more significant than actually attending the yoga class.

For months, every time Sister Li invited me, I would run through every possible excuse in my head. *Is my neck still hurting? Is work too busy? Do I just want to rest this weekend?* I could always find a "reasonable" excuse to protect myself. But today, I didn't. I just said yes.

Maybe it’s because my body really does feel better. Maybe it’s because no one in that group judges me. Maybe it’s because Sister Li’s warmth makes me feel needed. I’m not entirely sure. But what I am sure of is that, in that moment, I didn't feel afraid.

On the way back, I wondered: Is this what change looks like? Not some grand resolution or a meticulously planned transformation, but just one afternoon where you suddenly don't say no. And then you realize the sky hasn't fallen down.

I’m sitting at home now, looking at the night sky outside the window. Saturday is four days away. I’m a little excited, and a little nervous. But this nervousness is different from before—it’s not the "I’m going to make a fool of myself" kind of nervous, but the "I don't know what’s going to happen" kind.

It feels a bit strange. But I don't hate it.

January 3, 2024, Evening

Today still feels a bit surreal.

During this afternoon’s team meeting, I did something I haven’t done in five years—I took the initiative to fully express my professional opinion. It wasn’t a reluctant answer to a question; I actually stepped up and gave a detailed presentation on a plan to improve our risk assessment model.

I still remember the moment right before I spoke. Sitting in the conference room, looking at the data and logical framework I had meticulously prepared on the slides, my heart suddenly started racing. That familiar sense of anxiety washed over me—thoughts like "What if I don't say it well enough?" or "Will my colleagues think I'm just showing off?" But this time was different. I did the deep breathing exercises Dr. Wang taught me. Just those few simple breaths felt like they sucked all the intrusive thoughts right out of me.

And then, I started talking.

My voice was steadier than I expected. My logic was clear. I even handled my boss’s follow-up questions, explaining exactly why I adjusted the parameters that way. The whole thing lasted about five minutes, but it felt much longer.

The most striking part was after the meeting. A colleague asked me, "How come I’ve never heard you go into such depth before?"

I didn't answer immediately; I just smiled. But now, sitting at home, I finally truly understand the meaning behind that question—my ability was always there. Over these past five years, there was never an issue with my professional competence; the only issue was that I never gave myself the chance to show it.

Or rather, I was always waiting for someone else to give me that chance.

It’s a bit ironic when I think about it. I spent so long doubting myself, asking "Am I just not cut out for this?", when in reality, I just hadn't tried. Or perhaps I had tried, but I gave up halfway every time—because I was afraid, because I felt it wasn't worth the risk.

Things are different now. It’s not that I’ve suddenly become fearless, but because I finally believe one thing: change really does require support. Dr. Wang’s advice, the sense of "being accepted" from Sister Li and the yoga group, even the deepening understanding between me and my parents... all of these things are quietly changing me. They’ve let me know that I’m not someone "unfit for change"; I just needed to find the right way.

And I have found it.

When I was leaving work today, my boss patted me on the shoulder and said, "Great ideas, keep it up." At that moment, I didn't feel nervous; instead, I felt a sense of calm satisfaction. It wasn't about a promotion or recognition, but because I finally believed in my own value.

It might sound simple, but for me... it’s the first time in five years I’ve truly believed it.

📸

It’s finally happening.

I’ve gone through the manual three times, with all the key points highlighted. Giving it a shot this weekend.

[Image: A side view of a camera on a desk, next to an open manual with clear highlighter marks.]

It’s a small step, but for me, it feels different.

January 19, 2023, 9:30 PM

Sitting at my desk, my hands are still shaking a little.

It’s been two hours since the session ended, and I’ve been debating whether to write this down. I’m afraid that by writing it, it becomes a "formal event," making it even more real. But I’ve decided to write anyway, because if I don’t, I’m afraid I’ll wake up tomorrow morning and convince myself that none of this ever happened.

Dr. Wang said something that I keep playing over and over in my head. She said, "Your execution isn't the problem; the pressure is."

I was stunned. Because for the last five years, ten years—maybe longer—I’ve been stuck in this cycle of self-denial. I told myself I had no discipline, that I could never stick to anything, that all those books and courses I bought but never finished were proof of my own failure. I felt so guilty about it. I felt like a failure.

But she said the pressure is the problem.

I cried in front of her. It wasn't loud sobbing; my eyes just started welling up and the tears began to fall. I didn't even realize I was crying until she handed me a tissue.

She asked me why I was crying, and I said... I don’t know. Maybe because no one has ever said that to me before. Maybe because it’s the first time I’ve heard someone say, "Maybe you aren't the one who's broken."

I’ve written it down: *Maybe I am not the one who's broken.*

But...

Now that I’m back home, sitting here alone, the doubt is creeping back in. Can this really change anything? One session, one sentence, some tears—and then what? Tomorrow I still have to go to work, I still have to face those unfinished projects, and I still have to see my former classmates' posts about promotions and kids. This doesn't change the fact that I’m 35 and single, that I haven't been promoted in five years, or that my parents are getting older.

Maybe I’m just running away. Maybe this is just psychological comfort.

But... I don’t want to let go of this feeling either. In that moment, when she said those words, I felt something. A feeling I’ve never had before—the feeling of being seen. Not judged, not told to change, just... seen.

I don’t know what this means. I don’t know what the next step is.

But at least for tonight, I’m allowing myself to believe—maybe, just maybe—that I don’t have to live my life in constant self-denial.

Maybe.

3:30 AM. Can’t sleep.

Scrolled through my phone and saw a college classmate posted more photos of their kid. Really cute. I gave it a like and put the phone down.

Lying here in the dark, my mind is still stuck on the data for that project from this afternoon. It needs revisions tomorrow. Probably more the day after.

My body is exhausted, but my brain won’t shut off. My neck hurts.

Whatever. I’m done thinking.

I’ve always felt I was too quiet, too slow to warm up to people—as if something was inherently wrong with me. But perhaps the problem isn't me; it's that I’ve been trying so hard to become the person everyone else expects me to be.

Today, Sister Li mentioned that she’s also an introvert, but she’s learned to connect in her own way. You don't have to be an extrovert or talk a lot. You just need to be there, with sincerity.

That really hit home.

Sometimes, it takes more courage to accept yourself than it does to change yourself.

I know it sounds like an exaggeration, but I couldn't stop myself from spiraling through these scenarios.

My mother said, "It’s normal at his age," but that actually made me more nervous. What does "normal" even mean? Does it mean that aging is just this irreversible decline?

I couldn't sleep last night. I just lay in bed, obsessing over everything—the eldercare plans I’ve made, the insurance comparisons, the cost projections... is it really enough? If something actually goes wrong, is everything I’ve prepared already too little, too late? I even opened up my spreadsheets and checked them again, but the act of checking just made me more anxious.

I wanted to ask: is this constant overthinking and jumping to the worst-case scenario a symptom of anxiety? Am I overreacting, or are these concerns actually rational?

I’m just so tired right now. The rational part of me is telling me to stay calm and analytical, but the other part feels like it’s screaming.

Could you help me look into this?

# Work Log - March 31, 2023

**Project Wrap-up Meeting Notes**

A few things happened during today’s project wrap-up meeting that are worth noting down.

Around 10:00 AM, the team was discussing optimization plans for the Q1 risk assessment model. At first, I did what I usually do—listened to everyone’s ideas and took notes. But when the discussion turned to data sampling methods, a potentially more efficient approach suddenly clicked: using stratified sampling combined with time-series weight adjustments. This could ensure sample representativeness while better capturing market dynamics.

I hesitated for a moment. In the past, I probably would have just scribbled it in my notebook and emailed my boss after work. But this time, I raised my hand.

To be honest, I was a bit nervous. But I stood up and laid out my idea anyway. I tried to explain the advantages with clear logic and even drew a simple flowchart on the whiteboard. I could feel my voice was steady, without that sense of insecurity I used to have.

After I finished, my boss nodded and said the idea was worth exploring further. He didn’t greenlight it on the spot, but he didn’t dismiss it either; instead, he suggested I work with the data team to run a small-scale validation. My colleague, Mei, even asked a few follow-up questions about the details and seemed genuinely interested.

After the meeting, Mei told me she thought the idea was very practical and asked if I’d been sitting on it for a while. I smiled and told her that was exactly the case.

Sitting at my desk now, I’m reflecting on this. My boss’s feedback wasn’t immediate adoption, but I find that I’m not disappointed. In fact, I feel like this is actually quite good—change isn't an all-at-once event; it’s a continuous, gradual process. I put my idea forward, it was heard, and it was respected. That in itself is a huge step forward.

*I can express myself at work without being afraid.*

I wrote that sentence on a sticky note and tucked it next to my monitor.

Maybe I’ve been underestimating my own value all along. It wasn’t a lack of ability; it was that I never truly put it on display.

I’m continuing with the design of the validation plan this afternoon. Things feel a little different now.

Dr. Wang, I wanted to share some things that have been happening lately.

Last week, when I went home for dinner, my dad asked me, "How’s work going lately?"—just a very ordinary question. But I suddenly realized that I’ve been misunderstanding him all this time. I always thought he was cold, but the truth is he’s always cared; he just doesn't know how to express it. In that moment, I felt a bit sad, but also a sense of relief.

And then there’s my mom. Out of the blue, she told me that when she was younger, she also wanted more choices, but eventually, she just accepted the life she had. I’d never heard her talk like that before. I used to think her "over-cautiousness" was a way of restricting me, but now I understand that it was her way of protecting me as best as she knew how. She wasn’t entirely satisfied with her own life either, but she’s coming to terms with it.

This has made me look at myself differently. I’ve been judging them instead of trying to understand them. I even felt guilty for being their daughter—feeling like I wasn’t outgoing enough, successful enough, or living up to their expectations. But the truth is, they never asked those things of me. I was the one demanding them of myself.

Last week, I cooked a meal for them—just simple, home-cooked food. In the past, when I cooked, my mind would be racing with thoughts like "I have to make this perfect," but this time was different. I was thinking, "I want them to feel loved." I felt so peaceful while I was doing it, even a little happy. Seeing them look so content while they ate made me feel content, too.

I’ve noticed that I’m sleeping much better lately, and I don’t get as caught up in overthinking the small things. That anxiety and self-blame haven't completely disappeared, but they feel different now. It’s not because all the problems have been solved, but because I’ve started to accept the problems themselves. I’ve accepted my parents, and I’ve accepted myself.

What you said before about "understanding doesn't mean agreeing"—I think I truly get it now. I don’t need to change them, and I don’t need to change myself to fit their mold. We are who we are, each with our own limitations, but we are loving each other in our own ways.

Thank you for being there for me during this time. These conversations have helped me see so many things I couldn't see before.

Dr. Wang, I wanted to share something with you, but I’m a bit unsure how to even start.

Last Friday afternoon, my boss suddenly messaged me saying there was an important report that needed to be finished over the weekend. My immediate reaction was that familiar sense of tension—you know, in the past, I definitely would have just said yes and spent the whole weekend glued to my computer.

But this time was different. I paused for a few minutes, took a deep breath, and typed out: "I have important personal plans this weekend. Could we adjust the deadline for this report to next Monday?"

My hands were shaking a bit after I hit send. I kept wondering if he’d be angry, if he’d think I was being unprofessional, or if it would affect my performance review. I even re-read the message several times just to make sure the wording was okay. You see, I’m still that same person who overthinks everything.

Then he just replied, "That’s fine, thanks for letting me know." That was it.

I sat there in the office, almost unable to believe it. I wrote "I am allowed to say no" on a post-it note and stared at it for a long time.

I know this might not sound like a big deal, but it felt different to me. Before, I always felt that if I refused, my professional value would drop. But now I realize that doesn't seem to be the case. My competence didn't vanish just because of one refusal, and my boss didn't write me off just because I had personal plans.

I wanted to ask you—do you think this is real change? Or did I just happen to succeed this one time, and I’ll go right back to my old patterns the next time the pressure is on? I’m a little afraid that this was just an exception.

But at the same time, I do feel a bit proud. It feels like those things we discussed about boundaries are finally actually happening in real life.

Sunday, March 10, 2024 – Sunny

I’ve been replaying yesterday afternoon’s walk in my head.

Walking around the neighborhood with my camera felt a bit awkward at first. I felt like I was doing something forced—pointing a lens at ordinary trees, pavement, and patches of light, as if I were "playing photographer." But as I kept walking, that self-consciousness began to fade.

I spent a long time under the old locust tree. Its bark was etched with deep ridges, and the light filtered through the leaves, creating tiny, scattered flecks of brightness. As I captured the scene, it suddenly hit me: I wasn’t just "taking a photo"; I was looking. Truly looking.

I’ve passed this tree countless times before, but I’ve never observed it like this. The camera gave me a reason to stop, to observe, and to wonder why these patterns of light and shadow made me feel something.

I jotted down some notes on Douban later, but those words didn't quite capture the core of it. The core realization is this: my way of thinking isn't an obstacle. I’ve always blamed myself for not being fast enough, not being decisive enough, for always overthinking. But yesterday, I understood that this tendency to "look again" and "rethink" is actually a strength. At least for this.

When I look at the world through a camera, I see more than others do. I notice the pigeon tracks on the concrete, the profile of an old woman sitting on a bench, the texture of roots breaking through the soil. These details are always there, but most people walk right past them. I see them because I stop to think.

In my five-year plan, I wrote about learning photography and using images to document my life. Yesterday, I finally realized that this isn't just a skill-based goal. It’s a way of being—a way to let my contemplative nature become a strength rather than a burden.

I don’t need to turn into a fast-paced decision-maker. I just need to find a path where my way of thinking has a purpose. The camera is that path.

This thought makes me feel light. It’s as if something has finally clicked into place.

One more detail: Sister Li asked me what I was shooting yesterday, so I showed her a few photos. She looked at them intently and asked why I chose those subjects. As I tried to explain, she listened sincerely. In that moment, I didn't feel judged; I just felt understood. It’s a somewhat foreign feeling, but a good one.

It’s Sunday morning now. I’m drinking coffee, and it’s another sunny day outside. I’m thinking about going for another walk this week and bringing the camera again.

I no longer doubt whether I can stick to this plan.

Postscript: Slept very well last night. Didn't have that usual sense of anxiety when I woke up this morning. Just a clear, calm sense of anticipation.

I used to think of it as a luxury. You’d share your thoughts, and the other person would either try to change you or just meet you with silence. Over time, you simply learn to stop speaking up.

But recently, I’ve realized that being understood is actually quite simple—it’s just having someone listen to you finish, and then say, "I get it. That works too." It’s not about agreeing with every decision you make; it’s about accepting the way you make them.

I’ve been reading *Sapiens* lately. It mentions that humans are able to cooperate because we can create shared fictions. I think being understood is, in a way, two people creating a shared "imagination"—about who each other is, and about what is possible.

I had a small moment today that made me feel this. It was a warm feeling—warm enough that I wanted to write it down.

—

Sometimes we think we have to change ourselves to be accepted, when in reality, we just need to find the people who truly see us.

# Some Random Thoughts on Japanese Cuisine—or Rather, on "Improvising"

I conducted a little experiment today.

I had originally planned to make tamagoyaki following a recipe from that *Japanese Home Cooking* book I bought a while back. But when I opened the fridge, I realized I was short on scallions and completely out of ketchup. Usually, this would frustrate me—I’d blame myself for poor planning or just give up entirely. Today, however, for some reason, I thought: why not just try it with what I have on hand?

I swapped the scallions for onions, added a splash of soy sauce and mirin, and threw in half a green pepper on a whim. I had to adjust the heat several times during the process, too—I noticed that the green pepper released more moisture, so I had to lower the flame a bit.

The result was actually... pretty good? It wasn't a "standard" tamagoyaki, but it had an unexpectedly refreshing taste.

Writing this now, I realize I’ve been judging my attempts by a very strange set of standards. It’s as if "following the plan to the letter" is the only definition of success, and any deviation is a sign of "poor execution" or "giving up halfway." But in reality, throughout today’s process, I was thinking, adjusting, and making decisions based on the actual situation—isn't that also a form of "completion"?

I’ve bought so many cookbooks and signed up for so many classes in the past, yet I never stuck with any of them. I used to beat myself up over it. But thinking about it now, maybe the problem wasn't that I wasn't doing well enough, but that my definition of "doing" was too narrow. I viewed it as a goal that *must* be achieved, rather than a process that can be adjusted and enjoyed at any time.

So, I decided to document today’s tamagoyaki. Not to prove anything, but just to remind myself—sometimes, "going off-script" isn't a failure; it might actually be a form of creativity.

I’ll try it again next time. It doesn't have to be perfect; I just want to see what else I can play around with.

# The Trap of Reflection

I was re-reading a chapter of *Atomic Habits* recently when I realized something ironic—I’ve probably spent more time thinking about *why* I can’t stick to my habits than actually doing them.

The plan I made two months ago feels like a distant memory now. It’s not that I’ve completely given up; it’s just that work has been busier than expected, and by the time I get home, I’m drained. Then I fall into this loop: failing to follow the plan → self-blame → overanalyzing the reasons → more self-blame → continued inaction.

The most painful part is that I even bought several books just to "understand my executive dysfunction." And of course, I haven't finished those books either. That, in itself, is the best proof of my point.

I saw someone on Douban share a quote: "Excessive introspection is just another form of procrastination." I sat at my desk for a long time after reading that. It hit home because that’s exactly what I’ve been doing—using thinking as a substitute for action, and reflection as a substitute for change.

Last night, I spent an hour tweaking a single sentence in my journal, trying to find the most precise language to describe my "failure." I must have rewritten it a dozen times. It wasn't until I finished that I realized this obsession with wording was the problem itself.

Maybe the issue isn't that I lack willpower. Maybe my understanding of "willpower" is what's flawed. Or rather, I’ve turned it into another form of perfectionism—either I stick to the plan perfectly, or it’s a total failure.

I wonder if it’s just me.

Anyway, I’m going for a walk. I’ve realized lately that instead of getting tangled up in my own head, just moving for a bit actually helps me see things more clearly.

Mom’s letter is still sitting on my desk. I’ve read it so many times now, and every time, I find myself lingering on that one sentence: "I am proud of you."

I don’t know how to describe the feeling. It’s as if something that has been weighing on my chest for years suddenly let go today.

When Mom called this afternoon, my voice was trembling a little. She asked if I was crying; I told her no, but the tears were already falling. This time was different, though. In the past, my tears were born of anxiety, self-blame, and the feeling that I wasn’t good enough. These tears... were of gratitude. Of relief. I can’t even explain exactly why I wanted to cry; I just suddenly did.

I said something to her that surprised even me. I said, "Mom, I understand now. You’ve always loved me in the only way you knew how."

As I said it, I felt like I was forgiving someone, but it wasn’t exactly forgiveness. It was more like... finally seeing clearly. All that "be careful, don't cause trouble," all those things I used to find suffocating—I can see what was behind them now. It wasn't restriction; it was protection. It was love, given to the best of her ability within her own limitations.

Mom was silent on the phone for a moment, then she said, "You’ve grown up."

Such a simple sentence, but it made me cry again.

I’ve been thinking these past few days: why do I fall into self-blame so easily? Why do I always feel like I’m not good enough? I think it’s because I’ve been measuring myself by everyone else’s yardsticks. Zhang Hao wanted me to be more extroverted, so I saw my introversion as a flaw. My classmates all got promoted, so I felt like I was stagnating. Mom was cautious and conservative, so I felt restricted.

But now... I don’t know, it feels different.

My life isn't a "failed version" of something else. It is simply my life. I chose stability because stability matters to me. I chose solitude because I found peace in it. I chose this job because it allows me to focus on what I’m actually good at. None of these were "settling"; they were my choices.

Sister Li mentioned at yoga class that I look different lately, that my "vibe" has changed. I asked her how, and she couldn't quite put it into words, just that I seemed more relaxed. Maybe she’s right. Maybe I’ve finally stopped trying so hard to be who everyone else expects me to be.

I haven’t started the book Mom sent yet, but I’m looking forward to it. I imagine her picking it out back home, imagine her writing those words in the letter, character by character...

I feel very peaceful right now. Not a numb kind of peace, but a true one. Like someone finally being able to set down a heavy load and take a deep breath.

I still have to go to work tomorrow, still have to deal with those data sets and reports. But it feels different. Or rather, I am different.

I think I’m finally ready to embrace my life. Not to change it, but to truly embrace it.

# [Update] Japanese Tamagoyaki - Some thoughts on heat control and mindset

Last week's post was a bit rushed. I made it again today and wanted to add some details.

**On Heat Control:**
I didn't explain this in detail before, but the key to Tamagoyaki is the heat. I use medium-low heat; you can't rush it. When pouring the first layer of egg liquid, wait until the bottom is semi-set before rolling. If the heat is too high, it’s easy to burn the outside while leaving the inside raw. I use a Japanese square pan, which conducts heat evenly; if you're using a regular round pan, you might need to adjust the timing.

**On Choosing Soy Sauce:**
This time I used *Usukuchi* (light color) soy sauce instead of *Koikuchi* (dark). The resulting color is lighter, though the saltiness is actually about the same. I looked it up, and traditional recipes do tend to use light soy sauce more often. I missed this in the previous post, so I’m adding it now.

**On Nutrition:**
One serving of Tamagoyaki (4-5 rolls) contains about 8g of protein and 6g of fat. It’s a solid protein source for a bento box.

This was completely different from how it was with Zhang Hao. He wanted me to change myself to fit him. But today, it was because I am who I am—quiet, meticulous, with a slightly quirky way of observing things—that they saw what I saw.

On the way home, I kept thinking about what happened to me, from that initial anxiety to the eventual immersion. I was proactively pointing out details, discussing composition with everyone, and I even cracked a dry joke about photography (even though no one laughed, haha). Was that me? Or have I always been this way, just never having the chance to show it?

Dr. Wang once said, "Change isn't about overthrowing who you are; it's about finding the right environment to express yourself." I think I finally understand what that means today.

I haven't turned into an extrovert. I’m still that quiet person. But I know now that my quietness isn't a flaw. My power of observation, my meticulousness, my obsession with detail—these are my strengths. It’s just that I’ve spent so much time apologizing for what I lack that I failed to see what I already have.

Tomorrow, I want to look through today’s photos again. Not to be self-critical, but to confirm—that the things I see are truly worth being seen.

I think I’m finally starting to believe it: change really is possible. Not by becoming someone else, but by becoming a more complete version of myself.

Dr. Wang, there’s something I want to talk to you about.

A colleague invited me to lunch today—the female coworker who’s always very friendly. She asked, "Do you want to go together?" I was looking at my emails at the time, and I didn't even look up at her. I just said, "No, thanks." Just that one simple sentence.

She said, "Okay," and her voice sounded a bit disappointed. I could feel it. But I just... I didn't have the energy to deal with anything social. Even a simple lunch felt like too much work.

Now I’m sitting in the office, eating a convenience store rice ball by myself, and I keep replaying that moment in my head. I’m blaming myself—why did I treat her like that? She was actually being kind. But at the same time, I’m thinking that I can’t change; this is just who I am. Cold, rude, and disappointing.

It made me realize something. It’s not just that I’m tired; it’s like I’m... systematically isolating myself. I’m avoiding everyone—colleagues, friends, even neighbors. I’ve even started avoiding Sister Li’s yoga invitations. I use "being too tired" as an excuse to push everyone away.

But when I do that, I feel guilty. So I get stuck in this cycle—avoidance, guilt, self-blame, and then further isolation.

I know this isn't healthy. I know I probably need to do something about it. But I’m not even sure if counseling can help me, because the problem seems to be me. I’m just someone who can’t change. I can’t even stick to a meal plan, so how could I possibly change my entire personality?

I don’t know what I’m writing. I just... needed to tell you.

Can we add an extra session this week?

March 10, 2023, 8:00 PM

I did something today that I can hardly believe.

After yoga class this afternoon, I took the initiative to give Sister Chen my WeChat. I’m a bit surprised at myself—it’s not like me at all. Usually, I’d wait for the other person to speak first, or I wouldn't ask for contact info at all. But today, I just did it.

I was actually really nervous during class. Before going, I went back and forth at home about whether to even show up, but I eventually went. While putting on my yoga gear, I kept worrying that I’d look awkward if I couldn't nail a pose. But once class started, I realized it wasn't scary at all. Everyone was just doing their own thing; if someone couldn't get a pose right, they’d just have a little laugh at themselves, and nobody cared. One older lady even blurted out, "Oh, these old bones of mine," during a move, and we all laughed.

In that moment, I suddenly relaxed. No one was judging who was better, and there was no sense of competition. It just felt… safe?

Sister Chen chatted with me after class and asked if it was my first time. I said yes. She welcomed me to come back often and said their little group is all about relaxing, no pressure. We talked a bit more, and she mentioned she’d be back next week and asked if I wanted to add her on WeChat to stay in touch.

Without overthinking, I said yes. And then I actually added her.

Now I’m feeling a bit of "after-fear." I’m wondering, is this a real change? Or was it just a momentary impulse? Will I find an excuse not to go next week? Or give up after two or three sessions?

But there’s another voice saying—maybe not. Maybe this time is different.

My neck does feel a bit better. The physical change is real. But what about the mental side? Can I really stick with it?

I don’t know. But at least today, I did something I wouldn't normally do. That’s always worth recording, right?

I’ll see how I feel tomorrow.

# Diary

**December 22, 2023, 8:00 PM**

Sister Li and the others just left. I’m sitting on the sofa, still finding it hard to believe what just happened.

To be honest, I was quite nervous this afternoon. Taking the initiative to invite everyone over to watch a documentary—that’s something the "old me" would have found impossible. I even revised my WeChat message several times, terrified that I’d sound stiff or forced. But in the end, I sent it anyway. Maybe it’s because lately, I’ve really felt a need to share things with them.

After we finished the documentary based on *Sapiens*, we sat in the living room chatting. When Sister Li started talking about life choices, I suddenly blurted out: "No life is perfect; what matters is finding a life that is meaningful to you." I surprised myself the moment I said it, because that is truly what I believe now.

I used to scroll through my old classmates' Moments, seeing their promotions, marriages, and babies, and constantly asking myself why I was still standing still. Now I finally understand that the comparison itself is absurd. Sister Li’s life and my life are completely different, but that doesn't mean one is better or worse than the other.

What touched me most was when Sister Li took my hand before leaving and said, "We’re all better people for knowing you." My eyes welled up. Those words meant the world to me because I’ve always seen myself as someone who is "bad at socializing and has nothing to offer." But maybe I was wrong. Maybe my way is just *my* way—I don’t need to be that bubbly, go-getter type. I can be the one who listens intently, thinks deeply, and says something meaningful at the right moment.

I’m now able to organize activities on my own. It sounds like a small thing, but for me, it’s a huge breakthrough. It’s not because I’ve suddenly become an extrovert, but because I’m not afraid anymore. I’m not afraid of being rejected, I’m not afraid of not being "good enough," and I no longer need everyone to like me.

Sitting here in this quiet living room, I feel a sense of contentment I’ve never known before. It’s not an excited or flashy kind of satisfaction, but something deep and grounded. It feels as if I’ve finally found where I belong.

I remember Dr. Wang once asking me, "What do you feel you are missing?" Back then, I couldn't find the words. Now I know—I wasn't missing anything; I just needed to be seen. And now, I have been seen.

# WeChat Chat with Sister Li

**Morning, Sister Li! Yesterday’s photo walk was so much fun.**

I was actually a bit nervous at first, worried I wouldn’t be able to capture anything good. But then I realized I didn’t need to take "professional" photos; just using the camera to observe my surroundings made everything feel completely different.

**For example, that little alley I pass by every day—yesterday, I suddenly noticed the moss on the walls and the way the light filtered through.** It wasn't until I took the shot that I realized I’d never actually looked at those things so closely before.

After I got back, I jotted down some notes on Douban. I felt a little self-conscious while writing, thinking maybe I was over-analyzing things, haha. But you know what? Through the lens, I’ve started to see the world in a different way. It sounds a bit dramatic, but that’s truly how it feels.

**Do you have any creative hobbies like this?** I mean, besides yoga. You seem so passionate about so many things. If you have any advice, I’d love to hear it.

Actually, this is one of the things I want to take seriously this year. Not just photography, but also documenting my feelings while traveling. I’ve bought so many camera tutorials in the past that just sat there, but this time feels different.

**Thank you so much for inviting me on that walk.** Truly.

# Diary

**Saturday, June 29, 2024 – Sunny**

I just got back from the neighborhood, still in my walking clothes. I’m sitting on the sofa, scrolling through the photos I took today, looking at them over and over.

I still can’t quite believe it.

This was the third Photo Walk. I’ve actually organized three of these. And... I wasn’t nervous or awkward at all. When Sister Li introduced me, saying, "This is Xiaoyu, she’s the one who started this," I just naturally gave a brief introduction, explained today’s route and the shooting spots. Then we started walking and chatting. Chatting. I was chatting with a group of near-strangers, and it felt... comfortable.

It’s so strange.

Two months ago, if someone had asked me to organize an event, I would have definitely said, "No, I’m not good at that." Then I would have come up with a bunch of excuses—I’m not social, I get nervous easily, I have no leadership skills. But now... I’m doing it. And I’m doing it well.

Sister Li mentioned that someone suggested organizing other activities, like a movie night or something. Then, it just slipped out—"I could host a cooking party! I’ve been learning Japanese cuisine lately." I only realized after I said it that it was my own proactive idea. It wasn’t passive acceptance or forcing myself; I actually wanted to do it.

Dr. Wang once asked me where the label "lack of execution" came from. I told her it was because I’d signed up for so many courses I never finished and bought so many books I never read. She asked, "What about the Photo Walks?" I was speechless. She’s right... from the initial idea to implementation, I’ve stuck with it three times. People show up every time, and I have new ideas every time. It’s not a lack of execution; it’s just that... I hadn’t found the right thing.

On the way home, it suddenly hit me: maybe this five-year career plateau wasn't a tragedy at all. I spent all that time waiting for a promotion, waiting for recognition, as if my value could only be proven through work. But now I have photography, this little community, and my connections with Sister Li and the yoga group. I’m creating something. Not for a promotion, not to prove myself, but just... because I want to.

This feeling is so unfamiliar.

When I saw Mom last time, she asked again when I was going to find a boyfriend. I didn’t feel oppressed like I usually do. Instead, I thought—maybe my life is fulfilling enough as it is. It’s not that I’ve given up on finding a relationship, but I no longer feel like life has to follow that specific order. I have friends now, I have interests, and I have things I’m building. That in itself is pretty great.

It’s just... I’m still a little afraid this feeling will disappear. Afraid that no one will show up to the next Photo Walk, afraid that I’ll slip back into being that quiet, passive Lin Xiaoyu.

But I’m trying not to think that way. I’ve already proven that I can do this. It wasn’t a fluke; it really happened.

I’m starting to plan the cooking party next week. Just thinking about it makes me a little excited.

Accepting yourself is the best way to start.

There's no need to become who others expect you to be. I am enough.

Hi Sister Li, thank you so much for checking in on me today.

I’m actually okay, just a bit worn out lately with too much on my mind. I’m really sorry for what happened in class... I didn't mean to be a total buzzkill.

Everyone was enjoying the meditation so much, and then there was me... Anyway, I’ve just been under a lot of pressure recently. Work stuff, some family things—nothing major, just everything hitting me at once.

Please don't think I don't want to come to yoga. It’s actually one of the few times I can truly relax these days. I was just in a bad headspace today. I should be feeling better by next week.

Thanks again. I feel really lucky to have a neighbor like you who genuinely cares.

# January 6, 2024, Evening

Sister Li said I "don't seem as anxious anymore; I look calmer and more content."

When I heard her say that, I was in the kitchen, holding a chef’s knife and chopping onions. I paused for a moment, didn't respond immediately, and just smiled. But in that instant, I felt something settle gently in my heart.

This was the first time someone had said it so directly. It wasn't just a vague feeling I’d been trying to confirm in my own mind; it was a fact witnessed and voiced by another person.

We were making Japanese food together in the kitchen—Sister Li wanted to learn that potato curry I’ve been researching lately. I shared some tips quite naturally: things about the heat, the order of seasoning, and even a little trick I’d picked up from a video. I didn't rehearse the words in my head several times before speaking like I used to; I just said them. And as I spoke, I felt relaxed.

Later, when we all sat down to eat, Sister Li made that comment. She said it so naturally, as if she were simply stating the obvious.

I think she might be right. Over the past few months, something really has been changing. It’s not that I’ve suddenly become an extrovert or anything—I’m still me, I still prefer quiet, and I’m still not great at initiating social things. But... the anxiety really does seem to have lessened. That thought running in the background, the one about not being good enough, doesn't pop up nearly as often now.

I actually took the lead in setting a time for our next get-together. I surprised myself when I heard the words come out—usually, I’m the one passively waiting for an invite or being dragged along by Sister Li. This time, I was the one who said, "Let’s make grilled fish together next time; I’ve been wanting to try that lately."

Sometimes when I’m sitting on my yoga mat, I still think about what Zhang Hao said—about me being too introverted and not social enough. But it feels different now. I no longer see it as a flaw. I’m just... different. And that difference, in the right environment and with the right people, actually becomes a strength. I can listen intently, and I can feel a deep connection in moments of silence.

Sister Li, the friends from the yoga group, Dr. Wang... none of them tried to change me. They simply accepted me, and within that acceptance, I began to change on my own.

Looking back, the process was actually quite simple. I always thought change required some kind of heroic effort, that I had to force myself to become someone else. But in reality, change happened when I stopped trying to force it.

I have to go back to the office tomorrow. Lately, work hasn't felt as high-pressure either. I no longer view a promotion as a verdict on my self-worth. I’m just doing what I need to do, doing it well enough, and then there’s the rest of my life waiting for me. Thinking this way actually makes the work itself feel clearer.

I should go take a shower. But before that, I want to sit here for a little longer—just sitting quietly, letting this feeling linger.

It feels so good to be seen.

[Image: A blurred corner of a balcony at home, sunset glowing over a yoga mat, with an open book resting beside it.]

Sometimes, being understood is enough.

My mom told me she’s happy to see me like this and that she’s proud of me. I just sat there, not knowing what to say, feeling my eyes well up.

I guess this is how change happens—quietly. It’s not some grand spectacle, nor does everything transform all at once. It’s just a certain afternoon, a certain sentence, that suddenly makes you realize—you’ve been changing all along.

Thank you to those who are willing to truly see me.

September 21, 2022, 11:45 PM

It’s this time again. The only sounds left in the office are mine and the security guard’s footsteps downstairs.

My boss’s email is still flickering on the screen—"How’s the bug fix coming along? Need an update tomorrow." I’ve been staring at it for ten minutes now, still haven't replied. It’s not that I don’t want to, it’s just... I really don’t know what to say. I’ve been working on it all day, and there are still issues. I was so careful, I checked the code three times, yet something is still wrong. When will this finally be resolved?

My fingers hovered over the keyboard for a moment, but in the end, I just closed the email. I’ll deal with it tomorrow. I’m not going to finish it today anyway.

A moment ago, I typed "sleeping pills" into the search bar. Honestly, I just wanted to see if there was a way to fall asleep faster. Lately, I haven't been leaving the office until this time every day. By the time I get home and shower, it’s past midnight, and then I lie in bed for another hour or two before I can actually drift off. My head is just full of code, emails, and... other things.

I clicked onto a shopping site and looked at the descriptions for a few types of sleeping pills. In the end, I didn't buy any. My mom’s voice echoed in my head—"Don't go taking random pills, they all have side effects." Forget it, just forget it.

I saw a post from an old college classmate on my feed again today. Another promotion. She joined her company even later than I did. I’ve been here for five years, and I’m still in the same spot. My boss says my performance is "stable and reliable"—it sounds so sarcastic. Stable just means stagnant, doesn't it?

That Japanese cookbook I bought the day before yesterday is still on the coffee table, untouched. I failed again. One more time. Same with those video courses; I downloaded half of them and then gave up. Why can everyone else stick with things while I... I’m truly useless. Every time I tell myself "this time will be different," and what happens? It’s the same. Maybe it’s just because I’m too busy with work; maybe things will get better once the rush is over. But when will I ever not be busy?

My neck is starting to hurt again. I rubbed it, feeling how incredibly stiff it is. I should see a doctor, but when would I have the time?

It’s 11:45 PM now. I should pack up and go home. But I’m still here, alone in an empty office.

Am I really just incapable of changing anything?
