# Saturday, June 3, 2023 Sunny

I’m still processing what happened yesterday afternoon.

That working mom—the one in the yoga group who’s always sharing parenting tips—suddenly looked at me and said, "Your face looks so much more relaxed, and there’s a light in your eyes." I was stunned. She went on to say that the last time she saw me, I looked exhausted, but these past few weeks have been noticeably different.

I didn't know how to respond. I just smiled and said maybe it’s because I’ve been sleeping better lately. But in reality, I’ve been turning those words over and over in my mind.

A light in my eyes? It sounds like some kind of grand compliment, but to me, it wasn’t about my appearance or any achievement. What she saw was... something internal that has changed. And that change was witnessed.

It reminds me of yesterday afternoon when I sat on the sofa for nearly 20 minutes, debating whether to go to the outdoor activity. I kept asking myself, "Do you actually want to go? Or are you just forcing yourself again?" The old me probably would have found an excuse to decline. But this time, I didn't. I just put on my sneakers and walked out the door.

It’s not that I’ve suddenly become an extrovert. No. I was still a bit nervous when I got there, and I still spent more time listening than talking. But I went. And... I *wanted* to go. The shift is subtle, but it’s real.

Dr. Wang once said that change doesn't have to be dramatic. Sometimes it’s just this—a relaxed face, a light in the eyes, choosing to participate rather than being forced to. Others can see it, and you can feel it yourself.

I wonder if this is what they mean by "being seen." Not being seen through, not being judged, but just... truly seen. Sister Li never told me how I should be; she just accepted me as I am, and slowly, I’ve started to believe in myself.

Another thought keeps circling in my head: maybe many of the judgments I’ve had about myself were wrong. I said I lacked willpower, yet I’ve stuck with yoga. I said I wasn't cut out for socializing, yet I’ve found a sense of comfort in this group. Maybe the problem was never me, but that I was always in the wrong environment, measuring myself by the wrong standards.

I’m going home to see my parents tomorrow. I want to see if they notice anything different. Maybe I should share these feelings with them, instead of just sitting through dinner like I usually do.

It’s morning now, and the sunlight is beautiful. I’ve decided to keep up with my morning yoga meditation today.

I’m a little tired, but it’s the good kind of tired.

Hi Sister Li, are you there?

I want to try a different way.

What I really want to capture are just very ordinary moments. Like the way the morning sun spills through the stairwell, the view of my parents’ backs while they’re cooking in the kitchen, and... the way everyone in our yoga group looks when they’re laughing. These images are all in my head right now, but I always feel like words can’t quite describe that feeling.

I’ve been looking at some cameras online, and I’m a bit torn between buying one right now or thinking about it a bit longer. You know how I am—I usually have to overthink things forever before making a decision. But this time feels different. I’m not struggling with "whether I should change myself," but rather really thinking about "what way I want to record the things I want to record." It feels different.

I wanted to talk to you about it—not necessarily for advice, but just... I wanted to share this thought with someone who understands me.

# Care Plan for Dad

**Date Created:** April 8, 2023
**Update Cycle:** Review every Monday; adjust based on doctor’s recommendations

---

## I. Daily Living Assistance

**Morning Routine (6:30 AM – 7:30 AM)**
- [ ] Prepare Dad’s clothes (avoid styles that require excessive bending)
- [ ] Assist with washing up (watch for slowed movements due to back pain)
- [ ] Remind him to take medication before breakfast
- **Note:** Observe for any signs of suppressed pain in his facial expressions. Do not ask him directly, but keep an eye on his pace of movement.

**Evening Routine (9:00 PM – 10:00 PM)**
- [ ] Assist with changing into pajamas
- [ ] Confirm bed comfort (pillows, lumbar support)
- [ ] Final medication reminder
- [ ] Check bedroom lighting and placement of emergency items

---

## II. Medication Schedule

| Time | Medication | Dosage | Notes |
|------|------------|--------|-------|
| 7:00 AM | Painkiller | 1 pill | Take after breakfast |
| 12:00 PM | Anti-inflammatory | 1 pill | After lunch |
| 6:00 PM | Muscle Relaxant | 1 pill | After dinner |
| 9:00 PM | Painkiller | 1 pill | 30 mins before bed |

**Reminder Methods:** Phone alarm + paper list posted on the refrigerator
**Important:** If Dad mentions the pain is worsening, immediately record the time and symptoms to inform the doctor at the next follow-up.

---

## III. Physical Therapy Schedule

**Appointment Info:**
- Hospital: [TBD]
- Doctor: [TBD]
- Frequency: ___ times per week
- Time: _______________

**Pre-appointment Prep:**
- [ ] Remind Dad about the treatment one day in advance
- [ ] Prepare comfortable clothes for going out
- [ ] Budget extra travel time (do not rush)
- [ ] Bring medical records and insurance card

---

## IV. Other Daily Activities Requiring Assistance

- **Stairs:** Needs support; moves very slowly—do not rush him.
- **Bathing:** May need non-slip mats and grab bars for assistance.
- **Toileting:** Observe for difficulties; install toilet safety rails if necessary.
- **Cooking:** Temporarily handled by Mom or me; Dad should avoid standing for long periods.
- **Housework:** He is not allowed to perform any movements involving bending or twisting.

---

## V. Emotional Observation Checklist

- [ ] Is Dad feeling down due to the pain?
- [ ] Is there an increase in complaining or silence?
- [ ] Is he proactively communicating with Mom or me?
- [ ] Sleep quality (is he waking up during the night due to pain?)

**Note:** Dad isn't one to express himself, so I need to watch for subtle changes. If he becomes more withdrawn or irritable, it likely means the pain has intensified.

---

## VI. Key Points for Doctor Communication

To ask during the next follow-up:
- [ ] Severity and long-term prognosis of the lumbar disc herniation
- [ ] Whether further tests or treatments are needed
- [ ] Specific restrictions on daily activities
- [ ] Whether medication needs adjustment
- [ ] Expected duration of physical therapy

---

## VII. Work Adjustment Plan

**Items to Discuss with Supervisor:**
- Request flexible hours ___ days a week (for medical appointments and caregiving)
- Potential dates for remote work
- Leave procedure for emergencies

**Timeline:**
- Complete communication with supervisor by this Friday
- Prepare a specific work adjustment proposal

---

## VIII. Emergency Contacts

- Hospital ER: 
- Doctor’s Phone: 
- Mom’s Mobile: 
- My Mobile:

qa_items.question: Xiaoyu, over the past two months, I’ve watched you go from being eager to find a new job to now seemingly having second thoughts. How do you feel your mindset toward "changing your current situation" has shifted?
qa_items.answer: Well... how should I put this? Honestly, it’s been pretty discouraging. I’ve actually thought about quitting over the past two months—I even wrote it down in my notes—but in the end, I just quietly deleted it. Every time I open a job site and look at the requirements, I fall into this deep sense of self-doubt. I keep thinking, "I won't be able to land anything anyway; this is just who I am." This constant tug-of-war between wanting to escape and feeling powerless to change anything is exhausting. I’ve even started to feel like maybe this is just how my life is going to be, and I’ll never really have a breakthrough.

qa_items.answer: Sigh, it feels like being a frog in slowly heating water. It’s like 35 is this hurdle I just can’t clear. Then the pressure at work ramped up, my neck started aching intensely, and those Japanese cooking books and courses I bought but never touched just sit there, reminding me of my failure every single day. Now, looking at my boss’s email, my mind is a total blank. I know I should reply, but I can’t even find the strength to type a single word.

One time, I had even changed into my workout gear, but after talking to myself in the mirror for a long while, I ended up quietly changing back into my pajamas. Since that failed attempt to fix my burnout by changing my lifestyle, my tolerance for my current situation has truly hit rock bottom.

Actually, this state of mind started back when the department was restructured last year. Although my boss has always valued my ability to handle complex models, that kind of "predictable to the very end" stability has instead made me feel suffocated. At 3:00 AM the other night, I actually wrote "I should quit" in my notes app, but after typing it, I realized that running away won't solve anything, so I just deleted it in despair. This feeling of being useless, yet having no idea how to change—this sheer powerlessness—is really starting to crush me.

qa_items.answer: I'm ashamed to admit that I used to be the kind of person who had to mentally rehearse entering a yoga studio five times, terrified that someone would notice if I didn't do a pose perfectly. But eventually, I realized that anxiety was just me fighting with myself. Over the past few months in the yoga group, the acceptance I’ve felt from Sister Li and the others made me realize that I don’t need to become a social butterfly; I just need to allow myself to exist in whatever way feels most comfortable. Since bringing this mindset into my work, I’ve found that when I stop obsessing over how others perceive me and focus instead on whether the work is being done right, I actually speak with more confidence. The moment my boss praised me, I finally started to believe that I, too, deserve to be seen.

evidence.statement: Xiaoyu tried to manage her anxiety by searching for 'yoga tips for beginners,' but the act only intensified her unease—the more she learned, the more she realized how much she lacked.

choices.text: Actually, I have my solo hiking trip to Dali to thank for this. I got lost in the mountains back then with no cell service, which forced me to step up and talk to local villagers to ask for directions and even arrange a place to stay. The "sink or swim" pressure of that extreme environment completely stripped away my social awkwardness. After I returned, I realized that if I wasn't even afraid of being lost in the wilderness, a little disagreement in a meeting room was nothing. That renewed sense of confidence in the world and in my own ability to survive has become the source of my backbone today.

Actually, it’s because I signed up for a three-month online bootcamp called "Workplace Influence." I used to think communication was all about natural talent, but the mentors there taught us to build a "result-oriented" mental model. I started trying to write out a logical framework before every speech and practiced my eye contact repeatedly in front of a mirror. When I first tried that structured way of expressing myself at a weekly meeting and saw the director nod in approval, I suddenly realized that workplace confidence can actually be broken down into specific, learnable skills. This sense of control has allowed me to completely move past my old hesitation and shrinking back.

I’m ashamed to admit that I used to be the kind of person who had to mentally rehearse five times just to step into a yoga studio, terrified that someone would notice if I did a pose wrong. But eventually, I realized that anxiety was just me fighting with myself. Over the past few months in the yoga group, the way Sister Li and the others accepted me made me realize that I don’t need to become a social butterfly; I just need to allow myself to exist in whatever way feels most comfortable. Once I brought this mindset into my work, I found that when I stopped obsessing over "how others see me" and focused instead on "doing the right thing," I actually spoke with more confidence. The moment my boss praised me, I finally truly believed that I, too, deserve to be seen.

This is mainly thanks to the "daily review" habit I’ve stuck with since last year. I kept a dedicated notebook to record one small thing I did well and one moment where I spoke up courageously each day. In the beginning, I’d write down things as simple as "initiated a greeting with the receptionist." Gradually, I noticed the entries piling up, and this visual representation of my progress provided a powerful psychological boost. Later, during project debriefs, I tried replicating the communication techniques I had documented, and the feedback was excellent. This accumulation of positive reinforcement finally gave me the confidence to speak up candidly on much larger occasions.

qa_items.question: Xiaoyu, I remember you mentioning how stiff and formal things always felt when talking to your parents. But last month, you managed to respond so calmly when your mother questioned your yoga practice. It feels like your way of expressing yourself has become much more assertive than before, hasn't it?
qa_items.answer: You caught me—honestly, I was pretty surprised by myself that day too. In the past, if my mom questioned me, I probably would have spiraled into self-doubt or kept trying to explain myself. But that day, I suddenly remembered what my neighbor, Sister Li, said about "letting things be," so I just told her straight up that I was doing this for my own mental and physical well-being. Later, I even started proactively sharing stories about my yoga group over the phone instead of just passively listening to them. That feeling of finally being "seen" and understood by my parents has really given my confidence a huge boost.

Now, I’m no longer worried about them not understanding, because we’ve reached a common ground on the concept of "self-regulation."

Being able to openly discuss my hobbies like that made me feel like the wall between us had finally started to crack, and our communication has felt much more natural since then.

qa_items.question: Xiaoyu, looking back on your experiences lately, do you think your previous urge to "always avoid socializing" was because you truly didn't need it, or because you just hadn't found the "right environment"? How would you define your "comfort zone" now?
qa_items.answer: Well... how should I put this? I used to think I was a total island, but sticking with this yoga group lately has made me realize that it’s not that I don’t need social interaction—it’s that I’m terrified of environments filled with competition and judgment. I’ve come to feel that a "comfort zone" doesn't necessarily have to mean hiding at home alone; it can be any place where I feel safe and don't have to force myself to act like an extrovert. Like in yoga class, where everyone just says a simple hello and no one pressures me to change my personality. This feeling of being "seen" without being demanded of is actually what gives me the motivation to get out of the house.

Now, I feel that a "comfort zone" is no longer a physical location, but a collaborative relationship. As long as everyone is working toward the same objective, I don't find socializing to be a burden; instead, it gives me a real sense of accomplishment.

I used to think that solitude was the key to peak productivity, until I started working in "populated yet quiet" environments like cafes or shared bookstores. I’ve realized that I don’t actually dislike socializing; I just hate aimless small talk. It’s like being in a bookstore where everyone is reading their own thing without interruption—this kind of "weak tie" socialization is actually where I feel most relaxed, and it’s how I truly regain a sense of control over my life.

qa_items.question: Over the past six months, you’ve noticed that you’ve not only become more outgoing in your yoga group, but you’re also able to express yourself more honestly at work and with your parents. Do you feel that this sense of "being seen" has helped you overcome the struggles you used to have with "lack of follow-through"?
qa_items.answer: Well... how should I put it? It’s a truly strange and wonderful feeling. I used to think my lack of execution was just down to laziness or a lack of willpower. But looking back, it might have been because I was constantly forcing myself to do things I "should" do rather than things I actually enjoyed. Since being so naturally accepted by Sister Li and the others in the yoga group, I’ve realized that when I’m no longer afraid of being seen or so tense about avoiding mistakes, I actually have more drive to stick with something. For instance, I’ve kept up with yoga for six months now, and I even found the courage to point out a model flaw during a meeting—something that would have been unthinkable before. This positive feedback has made me realize that I actually do have the ability to change and follow through; I just needed a way of doing things that actually fits who I am.

qa_items.question: Xiaoyu, I was just thinking—if you had actually given in that night before yoga class and made up some excuse about being sick to Sister Li just so you could slack off at home, how different do you think your life would be right now?
qa_items.answer: To be honest, I had already typed out the draft on my phone and was a split second away from hitting send. If I had actually bailed that day, I think I’d still be curled up in my solitary "safety shell," living a life where I could see exactly how everything would end. Even though taking that step gave me such bad anxiety that I couldn't sleep all night—and I was even obsessing over which pants made me look thinner—it was that one moment of "not having the nerve to lie" that made me realize being welcomed by others is actually quite warm. It wasn't nearly as terrifying as I’d imagined.

qa_items.question: Overall, how do you view the relationship between "introversion" and "socializing" now? Are you still that person who is afraid of change?
qa_items.answer: Well... how should I put this? I used to think the two were like oil and water, as if socializing meant I had to "force" myself to be an extrovert. But lately, I’ve realized that in the right environment—like in my yoga group or when I’m with Sister Li—I don’t have to pretend at all. Just being there quietly feels perfectly comfortable. As for change, I’m not that afraid of it anymore. I’ve come to realize that change doesn't have to be some grand overhaul. Finding a "system" that works for me, even through the smallest attempts, makes me feel seen and needed. It’s actually a pretty warm feeling.

qa_items.question: Xiaoyu, after everything you’ve been through, if a friend came to you today who was just as afraid of socializing and worried about being judged as you used to be, what advice would you give her?

But later, I realized that when you’re in a low-pressure environment—like the yoga group I joined—and you feel that sense of "it’s okay, take your time," your tension just naturally melts away. You don’t have to force yourself to be a social butterfly. Just try shifting your focus from "how others see me" to "how I can make others feel comfortable." You’ll find that the warmth of being accepted has always been there; you just need to take that tiny step forward to feel it.

I just need to find a system where I feel safe and be with people who truly see me.

In that moment, I suddenly realized that when I stop overthinking things in isolation and put myself in a supportive environment instead, those voices of self-doubt get a lot quieter. Now, I’m more willing to give things a try. Even if the result isn't perfect, at least I got it done—and that feels so much better than hiding away.

It’s this small sense of security that gave me the courage to try and find my voice at work. If I had stayed curled up at home and never taken that step, I’d probably still be that person sitting in the corner of the meeting room, never saying a word.

qa_items.question: I've noticed that you seem to spend a lot of time preparing before trying out new social activities, sometimes even losing sleep over rehearsing scenarios in your head. Does this habit of needing to be in total control of every detail make you feel a bit safer when facing unfamiliar environments?
qa_items.answer: You caught me. I’m embarrassed to admit it, but I really do have this kind of "pre-social anxiety." Like that time I went to Sister Li’s yoga class—I knew perfectly well that everyone was nice, but the night before, I still couldn't stop myself from mentally rehearsing every little detail, from rolling out my mat to saying hello. I even went as far as searching for beginner tips, which only made me feel more inadequate the more I read. For me, if I can't anticipate every possible awkward moment, I just feel unsettled. Even though I know this so-called "sense of control" usually just ends up wearing me out.

qa_items.question: Xiaoyu, seeing how you’ve gone from being socially avoidant to even organizing your own gatherings over the past year, what would you say are the three core elements that must be present for a social circle to feel truly grounded and safe for you now?
qa_items.answer: Well... how should I put it? The experiences I’ve had lately have truly given me a new understanding of myself. I think the most crucial part is the "lack of forced change"—just like with Sister Li and the yoga group, no one demanded that I become an extrovert. Secondly, it’s the "zero-competition atmosphere"; everyone is just simply there together, without that judgmental feeling of who’s better than whom. That warmth of being accepted really allows me to let my guard down completely.
